Three technical obstacles are overcome with new proof techniques: (1) ReLU non-smoothness is addressed by tracking model output increments rather than weight increments and bounding the non-linear loss with a piecewise linear approximation; (2) DP randomness breaks the monotonic weight growth exploited by prior work, so the authors instead analyze output increments for any data point; (3) the non-linearity of softmax/cross-entropy is handled via a piecewise linear linearization lemma. This yields tight upper and lower bounds on test loss (Theorems 3.4, 3.5) and adversarial test loss (Theorem 3.7) as functions of the FNR, clipping factor Λ_{i,j}, and data proportion γ_{i,j}. The framework is then used to derive corollaries explaining disparate impact across classes and subpopulations, analyze adversarial robustness degradation, and characterize when public pretraining + private finetuning fails. Key Results: The test loss bounds reveal three root causes of disparate impact: (1) feature disparity — minority/long-tail data has lower FNR, so DP noise hurts it disproportionately; (2) gradient clipping — classes with larger gradient norms suffer more aggressive clipping; (3) data imbalance — the privacy protection error term scales as O(dσ_p^2 / (γ_{i,j} F_{i,j}^2)), penalizing underrepresented groups. The adversarial test loss bound grows as O(√(Td) σ_n), showing DP noise directly compounds adversarial vulnerability over T iterations. For public pretraining, finetuning test loss increases with the angular distance θ between pretraining and finetuning feature distributions (Proposition 4.4), and pretraining can even hurt performance when θ is large. Experiments validate the theory: on MNIST, accuracy degrades from 97% to 86% as padding ratio (proxy for lower FNR) increases from 0% to 75%, and adversarial accuracy collapses from 95% to 20%. On CIFAR-10, adversarial accuracy under DP falls from 3% to 0% with increased padding. Private finetuning of CIFAR-10 (ResNet-18) drops from 91% at 0° rotation to 37% at 67.5°, confirming distribution-shift sensitivity. A proposed stage-wise network freezing mitigation improves LNet accuracy on MNIST from 95.11% to 96.71%. Strengths: - Provides the first unified theoretical framework that simultaneously explains three distinct DP-SGD side effects (accuracy, fairness, robustness) for non-convex, non-smooth ReLU networks without NTK or Lipschitz-smoothness assumptions - The FNR metric is a clean, interpretable quantity that practitioners can directly reason about to predict which groups will be harmed most - Novel piecewise linear bounding technique for ReLU networks under DP noise is a genuine technical contribution reusable beyond this specific application - Experiments on both controlled synthetic data and standard benchmarks (MNIST, CIFAR-10) consistently confirm the theoretical predictions Weaknesses / Limitations: - The two-layer CNN and structured patch data model are highly idealized; it is unclear whether the FNR characterization and its corollaries carry over to deeper or attention-based architectures (the authors acknowledge transformers as future work) - The binary classification setup with orthogonal class features and the assumption that batch size B ≥ c_1 · n are non-trivial restrictions that limit direct application to real training regimes with small batches and many classes - The proposed mitigation (stage-wise network freezing) shows only a modest ~1.6% improvement on MNIST under DP, and the theoretical justification for why freezing specifically improves FNR is deferred to an appendix without tight bounds - The analysis of public pretraining assumes a simplified rotation-based distribution shift model; the real-world relationship between pretraining dataset diversity and fine-tuning DP performance remains largely empirical
Citations 2603.04881 PDF
#14 — Highly Innovative (76)

MoE-SpAc: Efficient MoE Inference Based on Speculative Activation Utility in Heterogeneous Edge Scenarios

Shuhuai Li, Jianghao Lin, Dongdong Ge, Yinyu Ye · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 76 for the genuinely creative conceptual reframing of speculative decoding as a lookahead sensor rather than a compute accelerator—an insight that is both theoretically well-grounded (formal proofs of entropy dominance and √(γ+1)× SNR improvement) and practically effective. The unified utility metric elegantly solves the prefetch/eviction coupling problem that plagues prior heterogeneous offloading systems. The 4.04× average speedup across seven benchmarks and two model families demonstrates broad applicability, not just a single favorable configuration. However, the reliance on a well-aligned draft model and single-hardware evaluation temper the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs achieve parameter efficiency by activating only a small subset of expert networks per token, but their total parameter footprint far exceeds edge GPU VRAM. The standard workaround—heterogeneous offloading, where expert weights reside in CPU memory and are loaded on demand to GPU—suffers from severe I/O bottlenecks. Existing prefetching approaches predict which experts to load next using auxiliary neural networks or historical activation counts derived from autoregressive (AR) decoding, but AR produces only binary {0,1} activation signals per step, which are low-information and noise-dominated (especially for rarely-activated experts). This makes accurate lookahead for prefetching scheduling fundamentally unreliable under AR, limiting throughput and leaving CPU-GPU utilization poorly balanced. Methodology: MoE-SpAc repurposes Speculative Decoding (SD) not as a compute accelerator but as an informative lookahead sensor. In SD, a lightweight draft model generates γ candidate tokens that the target model verifies in a single parallel forward pass, yielding per-expert activation frequency counts in [0, γ+1] rather than binary indicators. This richer signal offers two key properties: higher information content (Shannon entropy strictly dominates AR's binary signal) and improved signal-to-noise ratio (√(γ+1)× better than AR). MoE-SpAc integrates three components: (1) a Speculative Utility Estimator that maps activation frequencies to a discrete utility score s ∈ {0,...,K} via inertial utility transition (score increments/decrements only when frequency fluctuation exceeds adaptive boundaries, preventing noise-driven thrashing) with adaptive boundary calibration via exponential moving average; (2) a Heterogeneous Workload Balancer that, at each Transformer layer and verification step, solves an online integer optimization problem to find the optimal threshold τ* partitioning experts into "hot" (prefetch to GPU) and "cold" (offload to CPU), subject to I/O time, VRAM, and integer constraints—the optimization reduces to O(1) due to convexity; and (3) an Asynchronous Execution Engine implementing a multi-level priority queue for prefetching and a Red-Black tree ordered pool for eviction, both keyed on the same unified utility score to avoid synchronization overhead. The system is implemented atop llama.cpp, hacking the ids_tensor mechanism to route expert computation to GPU or CPU dynamically without breaking the computation graph. Key Results: Evaluated on seven benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, MT-bench, HumanEval, GSM8K, Alpaca, CNN/DM, QA) using Qwen3-30B-A3B as target and Qwen3-4B-FP8 as draft on a single RTX 4090 with PCIe 4.0 (32 GB/s CPU-GPU bandwidth): MoE-SpAc achieves an average 4.04× speedup in tokens per second (TPS) over all baselines (Accelerate, vLLM, llama.cpp, Mixtral Offloading, MoE-Infinity, SP-MoE, Fate, HybriMoE) and a 42% improvement over the best SD-based baseline (llama.cpp-w/SD). On a second target model (DeepSeek-V2-Lite with int4 draft), MoE-SpAc achieves 52.9% average TPS improvement and 45.6% latency reduction over the best baselines. Prediction accuracy for hot/cold classification stabilizes around 0.85 on MMLU-Pro (vs. ~0.65 averaged for HybriMoE's AR-based method). Ablation confirms each component is load-bearing: removing the utility estimator causes the largest drop; removing the heterogeneous workload balancer or either the prefetcher/evictor degrades performance to baseline levels. MoE-SpAc operates effectively at a 17% expert cache ratio on GPU, outperforming baselines that use 25% cache ratios. Strengths: - The core insight—repurposing SD's verification window as a lookahead sensor rather than purely a compute accelerator—is theoretically well-grounded with formal proofs of entropy dominance, SNR improvement (√(γ+1)× factor), and Lipschitz-bounded drift of expert gating scores justifying inertial transition - The unified utility metric for both prefetching and eviction eliminates the coupling mismatch between separate scheduling objectives in prior systems (e.g., HybriMoE), and the O(1) optimization solution for the threshold τ* is practically efficient - Strong empirical validation across seven benchmarks and two target models with different draft quantization schemes demonstrates generality, and sensitivity analysis across cache ratios, generation lengths, and the K hyperparameter shows stability Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework requires allocating ~8% of VRAM to the draft model, which is a fixed overhead that cannot be reclaimed even when the SD acceptance rate is low—the paper shows this trade-off is favorable (6× gain vs. 1× cost) but this is workload-dependent and the analysis assumes α ≈ 0.8, which may not hold across all tasks and model pairs - The evaluation is restricted to a single hardware configuration (RTX 4090 + PCIe 4.0) with a relatively high bandwidth; performance on slower PCIe 3.0 or NVMe-based CPU memory systems—common in budget edge deployments—is not reported, and the I/O constraint sensitivity to bandwidth changes is not characterized - The approach inherits SD's requirement for a well-aligned draft model; mismatched model families would lower α, shrink the expert reuse benefit, and potentially negate MoE-SpAc's advantage over simpler AR-based prefetching, but no analysis of degradation under low-α regimes is provided
Search Results 2603.09983 PDF
#15 — Highly Innovative (76)

ERASE--A Real-World Aligned Benchmark for Unlearning in Recommender Systems

Pierre Lubitzsch, Maarten de Rijke, Sebastian Schelter · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: ERASE establishes the first comprehensive, real-world-aligned benchmark for machine unlearning in recommender systems, spanning 3 recommendation paradigms, 9 datasets, 9 models, and 7 algorithms with 600+ GB of reusable artifacts. The practitioner-grounded design — sequential small-batch unlearning, domain-specific forget sets, and a deployability threshold requiring 3+ orders-of-magnitude speedup — represents a meaningful departure from prior benchmarks like CURE4Rec. The novel evaluation metrics (Sensitive@k, RelEff@k) address real gaps in measuring forgetting effectiveness. The key diagnostic finding that no existing method is deployable across all tasks provides a clear roadmap for the field.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Machine unlearning (MU) in recommender systems — the ability to remove the influence of specific training data post-hoc — is increasingly important for GDPR compliance, spam removal, and handling liability issues. Existing benchmarks like CURE4Rec fail to reflect real-world deployment constraints: they focus exclusively on collaborative filtering, simulate unrealistically large batch deletions (up to 5% of all data at once), overlook diverse recommendation tasks (session-based, next-basket), and do not require the 3+ orders-of-magnitude speedup over retraining that practitioners actually need. Interviews with industry practitioners confirmed retraining takes up to a day, and unlearning a single request must complete in minutes. Methodology: The authors introduce ERASE, a large-scale benchmark built atop the RecBole library covering three recommendation paradigms: collaborative filtering (CF), session-based recommendation (SBR), and next-basket recommendation (NBR). ERASE spans 9 public datasets (TaFeng, Dunnhumby, Instacart, RSC15, Books, 30music, NowP, MovieLens, Food) and 9 model architectures (BPR, LightGCN, SGL, GRU4Rec, NARM, SASRec, SR-GNN, DNNTSP, Sets2Sets). Two realistic unlearning scenarios are defined: (1) sensitive item unlearning, where domain-specific interactions (e.g., alcohol for addiction sufferers) are removed sequentially per-user; and (2) removal of poisonous data, where spam injections (1% of training) are identified and unlearned in batches. Seven approximate unlearning algorithms are evaluated — four recommender-specific or GNN-specific (SCIF, GIF, CEU, IDEA) and three general-purpose methods from the NeurIPS 2023 unlearning competition (Fanchuan, Kookmin, Seif) — plus a retrain baseline. Evaluation uses three axes: utility (nDCG, Recall, Hit Rate), forgetting effectiveness (Sensitive@k for sensitive items; RelEff@k for spam), and efficiency (wall-clock time per request). ERASE releases 600+ GB of artifacts including 1,069 trained/retrained model checkpoints and 769 unlearned checkpoints, enabling the community to evaluate new algorithms without re-running expensive training. Key Results: No single algorithm achieves robust performance across all tasks. SCIF (influence-function-based, recommender-specific) is the most consistently strong method: it maintains utility and forgetting effectiveness across CF, SBR, and NBR tasks, with the lowest variance and no divergence incidents. However, SCIF scales poorly — on large models/datasets, its inverse-Hessian computations become prohibitively expensive. GIF (GNN-specific) achieves strong forgetting effectiveness but frequently diverges under sequential unlearning; it and IDEA fail ("div.") in multiple NBR and SBR scenarios. General-purpose methods (Kookmin, Fanchuan, Seif) are efficient but degrade effectiveness with many sequential requests — Seif drops by up to 99.91 RelEff@20 in some NBR spam scenarios. Crucially, most methods fall outside the "deployability area" defined as ≥3 orders of magnitude speedup over retraining at ≤1% effectiveness loss: Kookmin achieves ~10,000× speedup but often at the cost of acceptable effectiveness; SCIF achieves only ~1 order of magnitude speedup for large models. Robustness analysis (Figure 2) shows GNN-based methods exhibit high variance and instability, while recommender-specific SCIF has tight distributions. For CF sensitive item unlearning, several unlearned models match or even slightly exceed retrained model utility (RelEff ≈ +4.29 for SCIF on Food/SGL), plausibly because retraining is compute-budget-limited. Strengths: - Comprehensive scope: 3 recommendation tasks × 9 datasets × 9 models × 7 unlearning algorithms × 2 scenarios, with 600+ GB of reusable artifacts that eliminate retraining costs for future researchers - Real-world alignment: sequential small-batch unlearning, domain-specific forget-set construction, and efficiency thresholds grounded in practitioner interviews rather than academic convention - Novel evaluation metrics (Sensitive@k, RelItems@k, RelEff@k) that are more meaningful than weight-space or membership-inference comparisons, avoiding the indeterminacy of alternative approximate-unlearning metrics Weaknesses / Limitations: - ERASE does not include exact unlearning algorithms (e.g., RecEraser, UltraRE) by design since their runtime scales with training set size, but this omission means the benchmark cannot characterize the utility-efficiency frontier from an exact-unlearning baseline - The benchmark reveals that no existing method is deployable across all tasks (most either lack sufficient speedup or sufficient effectiveness), yet it offers limited algorithmic guidance on how to close this gap — the paper is diagnostic rather than prescriptive - Hyperparameter sensitivity is substantial and task-dependent, and ERASE tunes hyperparameters per algorithm/scenario using a subset of data; generalizing these choices to new domains or models remains an open challenge not systematically addressed
Search Results 2603.08341 PDF
#16 — Highly Innovative (76)

The Model Knows Which Tokens Matter: Automatic Token Selection via Noise Gating

Landi He, Xiaoyu Yang, Lijian Xu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: AutoSelect's reframing of visual token pruning as capacity-constrained representation learning via variance-preserving noise gating is a conceptually clean departure from the heuristic proxy signals (attention magnitude, CLS similarity) that dominate the field. The VP noise gate is an elegant differentiable surrogate for hard token removal that provides full gradient flow while imposing an information bottleneck proportional to importance — a principled mechanism validated to match hard Top-K behavior. The diagonal attention design in the Denoiser preventing cross-token information leakage shows careful theoretical reasoning, and the method achieves Pareto dominance across both accuracy and latency dimensions with 0.69ms overhead (60× faster than PRUNESID).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Visual tokens dominate inference cost in vision-language models (VLMs) due to the quadratic scaling of self-attention with sequence length. While many visual tokens carry redundant information, existing pruning methods rely on heuristic proxy signals such as attention magnitude, CLS-to-patch similarity, or diversity scores. These approaches frame the problem as identifying and discarding "unimportant" tokens — a local, greedy perspective that lacks a principled global information budget. A more fundamental question is: given a fixed computational budget K, how should representational capacity be allocated across visual tokens to maximize downstream task performance? Methodology: AutoSelect recasts visual token pruning as capacity-constrained representation learning. Two lightweight modules — a Scorer and a Denoiser — are inserted between the frozen vision encoder and the projector, while all base VLM weights remain frozen. The Scorer assigns per-token importance scores using L Transformer encoder blocks followed by a linear projection. During training, a differentiable Soft Top-K operator (with temperature annealing from τ_start to τ_end on a cosine schedule) converts scores into polarized weights α ∈ [0,1]^N summing to K. Rather than discarding low-scoring tokens, a variance-preserving (VP) noise gate modulates each token's information capacity: x̃_i = √α_i · x_i + √(1−α_i) · ε_i, where ε_i ~ N(0,I). This keeps sequence length fixed at N during training, provides full gradient flow, and enforces an information bottleneck proportional to each token's importance score. A lightweight Denoiser with diagonal attention (preventing cross-token information leakage) then maps perturbed representations back toward the LLM's expected input distribution. At inference, the Denoiser and noise injection are removed entirely; a hard top-K selection retains only the K highest-scoring tokens at their original position indices (preserving RoPE spatial encoding). Training uses only the standard next-token prediction loss on ImageNet-1K-VL-Enriched captioning data, adding ~84M trainable parameters. Key Results: On LLaVA-1.5-7B across ten benchmarks (GQA, MMBench, MMBench-CN, MME, POPE, ScienceQA, VQAv2, TextVQA, SEED-Bench, VizWiz), AutoSelect achieves 98.2% average performance retention at 192 tokens (66.7% pruning), 97.6% at 128 tokens (77.8% pruning), and 96.5% at 64 tokens (88.9% pruning) — surpassing all pre-LLM baselines including PRUNESID at the aggressive 64-token budget by 1.4%. On LLaVA-NEXT-7B with 2,880 input tokens (88.9% reduction to 320), AutoSelect achieves 96.1% retention, beating the best baseline (HoloV) by 0.4%. On Qwen2.5-VL-7B at all three pruning rates, AutoSelect consistently outperforms FastV and HoloV by 1–6 percentage points. Crucially, the pruning module adds only 0.69 ms overhead (vs. 43.39 ms for PRUNESID and 2.77 ms for HoloV) and achieves a total TTFT of 72.73 ms vs. 149.51 ms for the full model — a 2.06× prefill speedup. An LLM-free evaluation on ImageNet-1K using a separately trained ViT-B/16 confirms the accuracy gains originate from better token selection, not LLM adaptation. Strengths: - The information-theoretic reframing of token pruning as capacity-constrained communication is conceptually clean and principled, contrasting sharply with heuristic-driven competitors. - The VP noise gate is an elegant differentiable surrogate for hard token removal: it is empirically validated to impose equivalent information constraints to hard Top-K pruning (Figure 5), yet enables end-to-end gradient flow without auxiliary losses. - Diagonal attention in the Denoiser is a well-motivated design choice that prevents high-importance tokens from leaking information to low-importance ones, preserving the integrity of the capacity constraint during training — and this ablation is validated rigorously (−2.4 to −2.8% when replaced with global attention). - The Scorer is text-agnostic (operates on visual features only), making scores reusable across dialogue turns and deployable on architectures without a CLS token (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL), where methods like FasterVLM and PruMerge fail. - Efficiency is genuinely competitive: 0.69 ms pruning overhead is over 60× faster than PRUNESID and 4× faster than HoloV, making AutoSelect the Pareto-dominant method across both accuracy and latency dimensions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Scorer is trained exclusively on ImageNet-1K captioning data with no text conditioning, raising questions about generalization to tasks requiring text-grounded visual attention (e.g., OCR, fine-grained referring expression comprehension) where query-dependent token importance matters. - The method requires a training phase (even if lightweight), unlike plug-and-play training-free methods such as FastV or HoloV, creating a deployment barrier for users who cannot fine-tune or who work with proprietary VLMs. - The fixed budget K does not adapt per image — a uniform budget is applied regardless of image complexity or information density, which may over-prune detail-rich images and under-prune simple ones. - Evaluation is limited to three LLaVA-family or Qwen architectures; generalization to encoder-free or decoder-only VLM architectures remains undemonstrated.
Search Results 2603.07135 PDF
#17 — Highly Innovative (76)

ICLR: In-Context Imitation Learning with Visual Reasoning

Toan Nguyen, Weiduo Yuan, Songlin Wei, Hui Li, Daniel Seita, Yue Wang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: ICLR scores 76 for its creative integration of visual reasoning traces into in-context imitation learning, yielding a 3× improvement over baselines. The joint autoregressive modeling of reasoning and actions within a single transformer is technically elegant, and the reasoning dropout mechanism that produces two useful model variants from one training run is a genuinely clever design choice. Real-world validation on a Franka arm strengthens the contribution beyond simulation-only work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: In-context imitation learning allows robots to adapt to new tasks from a small number of prompt demonstrations without additional training. However, existing approaches condition only on state-action trajectories and lack explicit representations of task intent. This is problematic in ambiguous settings where the same low-level actions may be consistent with multiple different objectives — the robot cannot distinguish which object to manipulate or which receptacle to target without access to the underlying reasoning process that motivated the demonstrator's decisions. The paper addresses this gap by incorporating explicit visual reasoning into the in-context learning framework. Methodology: ICLR augments demonstration prompts with structured visual reasoning traces that represent anticipated future robot trajectories in image space. Specifically, a visual reasoning trace is a polyline of 5 gripper keypoints sampled evenly from the future horizon of an episode, projected into the third-view camera frame. These traces are generated using Molmo2, a VLM prompted to localize the gripper in images, and represented as ordered numerical vectors (not text). The architecture is a Llama2-style causal transformer with modality-specific encoders: a frozen pretrained vision transformer for camera images (with attention pooling), an MLP for proprioception, an MLP reasoning encoder for keypoint traces, and an MLP action encoder. Tokens from all modalities are interleaved and processed autoregressively, with the model trained to jointly predict the next reasoning trace and the next action chunk (16 steps). The combined loss is L_action + 0.3 × L_reasoning, with the reasoning weight empirically chosen for balanced trade-off. A key training technique is reasoning dropout: randomly masking 0-100% of reasoning tokens in target trajectories, which prevents the model from over-relying on generated reasoning traces and enables an efficient inference-time variant that skips reasoning generation entirely. At inference, the model first predicts a visual reasoning trace then produces the corresponding action chunk autoregressively, with KV caching for efficiency (0.0278 ms per trace or action prediction on RTX 5090). Key Results: On the LIBERO simulation benchmark (LIBERO-Object: 3 unseen tasks; LIBERO-90: 15 unseen tasks across kitchen, living room, study scenes), ICLR Dropout achieves 54.05% average success rate versus 16.27% for the ICRT baseline and 27.17% for Target-Only — roughly a 3× improvement over the best prior method. In real-world manipulation experiments on a Franka Research 3 arm with a UMI gripper (6 unseen poking tasks, 6 unseen pick-and-place tasks), ICLR achieves 71.67% average poking success and 60.00% pick-and-place success, compared to 48.33%/22.50% for ICRT. The complete ICLR model (with test-time reasoning) outperforms the dropout variant in real-world settings, while the dropout variant performs better in simulation — attributed to the lower visual diversity of LIBERO training data. Failure analysis shows visual trace errors account for 40-45% of failures but are not the primary failure mode; grasp and placement errors dominate. An efficient reasoning variant that reasons every 8 steps performs on par with full-frequency reasoning while being roughly 8× faster. Strengths: - The joint autoregressive modeling of reasoning traces and actions within a single transformer is technically clean and avoids the need for a separate reasoning module or two-stage pipeline - The reasoning dropout mechanism elegantly produces two useful model variants from a single training run — a full reasoning model and an efficient inference variant — while also serving as regularization - Evaluation is thorough: both simulation (LIBERO-Object and LIBERO-90) and real-world experiments are conducted, ablations cover prompt type, reasoning interval, and failure modes, and the failure analysis using interpretable visual traces adds genuine diagnostic value Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach is limited to pick-and-place and poking tasks; generalization to longer-horizon, dexterous, or bimanual manipulation is undemonstrated and explicitly flagged as future work - Visual reasoning traces are gripper keypoints in image space, a relatively simple representation; the paper acknowledges that richer representations (bounding boxes, affordances, depth) may be needed for more complex tasks, but does not investigate them - Collecting training demonstrations with synchronized visual reasoning traces requires running Molmo2 offline on all trajectory frames, adding data pipeline complexity and coupling the method's quality to the gripper localization accuracy of an external VLM
Citations 2603.07530 PDF
#18 — Highly Innovative (76)

Understanding by Reconstruction: Reversing the Software Development Process for LLM Pretraining

Zhiyuan Zeng, Yichi Zhang, Yong Shan, Kai Hua, Siyuan Fang, Zhaiyu Liu, Jiaheng Liu, Haozhe Wang, Yining Zheng, Ming Ding, Ke Shen, Ge Zhang, Wenhao Huang, Xipeng Qiu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 76 for the creative reframing of code pretraining as trajectory reconstruction—reverse-engineering the latent agentic development process behind static repositories is a genuinely novel problem formulation that challenges the standard 'train on final artifacts' paradigm. The grounding mechanism that replaces simulated outputs with actual repository content is an elegant solution to hallucination drift, and the perplexity-driven CoT search offers a lightweight, principled alternative to RL for optimizing reasoning quality. The controlled experimental design fixing total token budget cleanly isolates information density from scale effects. However, the reliance on a much larger teacher model and single-architecture evaluation temper the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs trained on static code repositories struggle with the deep, long-horizon reasoning required for complex software engineering tasks. The root cause is a mismatch between the training signal and the generative process: a repository in its final form is a compressed artifact that has abstracted away the intermediate planning, debugging, and iterative refinement steps that produced it. Training on this static code teaches models to mimic surface-level structural patterns rather than the causal reasoning required to derive them. This explains why models that excel at short snippet generation fail on repository-scale tasks. The paper proposes bridging this gap by reverse-engineering the latent agentic trajectories hidden behind static repositories to provide a richer supervision signal. Methodology: The framework, called Repo2Agent, consists of two stages. First, Multi-Agent Trajectory Curation simulates the software development process using an LLM-driven multi-agent workflow. A Main Agent generates high-level project requirements and a dependency-aware implementation plan from a source repository, then delegates file-level implementation to Sub-Agents. Each Sub-Agent plans its file, uses a Read tool to gather inter-file context, and writes the final code. To prevent hallucination drift, the simulation is grounded with factual information extracted directly from the repository: the file structure tree, an inter-file dependency graph (from import analysis), and intra-file structure (from AST parsing). Tool call responses and final Write outputs are replaced with ground-truth content from the actual repository. Second, LongCoT Optimization refines the quality of the synthesized Chain-of-Thought reasoning via a perplexity-driven search. Each CoT step is iteratively resampled; a candidate replacement is accepted only if it lowers the perplexity of the ground-truth code (i.e., maximizes log p(x|z)). This avoids the instability of RL while still optimizing reasoning quality. The resulting trajectories are flattened into sequential documents and used for continuous pre-training of Llama-3-8B on 20B tokens with a 64k context window. Loss masking is applied to Observation tokens so the model learns only to predict Think and Action tokens. Key Results: Experiments compare four variants: the Prolong baseline (external), Raw-Repos (raw code), Repo2Agent (unoptimized trajectories), and Repo2Agent-Search (search-optimized trajectories). On long-context understanding, Repo2Agent-Search achieves 61.80 average on Ruler at 64k tokens versus 61.00 for Raw-Repos and 57.10 for Prolong, demonstrating that reconstructed trajectories are more robust at extreme context lengths. On the Helmet benchmark at 32k, Repo2Agent-Search scores 62.65 versus 60.98 for Raw-Repos. Coding improvements are clear: HumanEval scores rise from 34.76 (Raw-Repos) to 37.20 (Repo2Agent-Search), and LongCodeBench-32k improves from 34.16 to 36.46. General reasoning also benefits: MATH improves from 2.18 to 3.76 and BBH from 66.27 to 67.03. On APTBench, which measures agentic potential of base models, Repo2Agent achieves 30.10 overall versus 29.02 for Raw-Repos, with particular gains in planning-centric sub-tasks. The CoT optimization analysis confirms the mechanism: as search iterations increase, CoT length grows and code perplexity monotonically decreases, validating that more explicit reasoning directly eases the code generation task. Trajectories expand average per-repository token count from 4,865 (raw code) to 12,083 (Repo2Agent-Search). Strengths: - The grounding mechanism — replacing simulated tool outputs with actual repository content (file contents, AST-derived structure, dependency graphs) — is a practical and effective solution to the hallucination problem in purely LLM-simulated trajectories, ensuring the terminal state of every trajectory is factually correct. - The search-based CoT optimization is a computationally lightweight alternative to RL that directly operationalizes a clear objective (minimize code perplexity given reasoning), and the case studies demonstrate it produces qualitatively richer, more technically precise reasoning rather than mere paraphrasing. - The controlled experimental design — fixing total token budget at 12% of 20B tokens across all variants — cleanly isolates the effect of data structure (trajectory vs. raw code) from data volume, making the performance gains attributable to information density rather than scale. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is restricted to a single base model (Llama-3-8B), and it is unclear whether the gains generalize to larger models or different architectures; it is possible that larger models already implicitly recover more of the latent process from static code. - The method uses a powerful LLM (Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct) to generate trajectories for a much smaller target model, creating a significant teacher-student quality gap and raising questions about scalability of the data generation pipeline in terms of computational cost per repository.
Recommended 2603.11103 PDF
#19 — Innovative (73)

TimeWarp: Evaluating Web Agents by Revisiting the Past

Md Farhan Ishmam, Kenneth Marino · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: TIMEWARP introduces a genuinely novel evaluation dimension — temporal robustness of web agents — that no prior benchmark addresses. The controlled, containerized multi-version environments and the TIMETRAJ plan distillation scheme are creative solutions to real scalability bottlenecks. The finding that multi-version training substantially improves generalization is actionable and non-obvious. Limited by modest task scale (231 goals) and unresolved catastrophic forgetting in continual settings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing web agent benchmarks use static, fixed environments—either simulated (WebArena, WebShop) or the live web—neither of which captures the dynamic, evolving nature of real websites. Static benchmarks cannot reveal whether agents trained on today's web will generalize as UIs, layouts, and workflows change over time. Live web benchmarks are unreproducible and uncontrollable. This paper addresses a fundamental gap: there is no controlled benchmark for studying how web agent performance degrades or transfers across different temporal versions of the same website. Methodology: The authors introduce TIMEWARP, a benchmark consisting of three containerized web environments—Wiki (Wikipedia-style), News (BBC-style), and Shop (Amazon/WebShop-style)—each with six frontend versions (v1–v6) spanning distinct historical eras of web design from 1998 to 2025. The 1,386 tasks (231 unique goals × 6 versions) are manually written and independently verified, covering encyclopedic retrieval, information search, e-commerce ordering, and multi-site navigation. Versions are implemented using lightweight Flask backends with modular frontends, making the benchmark expandable at low cost. Evaluation uses GPT-5 as an LLM judge with fuzzy semantic matching. To address the scalability problem of collecting training trajectories across multiple website versions, the authors propose TIMETRAJ: a hybrid human-in-the-loop plan distillation algorithm. A planner LLM drafts checkpoint-based execution plans that humans refine once per task goal into version-invariant plans; a GPT-5 teacher executor then rolls out trajectories automatically across all six versions. This decouples expensive human annotation from per-version trajectory collection. They also introduce TIMEWARP-BC, a behavior cloning variant that trains on the full teacher response—including thinking, planning, memory, and action tokens—rather than action tokens alone, enabling agents to learn reasoning and memory required for complex multi-step tasks. Key Results: Zero-shot VLMs exhibit substantial performance variation across website versions, with success rates spanning [2.6%, 21.4%] for Qwen-3 VL 8B in Set-of-Marks mode, confirming that visual agents are brittle to UI changes. Text-based (HTML/AXT) agents are considerably more robust, showing only ~6% variation across versions; Qwen-3 4B Thinking achieves the highest zero-shot performance at 29.2%. TIMEWARP-BC provides large training gains: Qwen-3 4B improves from 20.4% to 37.7% overall (+17.3 points), and Llama-3.1 8B improves from near-0% to 27.0% (+26.3 points), the latter becoming a functioning agent from a completely non-functional baseline. Training on all five versions (v1–v5) generalizes substantially better to held-out versions than single-version training, confirming the value of multi-version exposure. TIMETRAJ achieves a 98.57% trajectory collection success rate using human refinement. Full fine-tuning significantly outperforms LoRA across all adapter ranks. The optimal configuration is a 64k context window trained for 3 epochs on the complete response (action + thinking + memory + planning tokens). Strengths: - Novel and well-motivated problem formulation: temporal robustness of web agents is an underexplored but practically critical dimension, and TIMEWARP provides the first controlled, reproducible benchmark for studying it. - TIMETRAJ is a genuinely scalable trajectory collection scheme—collecting plans once and executing across versions removes the O(versions × tasks) annotation bottleneck, achieving high coverage (757/768 training trajectories). - Thorough experimental analysis with ablations on observation modality, training tokens, context length, LoRA vs. full fine-tuning, training epochs, sample count, and continual learning, giving actionable engineering guidance. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark's 231 unique task goals is modest; task diversity within each environment category remains limited, and performance saturation effects at this scale are unclear. - TIMEWARP-BC still requires human involvement for plan refinement, and removing it causes a 30–40% drop in collected trajectories, limiting full automation. The human refinement step also does not generalize well without human effort for genuinely novel website designs. - Continual fine-tuning across versions suffers from catastrophic forgetting (version v6 performance drops significantly after sequential fine-tuning on v1), and no successful remedy is presented—this is acknowledged as an open problem rather than a solved one.
Citations 2603.04949 PDF
#20 — Innovative (73)

Timer-S1: A Billion-Scale Time Series Foundation Model with Serial Scaling

Yong Liu, Xingjian Su, Shiyu Wang, Haoran Zhang, Haixuan Liu, Yuxuan Wang, Zhou Ye, Yang Xiang, Jianmin Wang, Mingsheng Long · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Timer-S1 introduces Serial-Token Prediction (STP), a genuinely novel inference-time architectural choice that retains auxiliary prediction blocks rather than discarding them post-training, directly addressing the train-test gap in multi-token prediction. Scaling to 8.3B parameters with MoE for time series is significant engineering, and the ablations cleanly validate STP over NTP and MTP. However, the core contribution is an architectural refinement within an established scaling paradigm rather than a paradigm shift, and the univariate-only limitation weakens the zero-shot generalization narrative.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Time series foundation models have struggled with a fundamental scalability bottleneck: existing approaches either use parallel forecasting (predicting all horizons simultaneously in one pass), which misses the inherently serial nature of forecasting where each prediction depends on prior estimates, or autoregressive rolling inference, which accumulates errors and incurs prohibitive computational cost at long horizons. Prior models in the Timer family (Timer, Timer-XL, Timer-3/Sundial) operated at limited parameter scales. This paper addresses how to effectively scale a time series foundation model to billions of parameters while respecting the serial structure of the forecasting task. Methodology: Timer-S1 is an 8.3B parameter sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model with 0.75B activated parameters per token and a context length of 11,520. The core architectural innovation is the TimeSTP (Serial-Token Prediction) block, which extends the standard TimeMoE transformer block by concatenating the current token embeddings with the original input patch embeddings, projecting them, and iteratively refining predictions one patch offset at a time. A sequence of H = ⌈F/P⌉ − 1 TimeSTP blocks is stacked to generate multi-step forecasts, where longer-horizon predictions traverse more serial computation — directly mirroring the increasing uncertainty of long-range forecasting. Critically, TimeSTP blocks are retained at inference (unlike multi-token prediction in LLMs, which discards auxiliary blocks post-training), eliminating rolling-style autoregression while preserving serial computation. The backbone uses sparse MoE with E=32 experts, K=2 activated per token, causal self-attention with QK-Norm and RoPE positional encodings, and Pre-RMSNorm for training stability. The forecasting head is a shared quantile head predicting Q=9 quantile levels via weighted quantile loss (approximating CRPS). Training follows a three-stage pipeline: (1) pre-training on TimeBench — a curated corpus of 1.032 trillion time points spanning finance, IoT, meteorology, healthcare, and synthetic signals — using the combined NTP + STP objective; (2) continued pre-training (CPT) with a horizon-decay weighted STP objective (weight 1/√j for the j-th STP block) to prioritize short-term accuracy; (3) long-context extension via RoPE scaling from context length 2,880 to 11,520. Data augmentation includes resampling (Fourier-domain interpolation/downsampling to vary temporal resolution) and value-flipping (multiplying series by −1 to counteract directional bias). Key Results: On the GIFT-Eval benchmark (24 datasets, 144,000 time series, 177M data points), Timer-S1 achieves state-of-the-art MASE of 0.693 and CRPS of 0.485 as a pre-trained zero-shot forecaster. This represents a 7.6% lower MASE and 13.2% lower CRPS than Timer-3 (Sundial), which was trained on the same TimeBench corpus. Timer-S1 ties with Chronos-2 on CRPS (0.485 vs. 0.485) despite Chronos-2 explicitly modeling multivariate interactions. The performance advantage is most pronounced on medium- and long-term forecasting horizons (relative MASE of 0.70 vs. 0.72–0.82 for competitors at medium term; 0.71 vs. 0.75–0.85 at long term), validating that serial computation specifically improves long-horizon accuracy. Scaling analysis confirms the model continues to benefit from increasing TimeMoE blocks up to 24 and TimeSTP blocks up to 16. Serial-Token Prediction outperforms both next-token prediction (NTP: MASE 0.733–0.738) and multi-token prediction (MTP: MASE 0.719–0.729) under the same total block budget. Post-training yields consistent gains: pre-training alone gives MASE 0.728/CRPS 0.512; adding CPT reaches 0.706/0.496; adding long-context extension reaches 0.693/0.485. Strengths: - The Serial-Token Prediction objective is a principled, well-motivated design choice: by retaining TimeSTP blocks at inference and adaptively determining inference depth based on required horizon, the model avoids the train-test gap inherent in LLM-style multi-token prediction, which discards auxiliary blocks post-training. - The ablation studies are thorough and directly validate key design decisions: STP vs. NTP vs. MTP comparison, TimeSTP retention at inference vs. rolling autoregression, data augmentation impact, and pre-training necessity are all cleanly isolated. - The three-stage training pipeline (PT → CPT → LCE) with distinct objectives for each stage is practically well-motivated and empirically validated — each stage yields measurable gains on GIFT-Eval. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Timer-S1 operates in a univariate channel-independent format and does not natively model multivariate correlations or exogenous covariates. The authors acknowledge this explicitly; leveraging cross-variate structure requires downstream fine-tuning, which undermines the zero-shot generalization claim in multivariate settings where Chronos-2 and others model joint distributions. - The fixed patch size P=16 introduces a frequency-specific blind spot: ablation Figure 17 shows a pronounced error spike at period T=16 for sinusoidal signals, a direct artifact of the patch boundary alignment. This artifact is identified but not resolved, suggesting the architecture has sensitivity to frequency-patch size alignment that could affect real-world datasets with dominant seasonal frequencies near P. - The paper is a technical report from ByteDance/Tsinghua without peer review, and the GIFT-Eval leaderboard ranking conflates zero-shot pre-trained models with fine-tuned models; the head-to-head comparisons against Chronos-2 are reasonable but both models benefit from similar data (TimeBench), limiting the independence of the comparison.
Citations 2603.04791 PDF
#21 — Innovative (73)

EgoReasoner: Learning Egocentric 4D Reasoning via Task-Adaptive Structured Thinking

Fangrui Zhu, Yunfeng Xi, Jianmo Ni, Mu Cai, Boqing Gong, Long Zhao, Chen Qu, Ian Miao, Yi Li, Cheng Zhong, Huaizu Jiang, Shwetak Patel · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: EgoReasoner scores 73 for its central empirical finding that task-agnostic reinforcement learning actively destabilizes spatial reasoning — a non-obvious and practically important insight. The task-adaptive structured thinking templates with per-task reward functions represent meaningful methodological novelty, and outperforming a 7B model with a 3B model through reasoning design rather than scale is a compelling result. However, evaluation on a single kitchen-domain benchmark limits demonstrated generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Egocentric video understanding requires continuous 4D spatial-temporal reasoning — tracking both static fixtures and moving objects from a constantly shifting first-person camera viewpoint. Existing multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) fail on a suite of six HD-EPIC benchmark tasks: fixture interaction counting, viewpoint-relative fixture location (clock-face orientation), object location, object movement counting, object movement itinerary tracking, and stationary object localization. The gap is not merely performance but structural: these tasks require fundamentally different cognitive operations — spatial anchoring to a moving camera, long-horizon temporal bookkeeping, and duration arithmetic — that generic Chain-of-Thought (CoT) methods cannot express. Critically, the authors demonstrate that uniform reinforcement learning across tasks actively destabilizes performance on spatial tasks, with accuracy on fixture interaction counting and fixture location declining as training progresses under standard GRPO. Methodology: EgoReasoner is a two-stage framework built on Qwen2.5-VL-3B. Stage I (Structured Cold-Start via SFT) designs six Task-Adaptive Thinking Templates — one per task type — that decompose each task into a sequence of cognitively appropriate sub-steps with explicit grounding slots for entity names, timestamps, and spatial metadata (e.g., clock-face angles, trajectory segments). A teacher model (Gemini 2.5) synthesizes structured CoT traces by reasoning backward from ground-truth 4D Descriptions, and the student model is fine-tuned to imitate these traces on 4.3K curated QA pairs. The 4D Descriptions themselves are produced by an automated metadata pipeline: 2D object detection + SLAM-calibrated 3D localization from Ego-Exo4D, fused with Gemini-refined text narrations, yielding precise bounding boxes, 3D positions, and timestamps for every object-fixture interaction across 443 videos (56 hours). Stage II (Grounded Reinforcement Fine-Tuning via GRPO) further optimizes the Stage I model using task-aware reward functions applied to 12K RFT pairs. The total reward is a weighted composite of four components: an accuracy reward (binary answer correctness), a grounding reward (entity name matching + soft-window timestamp alignment at ±2.0s), a logic reward (task-specific internal consistency — trajectory segment counts, angular accuracy on clock-face predictions via circular distance, duration arithmetic), and a format reward (structural tag compliance). Regex-based parsing extracts intermediate metadata from the structured traces for reward computation — made reliable by the consistent formatting instilled in Stage I. Key Results: The unified Stage 2 model (3B parameters, trained on 16K samples total) achieves 37.5% average accuracy on HD-EPIC, surpassing Qwen2.5-VL-7B (25.7%) by over 10 points and all other open-source baselines including LongVA (28.1%) and VideoLLaMA 2 (25.5%). On Object Movement Counting the model reaches 59.5% (+26.5% over the best baseline). Object Location reaches 50.4%. The ablation (Table 2) quantifies the contribution of each stage: baseline Qwen2.5-VL-3B averages 27.0%; adding CoT SFT raises this to 31.9%; adding task-aware RFT on top reaches 36.8% on per-task training and 37.5% in unified multi-task training. The ablation of reward components (Figure 5) confirms that standard RFT without task-aware rewards causes accuracy collapse on spatial tasks mid-training, while combined grounding+logic rewards stabilize and improve all task categories. Stationary Object Localization (21.0%) remains a persistent outlier due to 8–10 minute video lengths that exceed the model's effective temporal context window. Strengths: - The central empirical finding — that task-agnostic RL actively harms structured spatial reasoning — is clearly demonstrated and motivates the per-task reward design with a principled, non-obvious argument - The metadata-driven data pipeline (SLAM + object detection + Gemini narration fusion) produces genuinely grounded training supervision rather than relying on visual heuristics or manual annotation at scale, and the 4D Descriptions serve double duty as both CoT synthesis source and reward ground truth - Outperforming a 7B model with a 3B model via structured reasoning design rather than scale is a practically important result for embodied AI deployment Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework is evaluated only on HD-EPIC, a single kitchen-domain benchmark; generalization to other egocentric domains (outdoor, industrial, medical) or different camera rigs without SLAM metadata is not demonstrated - Stationary Object Localization (21.0%, below the best baseline of 27.0%) represents a clear failure case tied to long video context, and the paper offers no architectural remedy — the temporal context window limitation is acknowledged but left unaddressed - The reward hyperparameter search (weights α, λ, γ, δ per task) is non-trivial and task-specific; the paper defers details to the Supplementary, making reproducibility dependent on unreleased hyperparameter configurations
Search Results 2603.06561 PDF
#22 — Innovative (73)

Boosting deep Reinforcement Learning using pretraining with Logical Options

Zihan Ye, Phil Chau, Raban Emunds, Jannis Blüml, Cedric Derstroff, Quentin Delfosse, Oleg Arenz, Kristian Kersting · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Scores 73 for the creative insight that symbolic reasoning can serve purely as a pretraining scaffold, to be discarded entirely at inference time — an elegant resolution to the latency-versus-reasoning tradeoff in neuro-symbolic RL. The order-of-magnitude improvements over PPO/DQN baselines and the demonstration that standard RL agents achieve 0% success on actual objectives despite high scores is striking. However, the reliance on hand-crafted logic rules, per-environment option design, and restriction to three Atari games with similar reward-trap characteristics limits the generality of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep reinforcement learning agents are prone to reward hacking and shortcut learning — exploiting dense reward signals in ways that satisfy the metric but fail the underlying task. In environments like Seaquest and Kangaroo, PPO and DQN agents learn to attack enemies repeatedly rather than pursuing the long-horizon objectives (refilling oxygen, reaching the joey) that lead to sustained high performance. Purely symbolic neuro-symbolic approaches can mitigate this via logical reasoning, but suffer from inference-time computational overhead and difficulty scaling to continuous action spaces. Reward shaping is an alternative but requires tedious, domain-specific engineering. The gap is a method that instills goal-directed inductive biases into neural policies without requiring symbolic reasoning at inference time. Methodology: The paper introduces H²RL (Hybrid Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning), a two-stage framework inspired by human skill acquisition (scaffolded learning followed by free practice). The architecture has four components: (1) a differentiable symbolic logic manager that maps high-level object-centric state z_t to a distribution over a fixed set of options; (2) a set of pretrained option workers, each a low-level policy trained on a modified subtask environment (e.g., climb, use_hammer, jump_barrel for DonkeyKong); (3) a neural RL policy (PPO backbone) that operates on raw pixel inputs; and (4) a Mixture-of-Experts gating module that blends the logic-induced policy and the neural policy via learned weights. The logic manager implements differentiable forward reasoning using tensor-encoded meta-rules, softmax-normalized rule weights, and a log-sum-exp approximation to disjunction (softor), enabling end-to-end gradient flow through logical inference. In Stage 1 (pretraining), the neural policy, logic manager, and gating module are jointly trained via PPO — the logic manager steers option selection while the neural policy learns low-level control. In Stage 2 (posttraining), the symbolic components are discarded and only the neural policy (H²RL₊) is further trained via standard environment interaction, yielding H²RL₊₊. This separation means symbolic reasoning is used solely to initialize policy parameters with goal-directed inductive biases; inference-time cost equals a standard neural policy. Key Results: H²RL₊₊ achieves episodic returns of 131,842±1,221 in Kangaroo and 216,793±125,655 in DonkeyKong — orders of magnitude above PPO (14,592 and 4,536 respectively), DQN (14,822 and 3,205), and neuro-symbolic baselines NUDGE (404 and 21) and BlendRL (1,482 and 85). In the Continuous Atari Learning Environment (CALE), H²RL reaches 84,665±49,767 in Kangaroo versus PPO's 1,785±72 and hPPO's 19,854±18,586. Critically, policy alignment results (Table 2) show that PPO, DQN, and C51 achieve 0% success reaching floor 2 in Kangaroo despite high scores — they are trapped in reward loops — while all H²RL-pretrained variants achieve 100% success on floors 2 and 4. Ablations (Table 3) establish that neither symbolic-only (hReason: 2,238 in Kangaroo) nor neural-with-symbolic-inputs (exPPO: 14,247) nor hierarchical-neural (hPPO: 10,601) approaches replicate H²RL's performance, confirming the pretraining mechanism itself — not simply access to symbolic information — is the critical factor. H²RL also functions as a universal pretraining substrate: H²DQN₊ reaches 116,665 in Kangaroo versus DQN's 14,822, and H²C51₊ reaches 183,645 in DonkeyKong versus C51's 3,393. Strengths: - The inference-time cost is identical to a standard neural policy — symbolic reasoning is consumed entirely during pretraining, resolving the latency bottleneck that limits prior neuro-symbolic RL systems. - The framework is algorithm-agnostic: demonstrated improvements over PPO, DQN, and C51 baselines with a uniform pretraining interface, and extends to continuous action spaces without modification. - The ablation study is thorough and directly addresses confounds: it rules out that symbolic state access alone (exPPO), hierarchical structure alone (hPPO), or symbolic reasoning alone (hReason) explain the gains, isolating the pretraining mechanism as the critical factor. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The logic rules, pretrained options, and object-centric symbolic state (z_t via OCAtari) are all hand-crafted per environment, requiring substantial domain expertise and limiting generality. The options themselves may be suboptimal (explicitly acknowledged), and the sensitivity of the method to logic rule quality is unquantified. - Evaluation is confined to three Atari games; all with the same characteristic (deceptive dense rewards and long-horizon subgoals). It is unclear whether H²RL provides benefits in environments without reward traps or in domains lacking reliable symbolic state extractors. - The two-stage training pipeline increases engineering complexity: option pretraining requires modified environments with custom reward functions, and the posttraining phase adds another full training run, raising total compute costs that are not reported or compared against baselines.
Search Results 2603.06565 PDF
#23 — Innovative (73)

Quantum Hamlets: Distributed Compilation of Large Algorithmic Graph States

Anthony Micciche, Naphan Benchasattabuse, Andrew McGregor, Michal Hajdušek, Rodney Van Meter, Stefan Krastanov · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 73 for correctly identifying that minimum edge cut is the wrong optimization objective for distributed graph state compilation, replacing it with balanced minimum maximum matching — a reframing that meaningfully redirects the field. The VCG protocol cleanly ties partition quality to physical Bell pair cost, and BURY is a practical polynomial-time algorithm evaluated at scale against strong baselines including METIS. However, the greedy heuristic lacks approximation guarantees, and the all-to-all QPU connectivity assumption limits near-term applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Distributed quantum computing requires generating large graph states across multiple quantum processing units (QPUs), but doing so demands long-range entanglement (Bell pairs) between QPUs — a scarce and costly resource. Prior work on distributed graph state compilation has addressed this problem either for small numbers of QPUs (k=2), using non-scalable integer-linear programming approaches, or via circuit-model compilation rather than the more resource-efficient measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) model. Critically, all prior approaches minimize the number of edges cut between partitions, which the authors demonstrate is a poor proxy for minimizing Bell pair consumption. This paper addresses the gap: how to scalably partition large algorithmic graph states across arbitrarily many homogeneous QPUs to minimize the actual entanglement cost. Methodology: The paper makes two tightly coupled contributions. First, it introduces the Vertex Cover Grafting (VCG) protocol for generating an arbitrary graph state across k homogeneous QPUs using Y measurements on communication qubits (mayors). VCG requires a number of long-range Bell pairs exactly equal to the sum of maximum matching sizes between each pair of partitions — establishing that the correct optimization objective for partitioning is balanced minimum maximum matching k-partition, not edge minimization. Second, the authors design BURY (a greedy heuristic), which assigns vertices to QPU partitions by iteratively 'burying' vertices — assigning a vertex and its entire neighborhood to the same partition — prioritizing vertices with lowest burial cost (degree + 1), and decrementing neighbor weights to reflect reduced future matching contributions. For k > 2 partitions, BURY is applied repeatedly on residual subgraphs. The algorithm runs in polynomial time and is implemented in Julia as the open-source QuantumHamlets.jl package. The authors additionally show that minimizing matching sizes also reduces cut rank, a universal information-theoretic lower bound on entanglement, implying BURY's partitions are near-optimal for any future generation protocol. Key Results: BURY is evaluated against Kernighan-Lin (KL), Saran-Vazirani, random sampling, and METIS across compiled QAOA graph states, square grid graphs up to 10,000 vertices, and random 3- and 6-regular graphs. On compiled QAOA graph states (the primary target application), BURY outperforms all tested methods including METIS across all numbers of hamlets (4–20 QPUs), with the performance gap widening at larger graph sizes and more partitions. On grid graphs, BURY outperforms METIS on most sizes and partition counts. On random 3-regular graphs, BURY outperforms all methods including METIS; on 6-regular graphs, BURY similarly dominates. METIS (which uses graph coarsening, a multilevel approach) is the only competitive method against BURY, outperforming it on m-regular graphs for m ≥ 4 in the bipartition case. BURY also reduces cut rank consistently across all graph types tested, confirming its generality as an entanglement minimizer independent of the specific generation protocol. Strengths: - Correctly identifies and formalizes the right optimization objective (minimum maximum matching k-partition rather than minimum edge cut), which is a meaningful theoretical contribution that reframes the problem for the entire field - The VCG protocol provides a clean, explicit construction tying the partition objective directly to Bell pair cost, eliminating the gap between partitioning metric and physical resource consumption - BURY is a practical, polynomial-time algorithm with a released open-source implementation (QuantumHamlets.jl), evaluated at scale (up to 10,000-vertex graphs, up to 20 partitions) against strong baselines including METIS Weaknesses / Limitations: - BURY is a greedy heuristic with no approximation guarantee; the paper provides no theoretical bound on how far BURY's solutions can deviate from optimal, leaving the algorithm's worst-case behavior uncharacterized - The work assumes all-to-all connectivity between QPUs (any two hamlets can be entangled), which is unrealistic for most near-term quantum network topologies where connectivity is constrained; extending to constrained network graphs is acknowledged as future work but not addressed - The VCG protocol and BURY both assume homogeneous QPUs with equal qubit counts; the heterogeneous case is mentioned but not solved, limiting practical applicability to near-term deployments where QPU sizes vary
Search Results 2603.06387 PDF
#24 — Innovative (72)

Sparse-BitNet: 1.58-bit LLMs are Naturally Friendly to Semi-Structured Sparsity

Di Zhang, Xun Wu, Shaohan Huang, Yudong Wang, Hanyong Shao, Yingbo Hao, Zewen Chi, Li Dong, Ting Song, Yan Xia, Zhifang Sui, Furu Wei · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Sparse-BitNet identifies a genuinely novel synergy between ternary quantization and N:M sparsity — the mechanistic insight that 1.58-bit weight polarization naturally pre-sorts weights for magnitude-based pruning is well-supported by distribution analysis and rigorously ablated. The Dual STE design and custom 6:8 kernel delivering real 1.30× throughput gains ground the contribution in practical hardware reality. However, experiments are limited to ≤3B parameters, the method requires full pretraining from scratch (no post-training path), and the Pareto optimality claim against alternative compression combinations like 4-bit + 2:4 sparsity is not directly evaluated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference and training efficiency is increasingly bottlenecked by model size and compute cost. Two compression strategies — low-bit quantization (e.g., 1.58-bit ternary BitNet) and semi-structured N:M sparsity — have been studied in isolation, but their interaction is unexplored. The key observation motivating this work is that pretrained 1.58-bit BitNet models exhibit ~42% zero-valued ternary weights naturally (without explicit pruning), arising from a quantization-valley weight distribution. This suggests ternary models may be inherently more compatible with magnitude-based N:M pruning than full-precision BF16 models, which maintain unimodal near-zero weight distributions that make pruning boundary decisions destructive. Methodology: The paper proposes Sparse-BitNet, a unified training framework that jointly applies 1.58-bit ternary quantization and N:M semi-structured sparsity from scratch. The core building block is the Sparse-BitLinear layer, which follows a "quant-and-mask" paradigm: (1) compute the N:M binary mask M_{N:M} from the continuous master weights (not from discrete ternary weights, to avoid tie-breaking instability), (2) apply ternary quantization Q_t(W) to get W_q, (3) apply the mask to get W_eff = W_q ⊙ M_{N:M}. Gradient estimation uses a Dual Straight-Through Estimator (STE): gradients flow through both the quantization rounding and the sparsity mask, meaning masked (zeroed-out) weights still receive gradient updates during backprop. This dense gradient flow is critical — blocking gradients to masked weights causes premature structural collapse where pruned weights cannot re-enter the active set. The mask is recomputed at every training step, enabling dynamic topology exploration. Experiments run on Qwen2.5 architectures at 0.5B, 1.5B, and 3B scales, trained on 50B tokens from RefinedWeb, evaluated on perplexity and five zero-shot benchmarks (HellaSwag, ARC-E, PIQA, BoolQ, COPA). A custom 6:8 sparse CUDA kernel measures end-to-end throughput on NVIDIA A100/B200 GPUs. Key Results: Under 6:8 sparsity (25% weights removed), Sparse-BitNet incurs substantially smaller PPL degradation than sparse BF16: +0.32/+0.24/+0.17 PPL increase for BitNet vs. +1.20/+0.60/+0.45 for BF16 at 0.5B/1.5B/3B. On zero-shot downstream tasks, BitNet's average accuracy drop is 1.15/3.79/0.80 points vs. BF16's 3.02/7.71/3.20. Under the stricter 2:4 pattern (50% sparsity), BF16 exceeds a 10% normalized PPL degradation threshold (+18.8%) while BitNet remains stable (+5.7%), with BitNet only crossing the 10% threshold at 3:8 versus BF16 crossing at 4:8. The custom 6:8 sparse kernel achieves up to 1.30× end-to-end throughput speedup on prefill (A100) and 1.18× on decode (B200) for the 3B model. Ablations confirm that: (a) dense gradient flow through masked weights is essential (stopping gradients raises PPL from 26.31 to 27.48+); (b) mask construction from continuous master weights outperforms mask-from-quantized (PPL 26.31 vs. 32.23); (c) quant-then-mask ordering outperforms mask-then-quant; (d) sparse-from-scratch training is superior to dense-to-sparse schedules at equivalent token budgets. Strengths: - The core insight — that ternary quantization induces weight polarization (decreasing near-zero mass) that naturally pre-sorts weights for N:M pruning, creating structural decoupling between pruning thresholds and active weight population — is mechanistically well-motivated and supported by distribution analysis - The Dual STE design choice (dense gradient flow through masked weights) is rigorously ablated and shown to be critical, distinguishing this from prior N:M sparse training that gates gradients by the mask - Practical hardware integration is first-class: a custom 6:8 kernel is implemented and end-to-end throughput (not just FLOPs) is measured, yielding concrete 1.30× speedups Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are confined to Qwen2.5 up to 3B parameters trained on 50B tokens — substantially below production scale (70B+ models, trillions of tokens); whether the sparsity-friendliness advantage holds at scale remains unvalidated - The 1.30× inference speedup is modest given the compound compression (ternary weights + 25% sparsity), and the gap relative to dense BF16 absolute quality means Sparse-BitNet trades accuracy headroom for efficiency — the Pareto optimality claim against other compression combinations (e.g., 4-bit + 2:4 sparsity) is not directly evaluated - The method requires sparse-from-scratch training with a full 50B token budget to match quality; there is no post-training or fine-tuning path, limiting applicability to organizations that can afford pretraining runs
Citations 2603.05168 PDF
#25 — Innovative (72)

Characterizing Memorization in Diffusion Language Models: Generalized Extraction and Sampling Effects

Xiaoyu Luo, Wenrui Yu, Qiongxiu Li, Johannes Bjerva · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper makes a genuine theoretical contribution by generalizing the memorization extraction framework to unify prefix-conditioned ARM extraction and arbitrary-mask DLM extraction under one probabilistic definition, with the monotonicity theorem (Theorem 4.3) providing a principled and empirically validated link between sampling resolution and privacy risk. The controlled experimental design isolating architectural effects from confounds is exemplary. However, the core finding—that DLMs memorize less than ARMs—is somewhat expected given the stochastic masking training procedure, and the evaluation scope remains narrow.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autoregressive language models (ARMs) are known to memorize and reproduce training data verbatim, raising well-documented privacy and copyright concerns. Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as competitive alternatives (LLaDA-8B, LLaDA2.0), yet their memorization behavior remains largely uncharacterized. The fundamental incompatibility between the ARM memorization framework—which assumes prefix-suffix prompting under left-to-right decoding—and DLMs' bidirectional, iterative denoising generation makes existing evaluation tools inapplicable. This paper addresses that gap with the first systematic theoretical and empirical characterization of memorization in DLMs. Methodology: The authors develop a generalized probabilistic extraction framework that extends the (n,p)-discoverable memorization formalism of Hayes et al. (2025) to arbitrary masking patterns and stochastic diffusion sampling trajectories. The core idea replaces the fixed prefix-suffix structure with an arbitrary mask M over token positions; a sequence is (n,p)-extractable if, given the observed tokens, the model recovers the exact masked tokens within n independent queries with probability ≥ p. The single-trial recovery probability p_z is approximated by averaging over R trials and multiple mask patterns. A key theoretical result (Theorem 4.3) proves a monotonicity relationship: under a natural monotonicity-of-recovery assumption (more observed context cannot decrease recovery probability), increasing sampling resolution N—the number of denoising steps—monotonically increases the probability of exact memorization. This leads to Proposition 4.4: ARMs are a limiting case of DLMs where N = |M| (one token recovered per step), explaining why ARMs represent an upper bound on memorization probability. Experiments span pretrained DLMs of 170M, 629M, and 1.1B parameters trained on SlimPajama (matched compute budget of 10^21 FLOPs), an ARM-1.1B baseline with identical setup, and LLaDA-8B fine-tuned on the Enron email dataset. PII evaluation uses 3,000 email and 3,000 phone-number extraction prompts from Enron, with prefix-conditioned verbatim completion as the test condition. Key Results: The theoretical monotonicity result is empirically confirmed: moving from 1-step to per-token (max-step) reconstruction consistently increases exact-recovery probability, with a median log-probability ratio Δlog p rising from 0 at step 1 to approximately 0.8+ at max steps. In aligned PII leakage comparisons, DLMs exhibit substantially lower memorization than scale-matched ARMs: the ARM-1.1B recovers 213 emails at p=50% and 139 at p=99% (within n=10,000 queries over 3,000 prompts), while DLM-1.1B recovers 0 at one-step and only 16 at max-step (p=50%). Phone number memorization is zero across all DLM scales. LLaDA-8B recovers more (179 emails at p=50%, max-step) but was trained with ~100× more FLOPs and ~150× more token exposures than the 1.1B baseline. Memorization scales with model size: the 1.1B DLM extracts more PII than the 170M or 629M under identical conditions. Train/test distribution separation (Enron vs. TREC 2007 Spam) confirms that the metric captures true memorization rather than domain generalization. Strengths: - The generalized framework is a genuine theoretical contribution: it cleanly unifies prefix-conditioned ARM extraction and arbitrary-mask DLM extraction under one probabilistic definition, with ARMs recovered as a formal special case. - Theorem 4.3's monotonicity result is both theoretically grounded and empirically validated, providing a principled link between sampling hyperparameters and privacy risk that practitioners can act on. - The controlled experimental design—same tokenizer, same compute budget, same fine-tuning protocol for DLMs and ARMs—meaningfully isolates architectural effects from confounds, making the DLM vs. ARM comparisons interpretable. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The monotonicity proof (Theorem 4.3) relies on Assumption 4.2, which is validated only empirically and only for correctly-recovered intermediate tokens; it may not hold when the denoising trajectory contains errors that propagate, a realistic failure mode in practice. - The evaluation is limited to the Enron email dataset for PII and SlimPajama for pretraining validation; it is unclear how findings generalize to other PII types (addresses, SSNs), other domains, or instruction-tuned/RLHF-aligned DLMs—the paper explicitly flags post-training effects as future work. - DLM-1.1B models are small relative to frontier DLMs (LLaDA2.0 at 100B); scaling trends in Figure 4 show memorization growing with model size, raising questions about whether the low-memorization advantage persists at scale, which the paper cannot answer.
Recommended 2603.02333 PDF
#26 — Innovative (72)

SVLL: Staged Vision-Language Learning for Physically Grounded Embodied Task Planning

Yuyuan Yang, Junkun Hong, Hongrong Wang, Honghao Cai, Xunpeng Ren, Ge Wang, Mingcong Lei, Shenhao Yan, Jiahao Yang, Chengsi Yao, Xi Li, Yiming Zhao, Yatong Han, Jinke Ren · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SVLL's primary contribution is its precise diagnostic decomposition of two distinct failure modes in embodied VLM training — premature temporal binding and causal misalignment — each addressed by a targeted training stage with clean ablation evidence. B-DPO's threshold-triggered unlikelihood penalty and expert-anchoring SFT loss are well-motivated fixes to known DPO pathologies in constraint-governed action spaces. Outperforming GPT-4o and 32B models with a 7B architecture on both simulated and real-robot tasks is impressive, though the staged pipeline's training overhead and hyperparameter sensitivity temper the practical novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Embodied task planning requires vision-language models (VLMs) to generate action sequences that are both visually grounded and causally coherent over time. Existing training paradigms suffer from two fundamental failure modes. First, joint end-to-end supervised fine-tuning causes premature temporal binding: models learn to shortcut reasoning by over-relying on textual action history rather than grounding decisions in current visual observations, producing repetitive or stale actions when environment state changes. Second, applying standard Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to embodied planning causes causal misalignment: DPO's purely relative ranking objective ignores absolute likelihood constraints on expert trajectories, causing the policy to drift away from the expert distribution (likelihood displacement) and produce confident but physically invalid actions. These problems are especially acute in embodied domains where actions must satisfy strict physical affordance constraints. Methodology: The paper proposes SVLL, a unified three-stage training framework built on Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Stage 1 (Spatial Grounding) trains the model on history-free data by explicitly blocking the action history channel (h_t = ∅), forcing the model to resolve actions purely from current visual observations. The vision encoder is frozen throughout to preserve pretrained visual features. Stage 2 (Temporal Reasoning) initializes from the Stage 1 checkpoint and unlocks the full action history, fine-tuning on sequential trajectory data so the model learns causal temporal dependencies without becoming over-reliant on them. Stage 3 introduces Bias-DPO (B-DPO), a novel alignment objective that modifies standard DPO in three ways: (1) an amplified positive weight (w > 1) on chosen trajectories to reinforce alignment with expert behavior; (2) an auxiliary SFT loss L_SFT that anchors the policy to the expert manifold, explicitly preventing likelihood displacement; and (3) a threshold-triggered unlikelihood penalty L_UL that activates only when the rejected trajectory's likelihood exceeds a safety threshold τ, surgically suppressing overconfident hallucinations without destabilizing the policy. The total loss is L_total = a·L_DPO + b·L_SFT + L_UL. Negative preference samples for Stage 3 are constructed from four predefined physical constraint violation categories (invalid navigation, repetitive search, erroneous navigation, failure to terminate). Key Results: On the AI2-THOR interactive embodied planning benchmark, SVLL-Stage 3 achieves 78.35% Success Rate (SR), 68.53 Success weighted by Path Length (SPL), 89.64% Valid Action Rate (VAR), 7.22% Loop Rate (LR), and 26.34% Constraint Violation Rate (CVR). This outperforms closed-source models—GPT-4o (48.45% SR, 40.32% CVR) and Gemini-2.0-flash (58.76% SR, 31.55% CVR)—as well as the open-source embodied-specific RoboBrain2.0-32B (27.84% SR, 52.61% CVR), despite SVLL using only 7B parameters. Ablations confirm the contribution of each stage: skipping Stage 1 (Direct Stage 2 baseline) raises LR to 10.31% and CVR to 30.65%, demonstrating premature temporal binding; adding Stage 3 B-DPO over Stage 2 reduces CVR from 29.18% to 26.34% while raising VAR from 84.16% to 89.64%. In real-world robot deployment across 9 tasks on a 6-DoF manipulator, SVLL achieves 55.56% SR and 4.35% CVR versus RoboBrain2.0-32B's 22.22% SR and 10.94% CVR, demonstrating strong zero-shot sim-to-real transfer with a 4.6× smaller model.
