Strengths: - Precise diagnostic framing: the paper cleanly identifies and independently validates two distinct failure modes (premature temporal binding and causal misalignment) with targeted ablations for each stage, making the contribution scientifically rigorous rather than purely empirical. - B-DPO is a principled and well-motivated fix to likelihood displacement—a known but underaddressed flaw of standard DPO in structured, constraint-governed action spaces—with a clean theoretical justification. - Outperforms significantly larger models (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0-flash, RoboBrain2.0-32B at 32B parameters) with only 7B parameters, demonstrating that architectural alignment quality can substitute for brute-force parameter scaling. Weaknesses / Limitations: - B-DPO's strong anchoring to expert trajectories is also its Achilles' heel: it risks overfitting to suboptimal or noisy demonstrations, potentially missing more efficient or novel strategies that deviate from the training distribution. - Sim-to-real transfer relies on pixel-level visual alignment without domain randomization or perception augmentation, limiting robustness to unstructured lighting, occlusion, and sensor noise in diverse real-world deployments. - The three-stage pipeline incurs substantial training overhead (~29 total GPU-hours on 8× A100s) and requires careful hyperparameter tuning across multiple objectives (a, b, w, τ, ε), which may limit accessibility and reproducibility outside well-resourced settings.
Search Results 2603.11563 PDF
#27 — Innovative (72)

Retrieving Minimal and Sufficient Reasoning Subgraphs with Graph Foundation Models for Path-aware GraphRAG

Haonan Yuan, Qingyun Sun, Junhua Shi, Mingjun Liu, Jiaqi Yuan, Ziwei Zhang, Xingcheng Fu, Jianxin Li · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: GFM-Retriever introduces a principled Information Bottleneck formulation for label-free subgraph selection — a genuine theoretical contribution with tractable bounds and Gumbel-Sigmoid relaxation that avoids the need for ground-truth subgraph supervision. The cross-domain GFM pre-training grounded in graded modal logic expressivity (Proposition 4.1) provides rare theoretical justification for generalization in a field dominated by empirical claims. The path-aware prompting that converts opaque subgraph structure into interpretable relational chains is practically significant. However, the heavy pre-training cost (8×A100, 700K instances, 60 KG indexes) and reliance on upstream KG quality limit accessibility and real-world robustness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing GraphRAG systems treat graphs as intermediate artifacts and reduce retrieval outputs to ranked entity/document lists, discarding the relational structure essential for multi-hop reasoning. The few subgraph-based retrieval methods rely on heuristic rules tightly coupled to domain-specific distributions, making them brittle in cold-start scenarios where target-domain data is scarce. This yields reasoning contexts that are either informationally incomplete (missing critical paths) or structurally redundant (noisy, irrelevant neighbors). The paper identifies three compounding challenges: (1) generalizing subgraph retrieval across domains, (2) principled identification of minimal yet sufficient subgraphs without labeled ground-truth subgraphs, and (3) bridging retrieved graph structure to LLM generation via explicit relational paths. Methodology: The proposed GFM-Retriever is a three-stage framework. In the pre-training stage, a Graph Foundation Model (GFM) is trained as a cross-domain retriever via query-conditioned message passing that initializes query-entity embeddings with higher activation, propagates through relation-aware triple encoding, and is supervised with a combined BCE + ranking loss. Cross-domain alignment is enforced via prototype-driven contrastive learning (pulling entity embeddings toward their domain prototypes) and an Information Gain Contrast (IGC) regularizer that explicitly contrasts informative semantic alignment against random perturbations — yielding a two-phase pre-training objective over a corpus of 700K instances spanning 60 knowledge graph indexes with 14M triples. In the fine-tuning stage, the frozen GFM backbone is adapted via a lightweight parameter-efficient strategy to learn a label-free subgraph selector. This selector is formulated under the Information Bottleneck (IB) principle: minimize subgraph size (compress) while maximizing mutual information with the query (sufficiency). Since direct IB optimization requires ground-truth labels, the paper derives a label-free proxy replacing the answer with the query itself, then establishes tractable upper (size + Laplacian connectivity penalty) and lower (InfoNCE contrastive alignment) bounds. Discrete node selection is made differentiable via Gumbel-Sigmoid relaxation. In the inference stage, the retrieved subgraph is converted into structured in-context prompts by extracting relational reasoning paths via l-hop depth-first search, scoring paths by entity selection confidence and relation depth penalty, and formatting entity–relation chains as explicit path prompts for the downstream LLM. Key Results: On three standard multi-hop QA benchmarks (HotpotQA, MuSiQue, 2WikiMultiHopQA), GFM-Retriever achieves average rank 1.1 on retrieval quality across R@2/R@5 at both entity and document levels, outperforming the next best method (PropRAG, rank 2.8) and all 18 baselines including graph-enhanced methods (HippoRAG 2, SubgraphRAG, GFM-RAG) and multi-step methods (IRCoT, FLARE). On end-to-end QA, GFM-Retriever standalone achieves average rank 3.0, and GFM-Retriever+IRCoT reaches rank 1.3 — best overall. Specific numbers include 60.5 EM / 72.9 F1 on HotpotQA and 42.8 EM / 48.7 F1 on MuSiQue with IRCoT augmentation. On cross-domain generalization (7 out-of-domain datasets spanning biomedical, customer support, general knowledge), GFM-Retriever consistently outperforms all baselines on both retrieval (R@5_D) and generative QA (ROUGE-L), with especially strong gains on PubMedQA, DelucionQA, and HAGRID where domain shift is severe. Efficiency analysis shows sub-second retrieval latency at near-highest R@5_D recall, dominating the Pareto frontier. Ablations confirm that removing any of the five core components (Proto+IGC, fine-tuning, subgraph selector, path prompting) consistently degrades both retrieval and QA performance. Strengths: - Principled IB formulation for subgraph selection without labeled subgraph supervision is a genuine technical contribution — the label-free proxy derivation, tractable bounds, and Gumbel-Sigmoid relaxation form a coherent theoretical pipeline backed by four formal propositions with proofs. - Cross-domain GFM pre-training is explicitly grounded in a logical expressivity result (Proposition 4.1) showing query-conditioned GFMs can learn rules expressible in graded modal logic, providing theoretical justification for generalization rather than relying on empirical observation alone. - The path-aware in-context prompter converts opaque subgraph structure into interpretable relational chains that directly guide LLM multi-hop reasoning — this is practically significant and qualitatively validated through case studies showing irrelevant entities are excluded. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The pre-training cost is substantial: 8 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs, a corpus of 700K instances across 60 KG indexes with 14M triples, and a two-phase training procedure — this significantly limits reproducibility and accessibility compared to lighter retrieval baselines. - Relational path extraction via DFS has worst-case exponential complexity O(Δ^l) in the subgraph branching factor and hop depth l; the paper acknowledges this but relies on the compactness of the learned subgraph to make it practical, which is not guaranteed across all query types. - The framework assumes access to a pre-constructed, entity-linked knowledge graph, meaning performance is contingent on upstream KG quality (entity extraction, relation extraction, entity resolution via ColBERTv2 similarity thresholding) — a pipeline dependency that the paper does not systematically analyze.
Citations 2603.07179 PDF
#28 — Innovative (71)

Speculative Decoding Scaling Laws (SDSL): Throughput Optimization Made Simple

Amirhossein Bozorgkhoo, Igor Molybog · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The paper delivers a clean analytical contribution by connecting Chinchilla-style scaling laws to speculative decoding throughput via a closed-form Lambert W expression, yielding the surprisingly simple and actionable rule N* ≈ M/200. The hardware-agnostic formulation and validation across five model families lend credibility. However, this is fundamentally an empirical characterization and curve-fitting exercise rather than a new algorithm or paradigm — the scaling law framework and speculative decoding formula are both pre-existing, and the practical value depends on the affine acceptance-rate fit generalizing beyond HellaSwag.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by using a small draft model to generate candidate tokens that a larger target model verifies in parallel. Its effectiveness is highly sensitive to the choice of draft model: too small and acceptance rates drop; too large and the computational savings vanish. Current practice requires expensive empirical search across model families to find a good draft model. This paper asks whether the optimal draft model size can be predicted analytically, before any training occurs, by connecting pre-training scaling laws to speculative decoding throughput. Methodology: The authors build a closed-form framework (SDSL) in three layers. First, they establish an affine relationship α = Ax + By + C between draft model perplexity (x), target model perplexity (y), and the expected token acceptance rate α, fitting this plane empirically across dozens of draft–target pairs from OPT, Qwen 1.5, Qwen 2.5, LLaMA 3, LLaMA 3.1, and Seed-OSS model families evaluated on HellaSwag. Second, they express throughput in tokens per FLOP (hardware-agnostic) using the Leviathan et al. formula, optimize over lookahead length γ analytically, and obtain a closed-form throughput expression involving the Lambert W function: T = −log(α) / [2N(−1+α)W(−(α^{M/N−1})/e)]. Third, they substitute the Chinchilla-style pre-training scaling law L(N,D) to express throughput purely in terms of target size M, draft size N, and training token counts D, D'. Numerically maximizing this expression over a dense grid of (N, D, M, D') configurations reveals that the throughput-optimal draft size N grows approximately linearly with M and is largely insensitive to dataset sizes. They fit a simple linear rule N(M) = μM + M₀ via pooled least-squares regression with heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3) standard errors. Key Results: The dominant finding is that the asymptotic draft-to-target ratio μ ≈ 2.7×10⁻³, meaning the throughput-optimal draft model is approximately 200× smaller than the target. This ratio is stable across all tested model families and training data scales. The additive offset M₀ accounts for finite-size corrections (the ratio N*/M decreases monotonically toward μ as M grows). Dataset size effects are statistically mild: increasing draft training data slightly reduces optimal relative draft size (negative γ coefficient), while target training data size has negligible effect (γ' statistically indistinguishable from zero). The α affine plane fits with high R² across families, with draft perplexity being the dominant predictor and target perplexity adding only residual explanatory power. Wall-clock latency measurements on OPT-13B confirm that the tokens/FLOP predictions translate to real hardware throughput trends. Strengths: - Delivers a genuinely actionable and simple result (N* ≈ M/200) grounded in a principled analytical derivation rather than heuristics, enabling draft model architecture decisions before any pre-training begins. - Hardware-agnostic throughput formulation in tokens/FLOP is a methodologically sound choice that avoids conflating hardware-specific bottlenecks with algorithmic efficiency, and the authors validate the metric against wall-clock measurements. - Broad empirical validation across five distinct model families and both draft families (OPT, Qwen 1.5, Qwen 2.5) with confidence intervals reported, lending credibility to the universality of the linear scaling law. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework assumes draft and target models share comparable training data distributions and similar training recipes; violations due to domain specialization, post-training alignment (RLHF/DPO), or substantial architectural asymmetry (e.g., MoE targets with dense drafts) are explicitly out of scope and could shift the fitted coefficients substantially. - Empirical validation of α is conducted solely on HellaSwag (commonsense sentence completion); whether the affine fit and the derived optimal draft sizes generalize to code generation, long-form reasoning, or retrieval-augmented settings—where token distributions differ markedly—is not demonstrated.
Search Results 2603.11053 PDF
#29 — Innovative (71)

Optimal Expert-Attention Allocation in Mixture-of-Experts: A Scalable Law for Dynamic Model Design

Junzhuo Li, Peijie Jiang, Changxin Tian, Jia Liu, Zhiqiang Zhang, Xuming Hu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper earns recognition for identifying and formalizing the expert-to-attention FLOPs ratio as a first-order architectural variable with a quantifiable, predictable optimum—a design dimension that practitioners have ignored or tuned heuristically. The out-of-sample validation on a held-out sparsity level is a meaningful methodological strength. However, the work is fundamentally an extension of Chinchilla-style scaling law methodology to a new variable, the model scales are modest (≤5B active parameters), and the narrow sweep range limits confidence in extrapolation to frontier regimes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformers introduce an architectural degree of freedom absent from dense models: how to distribute a fixed per-token compute budget between self-attention layers and expert (feed-forward) layers. In practice this allocation is inherited from dense Transformer recipes or tuned heuristically, and existing scaling laws (Chinchilla and its MoE extensions) treat the internal compute split as a fixed constant. This paper asks whether there is a principled, compute-aware optimum for the expert-to-attention FLOPs ratio r = C_E/C_A, and whether that optimum varies predictably with scale and sparsity. Methodology: The authors define sparsity S = (E − E_act)/E (fraction of inactive experts per token) and the FLOPs ratio r as the two key architectural variables, then sweep r ∈ [0.2, 1.5] while holding total per-token compute C fixed across GPT-style decoder-only MoE models (100M–5B active parameters, up to 1×10²¹ training FLOPs, E ∈ {17, 33, 65, 129} experts, sparsity levels S ∈ {82–98%}). For each (C, S) pair they identify r empirically as the FLOPs ratio that minimizes validation loss. They fit the relationship r(C, S) = α_r(S) · C^{β_r(S)} and further show that both α_r and β_r follow power-laws in the activation ratio (1−S). Building on this, they extend the Chinchilla loss formula with two extra terms—one penalizing expert misallocation via an efficiency saturation term c·e^R(1−S)^γ/N^λ and one penalizing excessive expert allocation d·r/(r+1)—yielding an extended scaling law (Eq. 2) with nine fitted coefficients estimated via L-BFGS. Experiments fix context length to 4096 tokens, vocabulary to 128k tokens, and activate 3 experts per token (1 shared + 2 routed) throughout. Key Results: The optimal FLOPs ratio r is a well-defined, smooth function of (C, S): across all compute budgets and sparsity levels the loss surface exhibits a clear valley along r rather than a flat plateau. r increases monotonically with C on log–log scales following r* = α_r · C^{β_r}, confirming scale-dependent allocation. Fitting the sparsity dependence gives α_r = 6.7×10⁻⁵(1−S)^{−1.23} and β_r = 0.24(1−S)^{0.21}: lower-sparsity models (more activated experts per token) require steeper increases in expert compute at scale, while high-sparsity models should retain relatively more attention capacity. The extended scaling law (Eq. 2) fits observed losses tightly both on training data and on a fully held-out sparsity level (S=97.67%), demonstrating out-of-sample generalization. Estimated leading coefficients are α=0.629, β=0.045, λ=0.423, γ=0.043, with the loss baseline τ=13.74. Comparison against Wang et al. (2024) and Abnar et al. (2025) baselines shows those formulations fail to capture high-sparsity or small-model loss trends, whereas the proposed law tracks the full training trajectory accurately (Figure 4). Strengths: - Identifies and formalizes a previously ignored first-order architectural variable (expert–attention FLOPs ratio) and shows it has a quantifiable, predictable optimum—directly actionable for model design under fixed budgets. - The empirical coverage is broad: four sparsity regimes, six model scales spanning two orders of magnitude in active parameters, and training runs up to 10²¹ FLOPs, lending credibility to the power-law fits. - Out-of-sample validation on a held-out sparsity level (S=97.67% excluded from fitting) demonstrates the extended scaling law generalizes rather than merely interpolating. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study is restricted to autoregressive language modeling on a curated multilingual corpus; whether the discovered r*(C,S) law transfers to multimodal architectures, encoder-decoder models, or different routing mechanisms (e.g., adaptive or learned sparsity) is unexamined. - The experimental setup fixes several important hyperparameters—3 activated experts per token, fixed routing policy, no hardware-aware communication costs—that materially affect the compute decomposition in real deployments. The practical r* for production MoE systems trained across heterogeneous clusters may differ substantially from what this idealized FLOP accounting predicts. - The r sweep range [0.2, 1.5] is relatively narrow and the models are small by current standards (≤5B active parameters); whether the power-law extrapolation holds into the frontier 100B+ active parameter regime seen in DeepSeek or Mixtral remains unvalidated.
Recommended 2603.10379 PDF
#30 — Innovative (71)

RECOMMENDER SYSTEMS AND DEMOCRACY: LINGUISTIC DIVERSITY, EPISTEMIC AGENCY, AND CIVIC PARTICIPATION

tara.tcd.ie · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The thesis introduces a genuinely productive theoretical frame — recommender systems as 'cultural technologies of omission' — and backs it with a well-designed multi-strand empirical study bridging computational linguistics, policy analysis, and STS. The cross-jurisdictional finding that omission operates as a structural property of RS regardless of political system (US vs. Russia, YouTube vs. Dzen) is surprising and counterintuitive. However, the work is diagnostic rather than prescriptive, offering no concrete technical interventions, and the four-month observation window during politically heightened events limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Recommender systems (RS) on major video-hosting platforms curate the vast majority of content citizens encounter online — 70% of watched content on YouTube is algorithmically recommended — yet their effects on linguistic diversity, epistemic agency, and democratic participation remain underexamined. Prior work has focused on overt political radicalization (e.g., the "YouTube rabbit hole"), but subtler shifts in vocabulary, framing, and sentiment may reshape civic discourse without being visible to users or regulators. This thesis addresses three interlinked gaps: how RS reshape the language and perspectives users encounter on platforms; how policymakers in contrasting political systems conceptualize and regulate RS; and how academic research itself frames RS in ways that marginalize social and linguistic concerns. Methodology: The thesis employs a three-strand qualitative and comparative design grounded in Frank Webster's four-component theory of the information society (technology, economy, culture, quality). Strand 1 uses 60 demographically differentiated sock puppet (SP) accounts — new accounts with no interaction history to exploit cold-start conditions — divided across four political orientations (US Democratic/Republican, Russia anti-war/pro-war) on YouTube and Dzen. Each SP followed recommendation chains (top-10 next videos) every three days from December 2023 to March 2024, yielding 17,964+ videos. Recommendation outputs were analyzed via Type-Token Ratio (TTR), vocabulary size, lexical density, and VADER sentiment analysis, stratified by gender and five age groups (18–64). Strand 2 applies critical discourse analysis and corpus linguistics to AI policy documents from the EU, US, and Russia, using keyword frequency, co-occurrence analysis, and normalized per-million growth rates to compare how terms like "recommendation," "bias," "transparency," and "market" are deployed. Strand 3 analyzes a corpus of RS research publications (2010–2024) across four thematic frames: governance/accountability, agency/autonomy, technical instrumentalism, and risk/mitigation, mapping how social and linguistic concerns are treated relative to technical solutions. Key Results: The case studies show that RS narrow lexical diversity by amplifying formulaic, repetitive discourse: both US partisan camps and Russian pro-/anti-war accounts exhibit low TTR despite large raw vocabularies, indicating high repetition of dominant frames rather than exposure to varied perspectives. Sentiment analysis reveals demographically differentiated affective environments — younger users are consistently exposed to more negatively valenced content, suggesting age-targeted engagement optimization. The Russian case demonstrates that RS operate through similar omission patterns regardless of political system: Dzen amplifies pro-war material while YouTube amplifies anti-war content, yet both platforms narrow the linguistic range of political expression in structurally equivalent ways. Policy analysis reveals three divergent governance paradigms: the EU foregrounds rights and risk (AI Act framing); the US prioritizes market efficiency and innovation; Russia centers state control and information management. None operationalizes linguistic diversity or epistemic agency as regulatory objectives. The research discourse analysis finds that technical instrumentalism dominates the literature — loss functions, accuracy metrics, and algorithmic efficiency are central, while social, linguistic, and democratic dimensions are treated as secondary or externally imposed constraints. The thesis introduces the concept of RS as "cultural technologies of omission," situating them historically alongside other filtering technologies (indexes, search engines, editorial gatekeepers) that exercise power through what they exclude rather than what they promote. Strengths: - Innovative multi-strand design that bridges computational linguistics, policy analysis, and science and technology studies, connecting platform behavior to regulatory discourse to academic framing in a unified argument - The sock puppet methodology exploits cold-start conditions to isolate algorithmic influence from user history, providing cleaner evidence of RS-driven exposure patterns than studies using organic users - The cross-jurisdictional and cross-platform comparison (democratic vs. authoritarian context, US vs. Russian platforms) surfaces a key finding: omission operates as a structural property of RS regardless of political system, undermining exceptionalist narratives about any one regime - The "cultural technologies of omission" framing is theoretically productive, situating RS within a longer history of information-filtering technologies and providing a vocabulary for regulation that goes beyond bias or misinformation Weaknesses / Limitations: - The four-month observation window captures a politically heightened period (Super Tuesday 2024, Russian elections) that may not generalize to routine recommendation behavior; longitudinal replication is needed - The sock puppet accounts follow only the top recommendation chain without interaction (watching, liking, commenting), meaning the data reflects cold-start and passive-browsing RS behavior rather than the fully personalized RS that most active users encounter - Lexical diversity metrics (TTR, vocabulary size) are corpus-size sensitive and can conflate structural repetition with dataset artifacts; while the study applies close-reading diagnostics to mitigate this, causal attribution from TTR patterns to RS design choices remains inferential - The thesis does not produce prescriptive technical interventions — it diagnoses the problem and argues for reframing governance, but concrete RS design modifications (alternative loss functions, diversity-aware evaluation metrics) are left largely to future work
Citations
#31 — Innovative (71)

Blackbird Language Matrices: A Framework to Investigate the Linguistic Competence of Language Models

Paola Merlo, Chunyang Jiang, Giuseppe Samo, Vivi Nastase · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Blackbird Language Matrices introduces a genuinely creative evaluation paradigm by adapting Raven's Progressive Matrices to linguistic competence testing, enabling multi-dimensional probing of grammatical rule internalization that goes well beyond binary minimal-pair benchmarks like BLiMP. The error taxonomy with labeled violation types allows fine-grained diagnostic attribution absent in prior frameworks. However, the deliberate use of simple baselines (FFNN, VAE) rather than evaluating modern autoregressive LLMs via prompting limits the paper's immediate relevance to current practice.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models exhibit impressive surface fluency yet fail to generalize systematically or abstract linguistic rules in human-like ways. Existing diagnostic benchmarks such as BLiMP offer only binary minimal-pair contrasts, which cannot reveal whether a model has internalized the underlying grammatical rules or merely exploited surface cues. The paper argues that investigating LLM linguistic competence requires curated, richly structured datasets that encode multi-dimensional linguistic patterns—object induction, structure dependency, and compositional systematicity—across sequences of sentences rather than isolated pairs. No prior framework provided this combination of naturalistic grounding, formal linguistic rigor, and multi-level contrastive structure. Methodology: The authors introduce Blackbird Language Matrices (BLMs), multiple-choice linguistic puzzles modeled after Raven's Progressive Matrices (RPMs). A BLM instance consists of a context sequence of 7 sentences encoding a linguistic paradigm (e.g., spray/load verb alternation, subject-verb agreement, causative/inchoative alternation) and an answer set of 8–9 candidates, only one of which correctly continues the pattern. Incorrect candidates violate specific rules—syntactic form, lexical selection, syntax-semantics mapping, or sequence progression—enabling fine-grained error attribution. Datasets span 7 linguistic phenomena (spray/load alternation, Change-of-State verbs, Roll verbs, Object-drop, Agreement, Sequence of Tense, Mixture) in up to 4 languages (English, French, Italian, Romanian), with three lexical complexity levels (Types I–III). Data creation follows a semi-automatic pipeline: expert-designed templates define the linguistic phenomenon, seed sentences are sourced from corpora or crafted manually, and masked language models (DistilBERT, CamemBERT, RoBERTa-Large, GPT-4) augment seeds while human native-speaker linguists validate grammaticality and semantic plausibility. For experimentation, the authors test three model families. First, FFNN baselines operating on averaged token embeddings from Electra (encoder) and OpenAI-GPT (decoder) solve the BLM multiple-choice task via max-margin scoring. Second, a single-level VAE probes whether chunk-level syntactic structure (NP, PP, VP constituents with semantic role labels) is encoded in sentence embeddings—training the VAE to compress sentences into 5-dimensional latent vectors aligned by chunk pattern. Third, a two-level VAE models the BLM task end-to-end: the sentence-level VAE compresses individual sentences into structural latent codes, which the task-level VAE then sequences and decodes into the correct answer, explicitly exploiting cross-sentence systematic relations. Key Results: The FFNN baseline establishes that BLMs are solvable: F1 scores for verb alternations reach 0.63–0.99 across CoS and OD tasks in English and Italian, and 0.40–0.84 for the hardest spray/load configuration (Type I train, Type III test). Agreement BLMs yield F1 of 0.42–0.84 for English and French, dropping notably for Romanian whose embeddings are trained on less data. Error analysis reveals systematic biases: dominant errors in agreement are WN1/WN2 (wrong number of intervening attractors), indicating that local agreement is learned but sequential progression is not; dominant errors in CoS/OD are I-INT (incorrect intransitive argument mapping), reflecting a preference for Agent-first interpretation consistent with known human biases. The single-level VAE achieves F1 > 0.90 at chunk-pattern detection across all BLM datasets (Table 5), and latent traversal experiments confirm that individual latent units encode interpretable syntactic and semantic properties: active/passive voice, Agent vs. Theme subject, and agentive by-PP vs. other PPs for CoS; grammatical number and pp2 agreement for the French agreement data. Two-dimensional projections (Figure 22) show tight clustering by chunk pattern across BLM types and languages. The two-level VAE improves over the FFNN baseline on Type III (high lexical variation) data for Agreement and Object-drop (Figures 24–25), confirming that explicitly encoding sentence structure helps generalize beyond lexical memorization. Grammatical error rates (AEV, AEN1, AEN2) drop substantially in Agreement with the two-level VAE compared to the FFNN baseline, demonstrating that structurally-induced representations reduce pure grammar mistakes. Strengths: - Formal linguistic rigor: BLM templates are grounded in generative grammar and lexical semantics (Levin verb classes, argument structure), making the diagnostic questions theoretically motivated rather than ad hoc. - Multi-dimensional probing: The same dataset simultaneously supports sentence-level chunking studies, cross-sentence systematicity analysis, error taxonomy by rule violation, and multilingual comparison—a unique combination absent in BLiMP, Holmes, or ARC. - Transparent error attribution: Minimally contrastive answer sets with labeled violation types (e.g., AGENTACT, ALT, SSM, WN1) allow identification of exactly which linguistic rule was violated in each error, enabling far richer diagnostic analysis than accuracy alone. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Scale and coverage are limited: most BLM datasets contain only 2000 training instances and 300–4580 test instances, and phenomena requiring large combinatorics (Sequence of Tense, Mixture) have even fewer examples, constraining the statistical reliability of conclusions. - Baseline architecture is deliberately simple (FFNN over static sentence embeddings), which means the experiments do not directly evaluate modern autoregressive LLMs (GPT-4, Llama, etc.) performing BLMs end-to-end via prompting or few-shot inference—leaving open whether conclusions transfer to the models most relevant to current NLP practice. - The VAE latent dimensionality (size 5) and the particular encoder choices (Electra, OpenAI-GPT) are not ablated systematically, making it unclear how sensitive the structural encoding results are to these architectural decisions.

#32 — Innovative (71)

Matching clinicians with clinical trials using AI

nature.com · Jimeng Sun - new articles
Why this rank: Scores 71 for reframing clinical trial site selection as a population-level recommendation problem rather than patient-level eligibility matching — a genuinely underexplored formulation. The cross-modal fusion of unstructured trial documents, structured claims data, and enrollment graphs via heterogeneous GNNs is technically cohesive, and the genetic optimization for joint accuracy-fairness-conflict minimization addresses real operational constraints. However, the core components (ClinicalBERT, LightGCN, BPR loss, genetic algorithms) are individually well-established, and the reliance on OpenPayments as an enrollment proxy and zip-code demographics as fairness proxies introduces fundamental validity concerns that limit the practical reliability of the system.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Clinical trial site selection is a persistently inefficient process: 37% of sites under-enroll, 11% enroll no participants, and 80% of trials miss timelines at costs of $600K–$8M per day. Existing AI approaches focus on patient-level eligibility matching (individual classification), while the upstream problem of selecting which clinician investigators to recruit for a trial—a population-level recommendation task—remains largely unsolved. Manual methods are slow, geographically biased, and overlook qualified sites. The gap is an AI framework that jointly optimizes recommendation accuracy, demographic fairness, and operational efficiency (minimizing competing trial conflicts). Methodology: DocTr is a cross-modal deep learning recommendation framework that integrates three heterogeneous data sources: (1) unstructured trial documents (summaries and eligibility criteria from ClinicalTrials.gov), encoded via ClinicalBERT with mean pooling; (2) structured patient encounter data from IQIA medical claims, represented as ICD-10 code frequency vectors compressed into latent embeddings via a learned code encoder; and (3) historical clinician–trial payment relationships from OpenPayments, used as a proxy for past trial enrollment. These modalities are fused through a heterogeneous graph neural network (Hetero-LightGCN) that propagates information across a bipartite clinician–trial enrollment graph to produce structural embeddings. Semantic and structural embeddings are combined via a learned scalar α into a final matching score, trained with Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) loss. A genetic optimization algorithm post-processes ranked lists to balance accuracy against fairness (race, ethnicity, geographic diversity measured via entropy) and competing trial minimization, using a fitness function that combines matching scores, entropy-based fairness metrics for sensitive attributes, and a penalty for concurrent trial conflicts. The full dataset links 5,210 clinical trials with 24,984 US clinicians evaluated under both random and temporal split settings. Key Results: Under the temporally challenging split (train on 1989–2019, test on 2020–2026), DocTr achieves a composite similarity score (CS@GT) of 0.60, compared to 0.37–0.42 for the best baselines (NCL, DocTr-semantic), a 58% improvement. CS@5, CS@10, and CS@20 all reach 0.60, consistently outperforming all baselines including specialized clinical trial models (Doc2Vec, FRAMM). The ablation confirms both modalities are necessary: DocTr-semantic (no graph) scores 0.42 and DocTr-structural (no text) scores 0.36. The genetic optimization (DocTr-genetic) raises race entropy by 7% and ethnicity entropy by 17% over the regular recommender without meaningful accuracy degradation, and reduces average competing trials per recommended clinician from 2.1 to near zero (0.03), versus 3.7–4.5 for baselines. Cost estimation achieves CCC = 0.83 for total trial cost, 0.75 for total clinician cost, and 0.84 for average cost per enrolment per clinician. Strengths: - Principled multi-objective formulation combining accuracy, fairness, and operational feasibility in a single deployable system, with the genetic algorithm providing a tunable post-hoc optimization that sponsors can weight by priority - Large-scale evaluation on a realistic linked dataset (24,984 clinicians, 5,210 trials) with a stringent temporal split that genuinely tests zero-shot generalization to unseen trials and clinicians (27.3% new clinicians in test) - Practical cost estimation component (CCC ≈ 0.83) that translates recommendations into actionable financial planning, addressing a real operational need beyond mere ranking accuracy Weaknesses / Limitations: - ICD-10 billing codes as the sole clinician characterization introduce reimbursement-driven biases and sparse vectors for subspecialists (oncology, cardiology average L0 norm 0.4 vs 0.6 elsewhere), degrading performance precisely in high-value therapeutic areas - Fairness proxies are geographically coarse: census zip-code demographics substitute for actual patient panel demographics, a poor approximation for specialists drawing regionally diverse patients; gender, SES, and disability dimensions are entirely excluded due to data unavailability - OpenPayments-derived enrollment graph conflates consulting, speaking, and research payments with actual trial participation, and payments go predominantly to institutions rather than individuals, making cost estimation a directional signal rather than a reliable budget tool
Recommended
#33 — Innovative (71)

Fast and Optimal Differentially Private Frequent-Substring Mining

Peaker Guo, Rayne Holland, Hao Wu · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 71 for eliminating the quadratic-to-quartic computational blowup in differentially private frequent-substring mining, reducing O(n²ℓ⁴) to near-linear O(nℓ log|Σ|) while preserving asymptotically optimal utility. The structural insight combining prefix membership with suffix-tree reuse (Lemma 4.4) and the heavy-light decomposition for online DP counting are technically sharp and reusable. However, the contribution is primarily an algorithmic speedup of an existing framework with no empirical validation, and the binary encoding introduces a log|Σ| penalty that widens for large alphabets.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Frequent substring mining from user-contributed text is foundational for NLP applications (autocomplete, response suggestion, next-word prediction), genomic analysis, and transit pattern discovery. Mining from private corpora risks exposing sensitive personal information — medical conditions, locations, genetic variants — that language models may memorize. Differential privacy (DP) offers a principled solution, but the prior state-of-the-art (Bernardini et al., PODS'25) achieves near-optimal error guarantees at a prohibitive O(n²ℓ⁴) time and space cost — infeasible for real datasets such as Reddit, where n ≈ 10⁶ users each contribute strings of combined length ℓ ≥ 3000. The central question is whether near-linear time and space can be achieved while retaining asymptotically optimal utility guarantees under ε-DP. Methodology: The paper presents a new ε-differentially private algorithm reducing time from O(n²ℓ⁴ + |Σ|) to O(nℓ_bit log|Σ| + |Σ|) and space from O(n²ℓ⁴) to O(nℓ + |Σ|), where ℓ_bit = ℓ(⌈log|Σ|⌉ + 1). Three innovations drive this improvement. First, a binary encoding preprocessing step converts each symbol from Σ into a fixed-length binary codeword plus a terminal delimiter $. Working over {0,1,$} reduces the L₁-sensitivity of character-aligned substring frequency vectors from O(ℓ²) to O(2ℓ), enabling tighter noise calibration. Second, a refined candidate-generation strategy avoids the |C_k|² pairwise explosion of prior work. The key structural observation (Lemma 4.4) is that any τ_T-frequent substring of length k+t must begin with a string in C_k (the set of length-k frequent candidates) and end with a suffix that itself appears in C_k. This permits constructing a compact sparse suffix tree T_k over C_k once per phase and searching concatenated trees s ∘ T_k for s ∈ C_k — limiting candidates to O(nℓ/τ_⊥) rather than O(|C_k|²). Third, noisy frequency estimation during traversal uses the Binary Tree mechanism combined with a heavy-light decomposition of T_k. Each heavy path in T_k maintains its own Binary Tree mechanism tracking frequency-difference prefix sums, enabling on-the-fly DP counting in O(log d) time per node while bounding the total sensitivity across all traversals. The algorithm runs for ⌈log ℓ⌉ phases, inductively discovering frequent substrings of doubling lengths; pruning is applied whenever a noisy count falls below threshold τ, eliminating entire subtrees from search. Key Results: Time complexity drops from O(n²ℓ⁴ + |Σ|) to O(nℓ_bit log|Σ| + |Σ|), and space from O(n²ℓ⁴) to O(nℓ + |Σ|), eliminating both the quadratic dependence on n and the quartic dependence on ℓ. The frequency threshold τ_T is Θ(ℓ ln²(nℓ/β)/ε) — matching the prior work's guarantee up to polylogarithmic factors in |Σ|. For small alphabets (e.g., genomic data with |Σ| = 4), the log|Σ| factors are negligible, making the algorithm effectively optimal in practice. The algorithm satisfies ε-DP and outputs all substrings with frequency ≥ τ_T while excluding those with frequency ≤ τ_⊥ = Ω(ℓ log ℓ_bit), with probability at least 1 − β. All results are purely theoretical; no empirical experiments are presented. Strengths: - Eliminates the quadratic blowup in both n and ℓ, shifting differentially private frequent-substring mining from asymptotically infeasible to near-linear — a practically significant advance for real corpora - The structural insight in Lemma 4.4, combining prefix membership with suffix-tree reuse, is technically sharp and cleanly avoids the pairwise candidate explosion that made prior work quadratic - The combination of heavy-light decomposition with the Binary Tree mechanism for online DP prefix-sum counting is a reusable technique applicable beyond this specific problem - Full formal proofs of both privacy (ε-DP) and utility (inclusion-exclusion criterion) are provided, with near-matching lower bounds confirming optimality up to polylog factors Weaknesses / Limitations: - No empirical evaluation whatsoever: without experiments on real datasets, constant factors, practical speedups over prior work, and scalability behavior remain unknown — the authors themselves flag this as future work - The binary encoding introduces a log|Σ| multiplicative factor in the frequency threshold τ_T relative to Bernardini et al., meaning the algorithm detects slightly less frequent substrings; for large alphabets this gap widens - Memory requirement O(nℓ) for the full suffix tree of all input strings, while substantially better than O(n²ℓ⁴), remains non-trivial for very long strings in practice
Search Results 2603.09166 PDF
#34 — Innovative (69)

SkillTracer: Structural Failure Attribution and Refinement of Agentic Skills in Long-Horizon Web Tasks

openreview.net · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SkillTracer's reconceptualization of skills as attributed plan graphs rather than monolithic macros is a meaningful structural insight that makes failure attribution tractable by construction. The 56.3% cross-domain gain and clean ablations demonstrate the representation's practical value. However, the verifiable preconditions are limited to two simple predicates, and the 10-step episode cap constrains evaluation of truly long-horizon behavior.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-horizon web agents frequently fail without clear attribution of where or why execution broke down. Existing skill-based systems represent skills as monolithic program macros (e.g., Python functions), making the internal decision structure opaque during execution. When failure occurs — due to UI drift, unexpected page states, or grounding errors — the agent observes only a terminal failure signal with no visibility into which intermediate decision caused the breakdown. This prevents precise diagnosis and targeted repair, reducing recovery to blind global re-planning or episodic retries. The paper identifies 'attribution ambiguity' as the core problem: the inability to align negative outcomes with specific internal decisions that produced them. Methodology: SkillTracer reimagines skills as attributed plan graphs rather than atomic macros. Each skill is formalized as a directed attributed graph G = (V, E), where nodes encode atomic interaction decisions (each a tuple of interaction intent τ, UI component type γ, executable operation α, and semantic description δ), and edges carry programmatically verifiable preconditions — specifically binary checks for component availability and anchor text presence (deterministic string matching on the AXTree). During execution, a four-module pipeline operates: a Planner adapts the skill graph to the current subgoal, an Executor grounds abstract nodes to concrete UI elements, a Summarizer aggregates execution traces to detect failure and trigger repair, and a Skill Inducer updates the library. Upon failure at step t, a Diagnostic Reasoning Module synthesizes evidence from three signals — transition evidence (DOM state changes), verification evidence (edge precondition outcomes), and memory evidence (failure history) — to select a localized repair operator Ω targeting a specific plan node. Repairs are hierarchical: Level 1 corrects interaction intent, Level 2 corrects component type, Level 3 corrects the executable action, and Level 4 refines semantic description. A dual-layer memory system maintains short-term memory (failure-repair tuples within an episode to prevent cyclic loops) and long-term memory (validated repairs consolidated across episodes). Skills retrieved via RAG (top-k=3) are reusable structural templates with symbolic placeholders, enabling transfer across heterogeneous web environments. Key Results: Evaluated on WebArena (812 tasks across Shopping, Admin, Reddit, GitLab, Map, and Cross-domain settings) with GPT-4o as backbone, SkillTracer achieves an average success rate of 57.8%, compared to 40.4% (ASI), 29.8% (SkillWeaver), and 35.5% (AWM) — improvements of 43.1%, 94.0%, and 62.8% respectively. In the cross-domain setting, SkillTracer achieves 32.5% vs. the strongest baseline at 20.8% (+56.3% relative gain). Ablation studies confirm both components are critical: removing skill reuse drops Shopping success from 65.7% to 45.16% (−20.5pp) and increases token cost by 31.7%; removing the repair mechanism drops Shopping from 65.7% to 44.4% (−21.3pp). Skill induction token cost is reduced 66.5% vs. ASI (1,932 vs. 5,773 tokens) due to subgoal-scoped induction rather than full-horizon trajectory extraction. Strengths: - The plan-graph representation is a principled and practically useful alternative to monolithic macros — it makes failure attribution tractable by construction, since each node is independently verifiable and repairable without global re-planning. - The dual-layer memory architecture (short-term for cycle prevention, long-term for cross-episode knowledge accumulation) is well-designed and directly addresses the continual refinement problem; the structural conservative objective (minimizing failure risk + structural edit distance) provides a theoretically grounded update rule. - Empirical results are strong and consistently reproducible across multiple heterogeneous web domains with clean ablations that isolate each component's contribution; the 56.3% cross-domain gain is particularly compelling evidence that hierarchical plan graphs generalize better than flat skill representations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The verifiable preconditions are restricted to just two deterministic predicates (component availability and anchor text presence), which may be insufficient for complex multi-step dependencies or semantically rich page states where grounding failures arise from ambiguous or dynamic content not captured by string matching. - The system caps episodes at 10 interaction steps, which may artificially limit performance on genuinely complex tasks requiring longer trajectories; it is unclear how the repair and rollback mechanisms scale as horizon length increases beyond this cap. - The paper does not evaluate failure cases where the plan graph itself is structurally incorrect from induction (i.e., where the original skill abstraction was wrong rather than merely misgrounded), nor does it analyze how the skill library degrades or stabilizes over many episodes of continual refinement.
Search Results
#35 — Innovative (68)

Orion: Characterizing and Programming Apple's Neural Engine for LLM Training and Inference

Ramchand Kumaresan · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Orion delivers the first end-to-end system for both inference and training on Apple's Neural Engine by reverse-engineering undocumented private APIs and introducing delta compilation — a genuinely clever insight that exploits the ANE's unload/reload interface to surgically patch compiled weight blobs without re-invoking the compiler, reducing recompilation overhead by 8.5×. The systematic discovery and documentation of 14 previously unknown ANE constraints, combined with rigorous root-causing of three interacting NaN bugs, represents substantial engineering novelty. However, validation on a single 110M-parameter model on a toy dataset, dependence on fragile private APIs, and absence of quantization support temper the broader impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Over two billion Apple devices ship with the Apple Neural Engine (ANE), a dedicated 16-core NPU delivering up to 38 TOPS (actual ~19 TFLOPS fp16) — yet this hardware is entirely unused for LLM training and remains difficult to target directly for inference. Apple's public interface, CoreML, operates as a black-box scheduler that cannot guarantee ANE dispatch, does not support gradient computation, and buries the native instruction set (MIL, compiled to E5 microcode) behind undocumented private APIs. Prior efforts (maderix, ANEgpt) cracked the API surface and demonstrated transformer forward/backward passes, but none produced a complete, stable system: ANEgpt diverged to NaN after step 1, lacked a compiler, and used Python orchestration. Orion addresses this gap by delivering the first open end-to-end system that combines direct ANE programming, a compiler pipeline, and stable multi-step training with checkpoint resume. Methodology: Orion is structured in five layers: CLI applications, a model layer (tokenizers, weight loading), a compiler, a core runtime, and the private ANE APIs (_ANEClient, _ANECompiler). The compiler lowers a 27-operation graph IR through five optimization passes — dead code elimination, identity elimination, cast fusion, SRAM annotation, and ANE constraint validation — to ANE-native MIL text programs. The runtime manages IOSurface-backed zero-copy tensor I/O, program caching, and the key innovation: delta compilation. Since the ANE bakes weights at compile time, naive training requires full recompilation (~70 ms/kernel × 60 kernels = ~4,200 ms) after every gradient step. Delta compilation bypasses ANECCompile() entirely: it unloads existing _ANEModel objects, patches BLOBFILE weight files on disk, and reloads — exploiting the fact that reloading with identical MIL text and weight dictionary keys preserves the program's hexStringIdentifier without re-invoking the compiler. This reduces per-step recompilation from 4,200 ms to 494 ms (8.5×). Three NaN-inducing bugs inherited from ANEgpt were fixed: (1) stale programs compiled before checkpoint weights loaded (fixed via deferred compilation), (2) fp16 overflow cascades through softmax (fixed via activation clamping to ±65,504), and (3) corrupted BLOBFILE writes from tensor layout mismatches (fixed via gradient sanitization and post-load NaN/Inf validation). LoRA is implemented with adapter matrices A and B passed as IOSurface inputs rather than baked weights, enabling hot-swap of adapters without recompilation. The system is released as an Objective-C runtime (Python used only for one-time HuggingFace weight conversion) under the MIT license. Key Results: On an M4 Max, Orion achieves 170 tokens/s for GPT-2 124M autoregressive decode and 165 tokens/s for cached prefill (vs. 283 tok/s for CPU cblas_sgemm — ANE decode is slower due to ~2.3 ms IOSurface round-trip overhead per dispatch). CPU–ANE numerical parity is verified: identical 64-token greedy sequences with 100% top-1 agreement. For training, delta compilation reduces 1,000-step wall time from ~85 minutes (v1.0 full recompile + exec() restart per step) to 22.4 minutes (v2.0 delta reload), a 3.8× total speedup. Recompilation fraction drops from 83.9% to 36.8% of step time. Stability is validated across 5 independent 5-step resume chains (25 total steps), with zero NaN occurrences, monotonically decreasing loss in every chain, and consistent step-1 loss std of 0.003. Training throughput is 0.656 TFLOPS. For specific operations, ANE delivers 33.8× speedup over CPU for softmax over a 32,000-token vocabulary. A catalog of 20 ANE programming constraints is documented, 14 of which are newly discovered. Strengths: - Delta compilation is a practically important insight: it identifies and exploits the _ANEModel unload/reload interface to surgically update compiled weight blobs without invoking the ANE compiler, eliminating the fundamental tension between compile-time weight baking and gradient descent. - The constraint catalog (Table 3) is a rigorous, reproducible artifact — 14 previously undocumented MIL IR, memory layout, and numerical constraints discovered through 161 engineering tasks. This lowers the barrier for future ANE systems research. - The engineering rigor around numerical stability is notable: three interacting bugs causing 100% reproducible NaN divergence in the upstream system were isolated, root-caused, and fixed with targeted mechanisms (deferred compilation, fp16 clamping, gradient sanitization), producing verifiable stability across multi-chain stress tests. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Orion depends entirely on undocumented private Apple APIs (_ANEClient, _ANECompiler, _ANEModel) that can break without notice across macOS updates, creating a fragile foundation unsuitable for production deployment. - Delta compilation still consumes 36.8% of step time (494 ms) dominated by disk I/O for BLOBFILE writes (~8 ms/kernel × 60 kernels); the remaining bottleneck is inherent to the file-backed weight patching design, and in-memory patching may not be possible given the current IOSurface-backed model format. - The system is validated only on a single hardware configuration (M4 Max, Mac Studio) with a 110M-parameter model on a toy dataset (TinyStories); generalization to larger models, other Apple silicon variants (M2, M3, A-series), and non-trivial downstream tasks remains undemonstrated. Quantization (INT8/INT4) and learning rate scheduling are not implemented.
Search Results 2603.06728 PDF
#36 — Innovative (68)

SageSched: Efficient LLM Scheduling Confronting Demand Uncertainty and Hybridity

Zhenghao Gan, Yichen Bao, Yifei Liu, Chen Chen, Quan Chen, Minyi Guo · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: SageSched introduces a theoretically grounded scheduling framework that combines three well-motivated components: training-free semantic-aware prediction via embedding retrieval, a unified resource-bound cost model capturing both compute and memory regimes, and Gittins index scheduling with provable optimality guarantees. The training-free predictor is a genuine differentiator over fine-tuned alternatives, and the unified cost formulation C = O²/2 + IO elegantly sidesteps runtime bottleneck detection. The Gittins index application to LLM scheduling is a meaningful transfer of classical theory to a new domain, though the evaluation scope (two models, three English datasets) leaves generalization questions open.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference scheduling targets minimizing Time-to-Last-Token (TTLT), but two intrinsic properties of LLM workloads make this hard. First, demand uncertainty: output token length is non-deterministic and only known at inference completion, so schedulers cannot know job durations in advance. Second, demand hybridity: LLM inferences are simultaneously compute- and memory-intensive due to KVCache growth, unlike conventional OS workloads that are purely compute-bound. Existing schedulers either use FCFS (head-of-line blocking) or approximate shortest-job-first by predicting a single output-length value—losing distributional information and requiring expensive fine-tuned models. All prior work uses output token length alone as a proxy for service cost, ignoring KVCache memory consumption, which leads to suboptimal prioritization especially when memory is the binding constraint. Methodology: SageSched integrates three components on top of vLLM. (1) Semantic-aware history-based predictor: rather than fine-tuning a model to emulate the auto-regressive generation process, SageSched retrieves historically-served requests whose prompt embeddings have cosine similarity ≥ 0.8 to the incoming request (via FAISS over a 10,000-record sliding window) and uses their empirical output-length distribution as the prediction. This requires zero training and generalizes across LLMs. (2) Resource-bound-based cost model: the service cost of a request is modeled as C = O²/2 + I·O (where I = input length, O = output length). This formula captures both the memory-bound case (cumulative KVCache ∝ total sequence length, giving C_M = (O²/2 + I·O)·U_MT) and the compute-bound case (attention computation per step is linear in accumulated sequence length, giving C_C = (O²/2 + I·O)·U_CT). The two cases share the same structural form, so a single unified model works without detecting the bottleneck type at runtime. (3) Uncertainty-aware scheduling via Gittins index: scheduling a request is framed as selecting an arm in a multi-armed bandit with nondeterministic reward. The Gittins index G(D) = inf_{Δ>0} E[min{X,Δ}] / P(X≤Δ) is computed from each request's predicted cost distribution; lower-index requests are prioritized. Gittins scheduling is theoretically optimal for minimizing average latency under unknown duration distributions (Gittins 1979, 1989). Indices are refreshed at bucket boundaries (bucket size 200 tokens) to balance scheduling timeliness against thrashing. Key Results: Across testbed experiments with Llama3.1-8B (A40-48GB) and Qwen3-32B (H800-96GB) on three mixed real-world datasets (SharedGPT, Alpaca-Summarization, Document-Write), SageSched outperforms all baselines (FCFS, FastServe, SSJF, LTR, TRAIL) in average TTLT across all task-rate configurations. At 8 requests/second on Qwen3-32B, SageSched surpasses the second-best scheduler TRAIL by 28.7%. Gains are larger under higher load where resource contention is more severe. Ablation studies confirm all three components contribute: replacing the semantic-aware predictor with a semantic-unaware history predictor or a fine-tuned LLM predictor both degrade TTLT; replacing the resource-bound cost model with output-length-only or overall-length-based models also degrades TTLT by up to 32%; and replacing Gittins ordering with mean-value ordering degrades TTLT even without added noise, while Gittins is substantially more robust to prediction inaccuracies. Prediction latency is 0.37 ms per request (0.22 ms embedding + 0.15 ms retrieval), versus ~3.6 ms for fine-tuned LLM predictors and hours of offline training overhead. Scalability simulations on up to 64 GPU nodes show scheduling overhead grows only linearly with cluster size, remaining ~100 ms per request at 64 nodes—negligible relative to inference latency of seconds to minutes. Strengths: - Training-free prediction is a genuine differentiator: it avoids fine-tuning costs, generalizes across LLMs, and empirically outperforms DistillBert-based predictors in distribution accuracy, demonstrating that semantic similarity retrieval is a more effective proxy for output-length behavior than model emulation. - The Gittins index scheduling policy has a strong theoretical guarantee (optimal for average latency under unknown job durations), providing a rigorous grounding absent from prior heuristic approaches like MLFQ or mean-based ranking. - The unified resource-bound cost model C = O²/2 + IO is a clean formulation that correctly captures both compute- and memory-bound regimes without runtime bottleneck detection, and is validated to outperform simpler alternatives. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The history-based predictor degrades during cold-start when few similar prompts have been served; the fallback to public dataset embeddings partially mitigates this but is ad hoc and not rigorously evaluated. - Experimental evaluation covers only two LLM backends and three datasets, all English conversational/summarization workloads; generalization to code generation, multi-turn dialogue, or reasoning-heavy workloads (where output length distributions may be qualitatively different or more variable) is not demonstrated. - The 28.7% TTLT improvement is reported for a specific high-load regime (8 RPS on Qwen3-32B); improvement numbers vary substantially across setups and models, and the paper does not report statistical significance or variance, making it hard to assess robustness of the headline figure.
Search Results 2603.07917 PDF
#37 — Innovative (68)

Targeted Bit-Flip Attacks on LLM-Based Agents

Jialai Wang, Ya Wen, Zhongmou Liu, Yuxiao Wu, Bingyi He, Zongpeng Li, Ee-Chien Chang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Flip-Agent identifies a genuinely novel attack surface — hardware bit-flip attacks on multi-stage LLM agents — and elegantly solves the non-differentiability challenge through stage-level objectives with attention-enhancement. However, the highly privileged white-box threat model (full weight access plus precise DRAM bit-flipping capability) substantially limits practical relevance, and evaluation on only two benchmarks leaves generalization to complex agentic tasks uncertain.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Targeted bit-flip attacks (BFAs) exploit hardware fault-injection techniques (e.g., RowHammer) to flip bits in DRAM, altering model parameters to produce attacker-desired outputs on trigger-containing inputs while preserving normal behavior elsewhere. Prior BFA work exclusively targets single-step inference models such as image classifiers, where end-to-end differentiability enables gradient-based bit selection. LLM-based agents are fundamentally different: they execute multi-stage pipelines, invoke external tools, and process environmental feedback before generating final outputs. The non-differentiable boundaries introduced by tool calls and multi-stage execution break the assumptions of existing BFA methods, leaving this growing attack surface entirely unexplored. Methodology: The paper introduces Flip-Agent, the first targeted BFA framework for LLM-based agents, which formalizes and exploits two distinct attack surfaces. Attack Surface I (Final Output Steering) causes the agent's final output to be attacker-controlled whenever a trigger appears, either in the user prompt (prompt-level) or in an intermediate-stage input (internal-trigger). Attack Surface II (Invocation Manipulation) redirects which tool or service the agent calls at a specific intermediate stage while leaving the final output unchanged—enabling covert platform redirection or data exfiltration. Both surfaces are unified under a single stage-level objective (Eq. 2) combining a cross-entropy loss over attacker-desired token sequences on triggered inputs and an L2 regularizer to preserve clean-input behavior. Two extensions augment this objective: an attention-enhancement term (Eq. 3) that amplifies attention weights from trigger token positions to target output positions, increasing trigger sensitivity; and a teacher-forcing term (Eq. 4) that enforces structural format consistency in outputs following the injected content. For bit selection, a Prioritized-Search strategy uses gradient magnitudes to partition parameters into high- and low-influence groups (via a heavy-tail-motivated threshold), then iteratively evaluates the per-bit objective reduction among the top-50 high-influence parameters, falling back to low-influence parameters only when no beneficial flip exists there. All models are evaluated under 8-bit quantization with a budget of n_max=50 bit flips. Key Results: Flip-Agent is evaluated on six LLMs (Llama-3.2-3B, AgentLM-7B/13B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-4B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B) on the WebShop benchmark for Attack Surface I and ToolBench for Attack Surface II, against three SOTA baselines (TBT, TrojViT, Flip-S). For prompt-level attacks, Flip-Agent achieves ASR of 92.6%–99.2% across all models with CDA of 90%–100%, while the strongest baseline on each model reaches only 61.1%–88.9% ASR. For internal-trigger attacks, Flip-Agent achieves 22.2%–94.4% ASR vs. baselines often below 30%, with the difficulty increase attributed to longer intermediate-stage contexts diluting trigger influence (up to 7× longer than user prompts). For invocation attacks, Flip-Agent achieves 67.3%–100% ASR with 90%–98% CDA, again dominating baselines. Near-saturation is reached with ~40 flips while baselines fail to match even at 100 flips. An ablation study confirms both the attention-enhancement term and Prioritized-Search are critical: removing either drops ASR substantially (e.g., from 99.2% to 79.6% without attention enhancement, and to 18.5% without prioritized search, on AgentLM-7B). A defense experiment blocking the top-50/75/100 critical bits under oracle knowledge of the attack reduces ASR only marginally, remaining above 90% across all models. Strengths: - Clearly identifies a novel and practically relevant threat model—hardware-level attacks on deployed LLM agents—with well-formalized attack surfaces that generalize across pipeline stages and attack goals. - The unified optimization objective elegantly covers both output-steering and invocation-manipulation surfaces, avoiding separate ad hoc formulations. - Attention-enhancement and teacher-forcing extensions are well-motivated and empirically validated; the ablation cleanly isolates each component's contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The threat model assumes a white-box adversary with full knowledge of model weights, architecture, and execution workflow, plus the ability to precisely target and flip arbitrary bits in memory—a highly privileged position that may not reflect realistic deployment scenarios where cloud inference or encrypted weights reduce physical access. - Evaluation is limited to shopping and tool-use benchmarks; attack effectiveness on more complex, long-horizon agentic tasks (e.g., code agents, multi-agent systems) remains unexamined, and the paper explicitly defers multi-agent settings to future work. - The defense analysis is superficial: the proposed mitigation (blocking known critical bits) is evaluated only under an idealized oracle assumption and is shown to be ineffective, but no constructive path toward a viable defense is provided, leaving the paper as purely an attack contribution.
Citations 2603.10042 PDF
#38 — Innovative (68)

Disentangling Geometry, Performance, and Training in Language Models

Atharva Kulkarni, Jacob Mitchell Springer, Arjun Subramonian, Swabha Swayamdipta · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper delivers a methodologically rigorous falsification of the widely cited claim that unembedding matrix effective rank causally drives performance degradation in language models. The 108-model ablation study with partial Spearman and predictive delta-R-squared controls is unusually thorough, and the OLMo-14M vs. Pythia-14M comparison cleanly rebuts the saturation-causation hypothesis. The confinement to small models (≤75M parameters) leaves open whether these findings hold at frontier scale, but the corrective to over-interpreted geometric proxies is valuable.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: A growing body of interpretability research uses geometric properties of the Transformer unembedding matrix—particularly its effective rank—as proxies for model quality. Prior work on Pythia models claimed that low effective rank (concentrated singular value spectrum) causally degrades downstream performance and drives late-stage saturation in small language models. However, these findings rest almost exclusively on the Pythia family, and no prior study systematically varies training conditions to isolate whether low effective rank is a cause or merely a correlate of poor performance. This paper asks three questions: (RQ1) Does effective rank reliably predict performance across tasks and training setups? (RQ2) Why does it sometimes fail to predict performance? (RQ3) Do alternative geometric metrics—cosine similarity, isotropy—do better, and do these findings extend to final-layer representations? Methodology: The authors pre-train a controlled suite of 108 OLMo-style language models ranging from 4M to 75M non-embedding parameters on the Pile dataset (8B–128B tokens). They systematically ablate batch size over {32, 64, 128, 256, 512}, weight decay over {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}, and learning rate scale factors {0.1, 1, 10, 100}, while also varying learning rate annealing schedules (0%, 1%, 10% of peak LR). For each model, they compute three geometric metrics on the unembedding matrix W: effective rank R(W) (exponential of spectral entropy), cosine similarity A(W) (mean pairwise angular alignment of rows), and isotropy I(W) (partition-function-based uniformity). Analogous metrics are computed on the final-token last-layer representations H. Performance is evaluated across five settings: in-distribution loss (Pile-10K), out-of-distribution (Paloma/Dolma benchmark), fine-tuning (StarCoder-Python, OpenWebMath), post-training quantization (GPTQ), and catastrophic forgetting. Correlation is quantified via raw Spearman, Chinchilla-adjusted residual Spearman, partial Spearman (controlling all hyperparameters simultaneously), and predictive ΔR² (k-fold cross-validation). Key Results: Effective rank shows a strong raw Spearman correlation with in-distribution loss (−0.699 for R(W)), but this collapses to −0.261 as partial Spearman after controlling for hyperparameters. Predictive ΔR² is only 0.003—well under 0.5% additional variance explained beyond scaling laws alone. Crucially, batch size monotonically increases R(W) but shows a U-shaped relationship with performance, so many high-rank models are poor performers. Weight decay strongly raises R(W) yet has inconsistent performance effects across model sizes. Learning rate annealing (milder decay → higher LR at end) raises R(W) but degrades performance. In the saturation analysis, an OLMo-14M model trained identically to the saturating Pythia-14M shows no loss degradation or rank collapse under extended training, falsifying the hypothesis that low rank causes saturation. Alternative metrics offer marginal improvement: cosine similarity A(W) shows a non-monotone 'moon-shaped' correlation with rank, isotropy I(W) is tightly clustered around 0.5 and largely invariant to performance. Final-layer representation geometry (H) has higher rank but less variation than W and similarly fails to predict downstream performance. Across fine-tuning and catastrophic forgetting evaluations, no geometric metric consistently predicts outcomes; partial Spearman values remain near zero across all five evaluation regimes. Strengths: - Unusually rigorous experimental design: 108 models with systematic hyperparameter ablation and five distinct evaluation regimes provide much stronger causal evidence than previous single-family observational studies. - The use of partial Spearman and predictive ΔR² as stringent controls for confounding by model size and compute is methodologically sound and reveals that raw correlations are largely artifacts of these confounders. - The falsification of the saturation-causation hypothesis via the OLMo-14M vs. Pythia-14M comparison is a clean adversarial experiment that substantially narrows the scope of prior claims. - Practical takeaways are actionable: the paper correctly identifies effective rank as a symptom of hyperparameter choices rather than an independent diagnostic, redirecting practitioners toward standard performance metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study is confined to small models (4M–75M parameters), making it unclear whether the same decoupling between geometry and performance holds at the scale of modern LLMs (1B–70B+), where the mechanisms driving rank collapse may differ. - Only the unembedding matrix and last-token final-layer representations are analyzed; intermediate-layer geometry, which several concurrent works show exhibits distinct behavior, is explicitly left out of scope. - Because it is impossible to directly manipulate unembedding geometry while holding all other factors constant, the results remain observational. The paper calls for interventional studies but does not perform them, leaving causal claims about why geometry and performance decouple somewhat underspecified.
Recommended 2602.20433 PDF
#39 — Innovative (68)

XGenBoost: Synthesizing Small and Large Tabular Datasets with XGBoost

Jim Achterberg, Marcel Haas, Bram van Dijk, Marco Spruit · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: XGenBoost introduces a genuinely creative architectural paradigm by building generative models natively around XGBoost rather than adapting neural architectures, with the hierarchical classification approach for numerical features providing a principled ordinal inductive bias. The dual-architecture design (diffusion for small data, autoregressive for large) and the dramatic efficiency gains (minutes on CPU vs. GPU-hours for neural baselines) represent a meaningful departure from the deep-learning-dominated tabular generation landscape. Strong empirical results across 38 benchmark datasets reinforce the practical significance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Synthetic tabular data generation is critical for data augmentation, privacy-preserving data sharing, and federated learning. Current state-of-the-art generative models for mixed-type tabular data rely heavily on deep neural networks requiring GPUs, substantial hyperparameter tuning, and often massive training set extension (50-100× duplication) to function correctly. This makes them expensive and inaccessible in resource-constrained settings. The paper argues that tree ensembles like XGBoost—already dominant in discriminative tabular tasks due to better inductive biases, efficiency, and minimal tuning—should be the foundation for generative architectures instead of being adapted as an afterthought. Methodology: The authors present XGenBoost, two complementary generative architectures both built around XGBoost as the core learner. The first, XGenB-DF, is a Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) suited for smaller datasets. It uses separate XGBoost score-estimators per feature—one regressor per numerical feature and one classifier per categorical feature—trained on Gaussian and multinomial diffusion processes respectively, avoiding one-hot encoding and loss-scale weighting issues. A velocity-based reparameterization smooths the loss trajectory. The second, XGenB-AR, is a fixed-order hierarchical autoregressive model for large-scale synthesis. Numerical features are modeled using hierarchical XGBoost classifiers (meta-trees of binary classifiers providing ordinal inductive bias over quantile-based bins), with EQF-based de-quantization to handle non-continuous real-world distributions. Categorical features are modeled directly by multi-class XGBoost classifiers. High-cardinality categoricals are compressed via KMeans clustering on mean-vector embeddings before training. A feature-level dropout (p=0.1, masking with feature mean) reduces memorization in XGenB-DF. Crucially, neither model requires extending the training set at inference time, enabling true scalability to large datasets. Key Results: On the Small Benchmark (27 datasets from Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. 2024), XGenB-AR ranks 1st in Shape (marginal distribution fidelity) with average rank 2.278, and XGenB-DF variants achieve the best Trend (bivariate correlation) scores (rank ~4.0) and best MLE utility (rank ~3.0-3.5), outperforming all neural baselines including TabDDPM, CTGAN, TVAE, FD, FF, UT, and ARF across most metrics. On the Big Benchmark (11 larger datasets from Mueller et al. 2025), XGenB-AR achieves rank 1.000 in Shape, 1.364 in Trend, 1.818 in Detection and α-Precision, and 2.636 in MLE—outperforming SMOTE, ARF, CTGAN, TVAE, TabDDPM, and TabSyn on fidelity and utility at dramatically lower training cost. For example, XGenB-AR trains on the ACS Income dataset (1.66M rows) in ~3 minutes on 16 CPU cores with 64GB RAM, while GPU-based models require significantly more resources. DCR privacy scores for XGenB-AR are comparable to deep generative models, better than SMOTE and ARF. Strengths: - Principled data-first design: architectures respect tree learner constraints (single output, no mini-batching, native categorical splits) rather than force-fitting neural paradigms onto XGBoost - True scalability to large datasets without training set extension—XGenB-AR trains competitively in minutes where diffusion baselines are prohibitively slow or fail entirely - Hierarchical classification for numerical features imposes a valuable ordinal inductive bias, substantially improving β-Recall (diversity) and multivariate structure preservation over multi-class approaches - EQF-based de-quantization robustly handles skewed and non-continuous real-world distributions compared to uniform bin sampling Weaknesses / Limitations: - XGenB-DF cannot scale to large datasets due to the K-times training set duplication requirement inherent to DDPM score-matching; the paper omits it from the Big Benchmark - Neither model supports imputation natively (requires non-trivial extensions like Besag pseudo-likelihood or REPAINT-style inpainting) - Sequential inference in XGenB-AR over features raises scalability concerns for datasets with hundreds or thousands of features, where inference time may become comparable to or exceed that of diffusion models - The fixed permutation order for autoregressive factorization is heuristically chosen; attempts to use information-theoretic orderings showed no consistent improvement, leaving potential gains on the table
Citations 2603.06904 PDF
#40 — Innovative (68)

Turning Time Series into Algebraic Equations: Symbolic Machine Learning for Interpretable Modeling of Chaotic Time Series

Madhurima Panja, Grace Younes, Tanujit Chakraborty · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
