Why this rank: The first large-scale systematic benchmark of symbolic regression as a forecasting methodology (rather than equation discovery) is a meaningful contribution, and the result that SyTF statistically dominates all deep learning baselines on 132 chaotic attractors is genuinely surprising. The dual SyNF/SyTF framework covering both gradient-based and evolutionary symbolic approaches is well-designed. Limited by univariate one-step-ahead evaluation only.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern deep learning achieves strong short-horizon accuracy on chaotic time series but remains a black box, offering no mechanistic insight into the underlying dynamics. Classical mechanistic models are interpretable but require strong prior assumptions. This paper asks whether symbolic regression—which jointly optimizes both functional structure and parameters to produce explicit algebraic equations—can close the accuracy gap with deep learning while delivering interpretable forecasting models for chaotic systems. The gap being addressed is specifically the lack of systematic benchmarking of symbolic methods as forecasters (rather than as equation discovery tools) against modern deep learning and ensemble baselines. Methodology: The authors propose two complementary symbolic forecasting frameworks evaluated in a rolling-window, one-step-ahead (nowcasting) setting using lagged observations as inputs. The Symbolic Neural Forecaster (SyNF) adapts the Equation Learner (EQL) architecture to time series: a single hidden layer replaces standard activations with fixed symbolic units (identity, sine, cosine, pairwise multiplication), enabling end-to-end differentiable training via Adam with MSE loss. Regularized variants (SyNF-Reg) add ℓ₁ penalties on weight matrices to prune redundant symbolic terms. Extended variants (SyNF-Div, SyNF-Div-Reg) introduce learnable division units with a curriculum-relaxed stability penalty to model rational functional forms, stabilizing optimization against division-by-zero. The Symbolic Tree Forecaster (SyTF) builds on PySR's genetic programming engine: expression trees over lagged inputs evolve through tournament selection, mutation, crossover, and an evolve–simplify–optimize loop, with BFGS fine-tuning of constants. Complexity is controlled via an adaptive frecency-based penalty that dynamically reweights search pressure across expression complexity levels, and a Pareto front of accuracy vs. complexity is maintained. An extended variant (SyTF-Div-Exp) adds division and exponential operators. Both frameworks are evaluated on 132 low-dimensional synthetic chaotic attractors (from the 'dysts' benchmark, 1000 train/200 test, with 5/10/25 lags) and two real-world series (San Juan dengue incidence 1990–2013; El Niño 3.4 SST 1990–2021). Baselines span Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, LSTM, NBeats, NHiTS, TiDE, NLinear, and Transformer. Conformal prediction intervals are computed using a held-out calibration set. Key Results: On the 132 synthetic chaotic attractors, SyTF and SyTF-Div-Exp dominate across all metrics and lag settings. With 5 lagged inputs, SyTF achieves median RMSE 0.004, MAE 0.003, MARRE 0.034, and SMAPE 0.342—roughly 3–7× lower than the next best deep learning model (LSTM: RMSE 0.020, MAE 0.014) and an order of magnitude better than tree ensembles (XGBoost RMSE 0.046). SyNF and SyNF-Reg consistently outperform all deep learning baselines while being more variable than SyTF. The Transformer is worst across all settings. Multiple Comparison with the Best (MCB) non-parametric tests confirm SyTF's statistical superiority on both RMSE and SMAPE, with all other models falling outside the critical distance. On real-world data the roles shift: SyNF-Reg achieves best performance on San Juan Dengue (SMAPE 20.95, RMSE 20.69, MAE 15.76, MARRE 6.91), outperforming the next-best NHiTS (SMAPE 21.10). SyNF-Div-Reg achieves the lowest errors on El Niño SST across all four metrics (SMAPE 0.79, RMSE 0.26, MAE 0.20, MARRE 6.30), beating LightGBM (SMAPE 0.82) and NBeats (SMAPE 0.83). Discovered equations are human-readable: SyTF for El Niño SST produces ŷ_t = 0.985y_{t-1} + 0.417, while SyNF produces a richer expression with polynomial and trigonometric terms in lagged SST. Conformal prediction intervals from SyNF-Div-Reg adapt to volatility regimes, covering most test points at the 90% level. Strengths: - First large-scale benchmark (132 chaotic attractors + real-world series) systematically evaluating symbolic ML as a forecasting methodology rather than a scientific discovery tool, enabling rigorous comparison against a broad baseline suite. - SyTF delivers statistically significant accuracy improvements over all deep learning and ensemble baselines on synthetic chaotic systems while producing compact, interpretable equations—a rare combination of performance and transparency. - SyNF's neural-symbolic hybrid design allows gradient-based optimization, making it more robust to high-dimensional, noisy real-world data where pure evolutionary search (SyTF) struggles, with division and sparsity variants further capturing rational dynamics like oscillatory SST patterns. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is strictly one-step-ahead (nowcasting); multi-step forecasting stability is untested, and discovered equations may produce unstable iterative trajectories due to sensitivity amplification in chaotic systems. - Univariate-only: no exogenous covariates are incorporated, which matters practically for dengue (humidity, travel) and ENSO (wind anomalies), and omits a primary avenue through which mechanistic structure could be recovered. - SyTF's evolutionary search becomes computationally expensive and degrades on high-dimensional or noisy real-world inputs, limiting scalability, while SyNF's fixed symbolic architecture constrains the expressiveness of discoverable functional forms to the predefined operator set.
Search Results 2603.07261 PDF
#41 — Innovative (67)

Distilling LLM Semantic Priors into Encoder-Only Multi-Talker ASR with Talker-Count Routing

Hao Shi, Yusuke Fujita, Roman Koshkin, Mengjie Zhao, Yuan Gao, Lianbo Liu, Yui Sudo · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The core idea of distilling LLM semantic priors into an encoder-only architecture during training and discarding the LLM at inference is clean and well-executed, yielding a 26× inference speedup. The most compelling result is the dramatic improvement on three-talker mixtures (14.3% vs. 39.1% WER for the LLM baseline), directly addressing the key failure mode of decoder-centric approaches. However, the evaluation on synthetic LibriMix only and the binary talker-count routing limitation constrain the demonstrated generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-talker automatic speech recognition (MT-ASR) — transcribing all speakers from overlapping speech — has made progress through serialized output training (SOT) with large language model (LLM) decoders, but this approach has two compounding problems. First, autoregressive LLM decoding is slow (high real-time factor). Second, LLM decoders fail to reliably improve performance on challenging three-talker mixtures because the encoder remains talker-agnostic and becomes the bottleneck: the LLM must disentangle heavily overlapped representations over long sequences. Encoder-only CTC approaches are faster but unstable under heavy overlap without strong semantic regularization, and they require the number of talkers to be known in advance. The paper addresses the question of whether LLMs can provide semantic regularization during training without being present at inference. Methodology: The paper proposes a two-phase training framework built on WavLM-Large (24 Transformer blocks) as the backbone. A shared encoder (layers 1–12) feeds into two specialized Transformer branches — one for two-talker and one for three-talker conditions — each initialized from WavLM layers 13–24. Each branch is followed by an LSTM-based separator that produces talker-ordered streams, which are decoded via serialized CTC heads (one per talker in onset-time order). In Phase 1, a LLaMA-3.2-1B decoder is attached and jointly fine-tuned using the SOT cross-entropy objective while only LoRA adapters and token embeddings are updated, distilling semantic guidance into the encoder via backpropagation. In Phase 2, the adapted LLaMA is frozen and continues to provide an SOT teacher signal (weighted by α) while the separator and CTC heads are trained, yielding a hybrid CTC–attention objective. At inference, LLaMA is completely absent. To handle variable talker counts without a priori knowledge, a Talker-Count Head (TCH) is introduced: an attentive-statistics pooling module over shared-encoder features that predicts binary logits (two vs. three talkers) and dynamically routes to the appropriate branch. Key Results: On LibriMix (clean condition, evaluation set), the proposed encoder-only model (ID-8, with 6 TCH Transformer layers) achieves WER of 4.1% on Libri2Mix and 14.3% on Libri3Mix. For comparison, the LLaMA-1B SOT baseline achieves 4.6%/21.6%, and the best LLM-based system (SOP-LLaMA-3B) achieves 3.6%/16.5%. In the noisy condition, the encoder-only model reaches 9.7%/24.5% vs. 11.3%/39.1% for LLaMA-1B SOT — a dramatic improvement on 3-talker. Real-time factor is 0.0043 vs. 0.1150 for the LLM baseline on Libri2Mix (26× faster). Training without LLM distillation (ID-5) completely fails, with WER exceeding 66–81% across conditions, confirming that semantic regularization is essential for CTC convergence under heavy overlap. Talker count accuracy reaches 97–100% for two-talker conditions but degrades to 88–94% for three-talker in the best TCH configuration (12 layers). Strengths: - The train-time distillation idea is well-motivated and cleanly executed: LLM cost is amortized into training only, yielding a 26× inference speedup with minimal accuracy loss on 2-talker and actual accuracy gains on 3-talker relative to the LLM-based baseline. - The ablation study is thorough — testing CTC-only training, varying encoder depths, and varying TCH depths — providing clear evidence that LLM distillation is the critical enabling component, not merely depth or architecture. - The three-talker scenario, where prior LLM-decoder approaches degrade badly (39.1% WER), is handled significantly better (14.3% WER), addressing the key failure mode of decoder-centric methods. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to a single, synthetic benchmark (LibriMix), which is constructed from clean audiobook recordings. Generalization to real conversational scenarios with natural reverberation, acoustic variety, and more than three talkers is not demonstrated. - The Talker-Count Head accuracy for three-talker mixtures remains substantially lower than for two-talker (88–94% vs. 97–100%), and TCH errors directly propagate to recognition errors — the gap between oracle talker-count (ID-6, WER 12.9%) and predicted count (ID-8, WER 14.3%) represents headroom that the paper acknowledges but does not close. - The framework is constrained to two discrete branches (2-talker and 3-talker) and does not generalize gracefully to arbitrary or unknown talker counts; extending beyond this binary routing is left as future work without a concrete design.
Search Results 2603.10587 PDF
#42 — Innovative (67)

A large-scale database for clinical trial outcomes and features

nature.com · Jimeng Sun - new articles
Why this rank: Scores 67 for the novel weak supervision pipeline that aggregates LLM predictions, financial market signals, news sentiment, and cross-phase trial linkage to produce near-human-quality labels (F1=0.914) at unprecedented scale (125K labeled trials). The cross-phase trial linkage algorithm enabling a new class of supervision signal is a creative contribution. The scale and public release under MIT license fill a genuine data bottleneck. However, the baseline models evaluated are dated (no modern LLMs or clinical foundation models), and the systematic underperformance in Phase 1 reveals a structural limitation of the labeling approach where weak signals are sparse.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Predicting clinical trial outcomes is of enormous practical value—drug development costs exceed $1B per approved compound and trial failure rates are high. Existing datasets for outcome prediction are either small, restricted to specific phases or drug types, or gated behind industry sponsors (e.g., TrialTrove: 19,136 trials; Biomedtracker: 6,417; TOP: 17,538—all non-public or proprietary). This paper addresses the bottleneck of labeled data by introducing CTO (Clinical Trial Outcomes), a large-scale, publicly available dataset with automated outcome labels for 125,840 trials and predicted labels for 479,761 trials in total, covering all clinical phases. Methodology: CTO generates outcome labels via a weakly supervised data programming framework (Ratner et al., AAAI 2019) that aggregates five heterogeneous labeling functions: (1) LLM predictions—GPT-3.5-turbo is prompted on the top-2 PubMed abstracts (selected by title similarity) to predict trial success/failure and extract statistical features and QA pairs; (2) trial linkage—a novel algorithm connects trials across phases using PubMedBERT embeddings for top-K retrieval and a cross-encoder (pretrained on MS-MARCO) for re-ranking, then labels earlier-phase trials as successes if linked to later-phase trials or FDA approvals (matched via the FDA Orange Book); (3) news headline sentiment—Twitter-roBERTa-base classifies Google News headlines as positive/negative for ~3,688 trials; (4) stock price signals—5-day moving average slope of sponsor stock prices around trial completion; (5) 12 trial metrics from CTTI (patient counts, dropout rates, adverse events, P-values, amendments, etc.) thresholded against medians as weak labeling functions. These sources are aggregated via a covariance-based matrix completion approach that estimates the accuracy of each labeling function without ground-truth labels. A manually curated gold set of 11,012 trials (CTO labels refined by human experts) provides a benchmark. Baseline predictors use TFIDF features over trial text fields (phase, disease, intervention, eligibility criteria) for classical models (RF, LR, XGBoost, SVM, MLP) and PubMedBERT/BioBERT embeddings for neural baselines. Key Results: The CTO automated labels achieve F1=0.914 and Cohen's Kappa=0.873 agreement with human annotations. Ablation of labeling functions shows GPT predictions are by far the most critical component (removing them drops F1 by 0.071—an order of magnitude larger than model uncertainty), followed by news headlines (−0.015); stock prices and trial linkage contribute modest but detectable gains. On the manual benchmark (train on pre-2022 trials, test on post-2022): Phase 1 is easiest (RF F1=0.790, LR PRAUC=0.766), Phase 2 is harder (XGBoost F1=0.696), and Phase 3 is hardest (LR F1=0.601, ROCAUC=0.615). Classical models consistently outperform BioBERT and PubMedBERT, likely due to distribution shift and overfitting in large pretrained models. CTO-trained baselines outperform models trained on the prior TOP dataset across all phases and sponsor categories, matching human-labeled model performance in Phases 2 and 3 (though underperforming in Phase 1 where weak signals are sparse). The trial linkage algorithm achieves best F1=0.684 using the feature combination {title, intervention, brief summary, eligibility, condition}; including lead sponsor degrades performance due to frequent sponsor changes across phases. Strengths: - Scale and openness: at 125,840 labeled trials (479,761 with predicted labels), CTO is an order of magnitude larger than any prior publicly available dataset, and is released under MIT license with reproducible code. - Novel automated labeling pipeline: weakly supervised aggregation of diverse public signals (LLMs, financial markets, news, trial registries) eliminates dependence on proprietary industry data while achieving near-human label quality (F1=0.914). - First systematic cross-phase trial linkage algorithm, enabling a new class of weak supervision signals and linking earlier trial phases to downstream FDA approvals. Weaknesses / Limitations: - CTO-trained models underperform human-labeled models in Phase 1, where weak supervision signals (news, stock prices, trial linkage) are sparse, indicating a systematic limitation of the labeling approach for early-phase trials. - Baseline models are limited to classical ML and pre-2023 biomedical BERT variants; no GPT-4, clinical LLMs, or recent foundation models are evaluated, leaving the dataset's ceiling performance unknown. - The trial linkage algorithm has imperfect precision (F1≈0.68), introducing label noise; the noise propagation through the weak label aggregation is acknowledged but not fully characterized in terms of downstream prediction impact.
Recommended
#43 — Innovative (67)

Super Research: Answering Highly Complex Questions with Large Language Models through Super Deep and Super Wide Research

Yubo Dong, Nianhao You, Yuxuan Hou, Zixun Sun, Yue Zhang, Liang Zhang, Siyuan Zhao, Hehe Fan · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely novel graph-anchored evaluation methodology for complex research agents that demonstrably outperforms LLM-as-a-Judge on sensitivity and consistency. The five-dimensional metric suite capturing orthogonal failure modes and the rigorous benchmark construction process represent meaningful methodological contributions. However, the benchmark's high construction cost limits community adoption, and the absolute score ceiling (best: 28.62) leaves ambiguity about whether results reflect true capability gaps or over-constrained evaluation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing LLM research agents excel at either Deep Research (vertical, iterative evidence chains) or Wide Search (horizontal, broad data acquisition) but fail to combine both at the scale demanded by truly complex professional questions — ones requiring multi-hop reasoning over conflicting evidence, long-horizon planning, and synthesis across 1,000+ web pages. Current evaluation benchmarks exacerbate the problem: LLM-as-a-Judge scoring is inaccurate and biased, shallow fact-recall metrics ignore reasoning depth, and human evaluation is prohibitively expensive. The gap between what top agents can deliver and what professional intelligence or scientific discovery requires remains unquantified and largely unaddressed. Methodology: The paper introduces Super Research, a paradigm integrating Super Wide Retrieval (broad, multi-perspective horizontal coverage) with Super Deep Investigation (iterative follow-up queries to resolve uncertainty and verify individual claims). To operationalize this, the authors construct SuperResearch Bench: 300 expert-vetted, open-ended questions across 10 professional domains (Science & Engineering, Finance & Business, Medical & Health, etc.), each designed to require up to 100+ retrieval steps and 1,000+ web page syntheses. Tasks are generated via a Cognitive-Rank Constrained Expert Simulation protocol using frontier LLMs prompted as senior investigators, then filtered by Ph.D. candidates to eliminate hallucinations and trivially answerable questions. Ground-truth construction follows a four-phase pipeline: (1) Hierarchical task decomposition via a Planner agent that builds a MECE DAG of research phases and chapters; (2) Research Graph Construction, a human-AI collaborative process that extracts atomic Fact nodes (anchored to URLs) and derives higher-order Insight nodes, forming a verifiable knowledge topology; (3) Report Synthesis by a Writer agent informed by the graph; and (4) Automated construction of QA pairs and bias calibration queries. Evaluation uses five metrics: Coverage & Comprehension (R_weighted, a depth-weighted graph recall), Logical Consistency (C_logic, product of valid inference ratio and graph coverage), Report Utility (U_qa, closed-context QA accuracy), Objectivity Score (O_bias, calibration error between thesis/antithesis evidential support), and Citation Health (Source Dominance D_src, Narrative Monopolization M_mono). The graph-based metrics are validated against an LLM-Judge baseline via controlled perturbation (fact removal/injection) experiments. Key Results: Evaluating 12 systems across three paradigms (Deep Research Systems, Native Search-Integrated Agents, Search-Augmented Baselines), the benchmark reveals that no current system adequately handles super-complex queries. The best-performing system, Gemini Deep Research, achieves an overall score of only 28.62/100 — confirming super-complexity is an unsolved frontier. Sonar Deep Research (27.04) and Grok-4-1-fast (24.55) round out the upper tier, while search-augmented baselines (Claude-3.5-sonnet: 22.26, DeepSeek-r1: 22.57) trail. Logical Consistency scores are uniformly low (14–25), identifying multi-hop synthesis as the universal bottleneck. OpenAI o3/o4-mini exhibit a "Defensive Summarization Paradox": high objectivity (O_bias > 30) but low utility (U_qa ~14), indicating safe generalization at the cost of expert-level specificity. Graph-based metrics show dramatically better sensitivity to quality perturbations (57.4%–79.6% responsiveness rate) versus LLM-Judge (14.8%–22.2%), and lower cross-model variance (e.g., 1.07% vs. 7.82% normalized STD for Objectivity). Strengths: - The graph-anchored evaluation protocol is a genuine methodological advance: grounding scoring in a verifiable, expert-curated Research Graph sidesteps the well-known unreliability of LLM-as-a-Judge for complex tasks, and the ablation rigorously demonstrates the superiority of graph metrics over LLM judges on both sensitivity and consistency dimensions. - The benchmark's construction process is unusually rigorous — combining frontier LLM generation with Ph.D.-level expert filtering and human-in-the-loop graph construction — yielding tasks that are genuinely resistant to surface-level retrieval and keyword matching. - The five-dimensional metric suite captures orthogonal failure modes (coverage vs. logical grounding vs. actionability vs. neutrality vs. citation diversity) that existing single-score benchmarks miss, enabling principled diagnosis of model weaknesses such as the Defensive Summarization Paradox and Single-Source Regression. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The absolute scores reported (best: 28.62) make it difficult to distinguish whether the benchmark ceiling reflects a genuine capability gap or an inherently over-constrained evaluation standard; the Research Graph ground truth is itself constructed by LLM agents with human oversight, introducing potential systematic bias in what counts as a valid Atomic Fact or Insight node. - The benchmark and evaluation infrastructure carry high construction cost (100+ retrieval steps, human expert annotation per task) and are not designed for continuous or live evaluation — unlike leaderboard-oriented benchmarks, this limits broad community adoption and replication. - Cross-domain performance analysis (Figure 8) reveals that Gemini Deep Research achieves its strongest scores in Finance & Business and Environment & Energy, but the paper does not explain why domain-specific performance varies so dramatically across model families, leaving open whether the benchmark's domain distribution reflects realistic professional research demand or is skewed toward analytically-structured domains where graph construction is easier.
Recommended 2603.00582 PDF
#44 — Innovative (66)

UltRAG: a Universal Simple Scalable Recipe for Knowledge Graph RAG

graphcore-research.github.io · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: UltRAG's core insight — that neural query executors are fundamentally more robust than symbolic ones for KG-RAG due to tolerance of both LLM noise and KG incompleteness — is well-motivated and empirically validated with consistent double-digit gains. The framework's zero-shot, training-free, inductive design and demonstrated scalability to full Wikidata (116M entities) on a single GPU represent meaningful engineering novelty. However, the approach is ultimately an effective composition of existing components (FAISS, GNNs, LLMs, PageRank) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models frequently hallucinate — generating confident but factually incorrect content. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over document corpora is well-established, but adapting it to Knowledge Graphs (KGs) is non-trivial. KGs such as Wikidata (116M entities, 1.6B relations) store facts as subject-predicate-object triples, and answering multi-hop questions requires traversing multiple entities and relations simultaneously. Existing KG-RAG approaches fall into four categories — KG agents, path-based, GNN-based, and query-based — but each has critical weaknesses: they often fail on complex multi-hop logical queries, require retraining per dataset, or cannot scale to web-scale KGs. This paper fills the gap by introducing ULTRAG, a general, training-free framework that couples LLMs with neural (GNN-based) query executors for KGQA. Methodology: ULTRAG is built on two key insights: (1) a successful query executor must be robust to both LLM noise (imperfect query generation) and KG noise (missing relations), which requires it to be neural rather than symbolic; and (2) LLMs themselves are poor neural executors, underperforming on graph algorithms like Bellman-Ford by orders of magnitude compared to lightweight GNNs (roughly 10^6× more FLOPs per inference pass). The framework (Algorithm 1) iterates: the LLM generates a structured query φ in a custom domain-specific language (DSL), an entity linker maps query leaves to KG entities via fuzzy-set similarity using FAISS approximate nearest neighbor search over E5-large embeddings, a neural query executor (ULTRAQUERY — a GNN foundation model for logical query reasoning) executes the query over the KG returning fuzzy membership probabilities for all entities, and finally an LLM arbitrator ranks the top-k candidates to produce the final answer. The framework is universal: it does not fix the LLM, entity linker, decider, or executor type. The off-the-shelf instantiation (ULTRAG-OTS) uses GPT-5 for query generation and arbitration, ULTRAQUERY as the neural executor, FAISS+IVFPQ for scalable entity lookup, and Seed Entity Personalized PageRank (SEPPR) to extract a relevant top-30,000 node subgraph from the full KG. A simplified custom DSL (infix notation with `->` for projections and n-ary AND for intersections) reduces LLM-generated invalid queries from 15–30% to under 1%. Key Results: On GTSQA (WikiKG2 and Wikidata subgraphs), ULTRAG-OTS achieves 92.66% exact match hits, 91.05% recall, and 89.29% F1 — outperforming the next-best baseline SubgraphRAG(200) by +8.32%, +9.39%, and +8.03% respectively. On KGQAGen-10K (Wikidata), it achieves 92.04% hits, 90.77% recall, and 88.82% F1, again outperforming SubgraphRAG(200) by +2.28%, +5.25%, and +2.54%. Crucially, ULTRAG-OTS requires no retraining on the target datasets (zero-shot/inductive), while most baselines are transductive and fine-tuned per dataset. The neural executor (ULTRAQUERY) outperforms an equivalent symbolic executor by a class-average of 18.58% MRR and 24.09% Hit@10 on WikiKG2 subgraphs, validating the core design thesis. In terms of efficiency, ULTRAG-OTS is 19×/99×/167× faster than RoG/GNN-RAG/SubgraphRAG in non-API wall-clock time per query. While it processes 25–62× more input tokens than baselines (due to relation type context), 94–96% of these tokens are cached by GPT-5, keeping API costs only 23–27% higher. Using GPT-5-mini for arbitration reduces cost to $0.009/query with only ~4% performance drop on Hits/F1. Strengths: - Technically rigorous motivation for using neural over symbolic query executors, validated empirically across all query classes and hop counts with consistent double-digit gains. - Truly inductive and zero-shot: no retraining of any component is required for new datasets or KGs, unlike all competitive baselines which require dataset-specific fine-tuning. - Demonstrated scalability to Wikidata (116M entities, 1.6B relations) on a single GH200 96GB GPU via SEPPR subgraph extraction and FAISS, which no prior KG-RAG framework has achieved. Weaknesses / Limitations: - ULTRAG-OTS does not natively support temporal queries (e.g., "Who was president in 2010?"), limiting applicability to temporally-grounded knowledge bases without extending to Temporal KG frameworks. - The framework inherits ULTRAQUERY's known suboptimality on knowledge hypergraphs, as ULTRA's link prediction was not designed for hyperedges, restricting use cases to standard binary-relation KGs. - The evaluation deliberately excludes popular benchmarks (WebQSP, CWQ) citing low ground-truth correctness rates (~52% and ~49%), which may limit comparability to a broad body of prior KGQA literature that uses those datasets.
Citations
#45 — Innovative (65)

Novel Table Search [Technical Report]

Besat Kassaie, Renée J. Miller · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper earns credit for formally defining Novel Table Search as a distinct problem — the axiomatic framework, NP-hardness proof, and nscore formalization provide theoretical grounding that prior table search work entirely lacked. The ANTs algorithm achieves a favorable efficiency-effectiveness trade-off. However, the contribution is fundamentally a re-ranking layer atop existing systems, and the significant performance drop with automatic alignments tempers the practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Data lake search systems retrieve unionable tables—tables that can be vertically unioned with a query table to add new rows—but existing methods optimize purely for unionability (semantic similarity) and ignore novelty. In practice, a result set dominated by tables with the same values as the query table is useless for data augmentation, as it contributes no new information. This problem is especially acute in data markets where each acquired table carries a cost, making redundant results wasteful. The paper formally defines Novel Table Search (NTS) as a re-ranking problem: given a query table Q and k candidate unionable tables, select the l most novel ones. No prior work has formalized or directly addressed syntactic novelty in the table search setting. Methodology: The authors first establish an axiomatic framework identifying two properties any novelty scoring function must satisfy: the Blatant Duplicate Axiom (including an exact copy of Q in results must lower the score) and the Dilution Axiom (including a diluted version of a candidate table must lower the score). They define a concrete syntactic scoring function, nscore, based on a left-outer-union construction that measures tuple-level novelty via value distinctness, accounting for null values probabilistically. They prove that optimizing nscore exactly is NP-hard (reduction to the induced subgraph problem). To tractably approximate nscore, they propose ANTs (Attribute-Based Novel Table Search), which operates at the attribute level rather than the tuple level. ANTs computes an Attribute Novelty score for each aligned attribute pair combining syntactic dissimilarity—Jensen-Shannon divergence (JSD) for small-domain attributes, Jaccard similarity for large-domain attributes—with semantic similarity via Starmie embeddings: AttNovelty(Bi, Aj) = (1 − syn_sim)^b × sem_sim. The table-level novelty is the sum over aligned attribute pairs. ANTs exhaustively scores all k candidate tables and returns the top-l by novelty. Three baselines are also proposed: GMC (Greedy Marginal Contribution, adapting a diversification heuristic with a ½-approximation guarantee), ER (entity-resolution-based overlap estimation using ScaNN blocking + Levenshtein matching), and SemNov (semantic dissimilarity via TABBIE table embeddings). Key Results: On three benchmarks (Santos, TUS, Ugen-v2), ANTs achieves 0% blatant duplicate rate on Santos and TUS, and 43.9% on Ugen-v2 (versus 100% for Starmie, 81.9–98.8% for GMC), demonstrating that novelty-aware reranking reliably eliminates redundant results. On the Syntactic Novelty Measure (SNM), ANTs consistently dominates across all datasets and result-set sizes l ∈ [2, 10]. On the direct nscore metric (computed on the smaller Ugen-v2 dataset), ANTs achieves the highest average novelty score of 0.390 at l=2 and 0.247 at l=3, significantly outperforming Starmie and GMC (p ≤ 0.05), with SemNov as the runner-up. Crucially, ANTs runs in under 2.4 seconds per query on TUS and Santos, while GMC and ER incur hundreds of seconds—making GMC and ER impractical for interactive use cases. On a downstream IMDB rating prediction task using LGBM regression, ANTs reranking of Starmie results consistently and significantly outperforms both the no-augmentation baseline and Starmie alone on diluted datasets (R² gain of up to +4.89% over Starmie, p ≤ 0.05 for most k). On non-diluted datasets, ANTs is not detrimental—it ties or matches Starmie, making it safe to apply regardless of whether redundancy is known to be present. Strengths: - Rigorous formalization: the problem, axioms, scoring function, and NP-hardness proof provide a solid theoretical foundation that prior table search work lacks. - ANTs achieves near-instant runtime (<2.4s) while matching or exceeding the novelty quality of the provably ½-approximate GMC, which is orders of magnitude slower—a favorable efficiency-effectiveness trade-off for real data lake systems. - The evaluation is thorough: three datasets of different origin (hand-curated, open data, LLM-generated), multiple metrics (blatant-duplicate, SSNM, SNM, nscore, downstream ML), ablation studies on alignment quality, hyperparameters, and initial search redundancy levels. Weaknesses / Limitations: - NTS is a re-ranking step, not an end-to-end system; its effectiveness is bounded by the quality and novelty of the initial unionable table search results. When initial results are highly redundant (>40–70% diluted tables), ANTs' performance degrades substantially, which is acknowledged but not fully addressed. - Most experiments use ground-truth attribute alignments. With automatic alignment (RoBERTa-based k-means clustering), performance drops in 92% of tested configurations for SSNM and 89% for SNM—a significant practical concern since ground-truth alignments are unavailable in real data lakes. - The benchmarks (Santos, TUS, Ugen-v2) were not designed for NTS; the paper acknowledges they may not fully capture the NTS problem's characteristics, and commits to developing dedicated benchmarks in future work.
Citations 2603.07235 PDF
#46 — Innovative (65)

Impermanent: A Live Benchmark for Temporal Generalization in Time Series Forecasting

Azul Garza, Renée Rosillo, Rodrigo Mendoza-Smith, David Salinas, Andrew Robert Williams, Arjun Ashok, Mononito Goswami, José Martín Juárez · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The prequential, leak-proof live benchmark design directly addresses the critical and timely problem of data contamination and static-split inflation in TSFM evaluation. Using GitHub activity as a genuinely non-stationary live data source is clever. The contribution is important but primarily infrastructural rather than methodological, and the single-domain scope and very short evaluation window limit current evidentiary value.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Time-series foundation models (TSFMs) claim broad temporal generalization, but existing evaluation protocols rely on static train-test splits that fail to test whether performance persists over time in evolving, non-stationary environments. Static benchmarks like GIFT-Eval, FEV, and the Monash Forecasting Repository are frozen in time, making them susceptible to data contamination (models inadvertently trained on test data), memorization, and inflated performance through test-set-aware model selection. Real-world forecasting involves distributional shift, structural breaks, and emergent series — none of which static benchmarks can measure. This gap is particularly acute as TSFMs scale and their training data curation becomes less transparent. Methodology: The paper introduces Impermanent, a live benchmark built on GitHub open-source activity data sourced from GH Archive event streams. It tracks four event types (issues opened, pull requests opened, push events, new stargazers) across 400 top repositories by star count, constructing univariate time series at four forecast frequencies (hourly h=24, daily h=7, weekly h=4, monthly h=1). The evaluation protocol is prequential: at each cutoff date, models produce point and probabilistic forecasts before ground truth exists; scores are computed only after outcomes arrive. Metrics are MASE for point accuracy and scaled CRPS (estimated from nine quantile levels) for distributional calibration, both scaled by a zero-model baseline to enable cross-subdataset comparison. The infrastructure runs on serverless Modal pipelines with S3 storage, dispatching up to 125 parallel containers — CPU for statistical models, A10G GPUs for foundation models. Eleven models are evaluated: three naive baselines, five statistical methods (AutoARIMA, AutoETS, AutoCES, DynOptTheta, Prophet), and four TSFMs (Chronos-2, Moirai 2.0-R-Small, TimesFM 2.5, TiRex). Key Results: In an early snapshot through February 12, 2026, foundation models occupy the top four positions. TimesFM leads with median MASE of 0.609 and scaled CRPS of 1.055, and mean CRPS rank of 2.041 — substantially better than the next-best TSFM (Moirai at 2.153 CRPS). TiRex achieves the best MASE mean rank (2.938) among TSFMs. Statistical methods trail considerably, with the best (DynOptTheta) achieving median MASE of 1.522 vs. 0.609 for TimesFM. A notable nuance: SeasonalNaïve achieves competitive MASE rank (5.39) but poor probabilistic calibration (CRPS rank 9.50), while AutoETS/AutoARIMA show CRPS ranks comparable to DynOptTheta despite weaker point accuracy. The authors explicitly frame these results as an early snapshot, not a definitive ranking, since the benchmark is designed to track how rankings evolve under continued distributional shift. Strengths: - The prequential, leak-proof evaluation design is principled and directly addresses the contamination and static-split problems that plague existing TSFM benchmarks — forecasts must be issued before ground truth exists, making cherry-picking impossible. - GitHub activity is a genuinely live, non-stationary data source exhibiting intermittency, burstiness, level shifts, and heterogeneous spectral properties across event types — a harder and more realistic test than standard forecasting archives. - The infrastructure is fully automated and idempotent, allowing new models to be added without reprocessing history, and the open leaderboard enables reproducible community participation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is restricted to a single domain (GitHub software activity), which limits ecological validity for the broad cross-domain generalization claims TSFMs make; expansion to other live data streams (energy, finance, weather) is deferred to future work. - All series are univariate and repository-level; cross-repository co-movement and auxiliary covariates (e.g., release announcements, external events) that could enrich forecasting tasks are explicitly excluded from this iteration, potentially underrepresenting the complexity that TSFMs are designed to handle. - The evaluation window is very short (results snapshot through Feb 12, 2026), making it premature to draw strong conclusions about sustained performance or ranking stability — the paper's own framing acknowledges this, but the limited temporal depth reduces the evidentiary value of the current leaderboard.
Search Results 2603.08707 PDF
#47 — Innovative (65)

FlexServe: A Fast and Secure LLM Serving System for Mobile Devices with Flexible Resource Isolation

Yinpeng Wu, Yitong Chen, Lixiang Wang, Jinyu Gu, Zhichao Hua, Yubin Xia · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: FlexServe introduces genuinely creative systems mechanisms — page-granular secure memory via S2PT manipulation and time-multiplexed NPU isolation — that elegantly circumvent fundamental TrustZone limitations that prior work simply accepted. The multi-model pipelined scheduler is well-motivated by real agent workflows. However, the core contribution is engineering innovation within an existing security paradigm rather than a new approach to mobile LLM security, and the evaluation on a weak-NPU platform inflates the reported gains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying LLMs on mobile devices introduces severe security risks: a compromised OS kernel can steal model weights (worth millions to train) and sensitive user data processed during inference. ARM TrustZone is the standard hardware isolation mechanism for mobile TEEs, but its resource isolation is fundamentally inflexible — secure memory must be physically contiguous (slow to allocate, up to 6.44s for 8GB on a Llama3.1-8B setup), and the NPU cannot be switched to the secure world, forcing CPU-only secure inference that is ~15× slower. Existing TruzstZone-based LLM inference solutions inherit these bottlenecks and fail to handle multi-model agent workflows common in mobile AI applications. Methodology: FlexServe introduces a two-layer architecture: a Flex-Monitor (running at EL2/EL3 hypervisor and monitor modes) and a FlexServe Framework (a TrustZone secure-world Trusted Application). The core innovation is Flexible Resource Isolation, comprising two mechanisms. First, Flexible Secure Memory (Flex-Mem) uses ARM's Stage-2 Page Table (S2PT) to protect arbitrary, non-contiguous physical memory pages — any normal-world page can be dynamically promoted to secure and reclaimed back. A Flex-Mem Daemon process in the normal world manages allocation/reclamation, with Lazy Reclaim deferring page zeroing until actual reuse. DMA attacks are blocked by removing Flex-Mem pages from SMMU mappings. Second, Flexible Secure NPU (Flex-NPU) uses time-multiplexing: the NPU switches between normal and secure modes by remapping its MMIO region in the S2PT, and an isolated Flex-NPU sandbox hosts the normal-world NPU driver to prevent kernel tampering. An On-demand Protection mechanism disables S2PT overhead entirely when no secure inference is active, with EL3 integrity checks on the EL2 Flex-Monitor code. Building on these primitives, the FlexServe Framework implements: (1) a pipelined Secure Inference Pipeline that overlaps memory allocation, model loading, decryption, and forward computation layer-by-layer to hide cold-start latency; (2) LLM-Aware Memory Management with layer-granular model weight caching, paged KV cache (analogous to PagedAttention), and LRU-based eviction under memory pressure; and (3) a Multi-Model Scheduler that prefetches the next model during the decode stage of the current model, reclaims tail layers of model A while loading model B, and uses a zero-stall cache line to ensure prefill can proceed without I/O stalls. The prototype runs on a Rockchip RK3588 SoC (8-core CPU, 6 TOPS NPU, 16GB RAM) using Linux Kernel 6.1.57 and OP-TEE 4.5.0, supporting Llama3 and Qwen3 series models (1.7B–8B). Key Results: Against a CPU-only TrustZone strawman (CMA-based secure memory, no NPU), FlexServe achieves an average 10.05× TTFT speedup (up to 14.44×). Against Strawman-OPT (pipelined + secure NPU but still CMA memory), FlexServe achieves 1.43–2.42× TTFT speedup (1.85× average), with gains attributed primarily to avoiding CMA's slow contiguous allocation. Flex-Mem allocation is 11.33× faster than CMA; reclamation is 12.6× faster due to Lazy Reclaim. Flex-NPU mode switching costs only 0.21ms. For multi-model benchmarks (4GB cache, real-world datasets), FlexServe achieves 8.84× TTFT speedup over Strawman and 2.53× over Strawman-OPT. In agent workflow scenarios (10 representative workflows), end-to-end response latency improves up to 24.30× over Strawman and 4.05× over Strawman-OPT. Compared to an insecure normal-world baseline (NW-Base), FlexServe incurs only 4.41% TTFT overhead on average. Virtualization overhead (SPEC CPU) is 2.46%, fully eliminated by On-demand Protection when no inference is running. SQLite latency overhead is 1.31× vs. Strawman's 3.27×, due to avoiding TLB thrashing from GB-level CMA page migrations. Strengths: - The page-granular Flex-Mem design is a clean, principled solution to TrustZone's contiguous memory limitation, enabling fine-grained memory management optimizations (paged KV cache, layer-granular eviction, lazy reclaim) that are impossible with CMA-based approaches. - The On-demand Protection mechanism is practically important: it eliminates virtualization overhead entirely during idle periods, making the security substrate nearly transparent to normal-world applications (only 2.46% CPU overhead, fully amortized). - The multi-model scheduler with pipelined prefetching is well-motivated by real mobile workloads (agent workflows invoking 2–5 models sequentially) and delivers substantial end-to-end gains beyond single-model optimization. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation platform (RK3588) uses a relatively slow NPU (6 TOPS) that is not SOTA for mobile inference; the paper acknowledges this limits the compute stage, keeping memory I/O as the bottleneck — meaning reported speedups are partially inflated by a weak baseline NPU and may not generalize to faster NPU hardware where memory savings matter less. - The system cannot protect normal-world client inputs/outputs from a compromised kernel — a fundamental TrustZone limitation acknowledged but not addressed. For conversational LLM use cases where the prompt and response are the primary sensitive data, this is a significant gap that limits the practical security guarantee to model weight confidentiality only. - The prototype only supports Llama3 and Qwen3 model families, and the evaluation uses INT8 quantization throughout; the interaction of Flex-Mem's layer-granular management with architectures like Mixture-of-Experts (sparse activation patterns, irregular layer access) is left as future work without analysis.
Search Results 2603.09046 PDF
#48 — Innovative (65)

KGHaluBench: A Knowledge Graph-Based Hallucination Benchmark for Evaluating the Breadth and Depth of LLM Knowledge

Alex Robertson, Huizhi Liang, Mahbub Gani, Rohit Kumar, Srijith Rajamohan · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: KGHaluBench introduces a genuinely novel decomposition of hallucination into breadth-of-knowledge (entity recognition) and depth-of-knowledge (factual detail) failures, providing mechanistic insight that aggregate metrics obscure. The difficulty-weighted accuracy metric with entity-popularity correction is well-motivated and empirically validated. However, the core components (Wikidata querying, NLI entailment, embedding similarity) are established techniques assembled rather than invented, and the 79% entity-level human agreement raises reliability concerns for a benchmark intended to produce definitive model rankings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate — generating fluent but factually incorrect content — yet existing hallucination benchmarks are inadequate for diagnosing the underlying causes. Static QA datasets become outdated and fail to challenge increasingly capable models; multiple-choice and simple fact-seeking formats probe only surface-level recall; and one-dimensional aggregate metrics (accuracy, BLEU, BERTScore) obscure whether failures stem from lack of broad entity recognition or from insufficient depth of specific factual knowledge. KG-based benchmarks that dynamically generate questions exist but suffer from entity-popularity bias, favoring well-known entities that are overrepresented in training data. KGHaluBench addresses these gaps by leveraging a live knowledge graph to construct challenging, multifaceted questions while statistically correcting for both question difficulty and entity popularity. Methodology: KGHaluBench consists of two modules. The Question Generation Module queries Wikidata (118M+ entities as of August 2025) to randomly sample focal entities across a stratified distribution of entity types (human, film, business, painting, etc.), retrieves three KG triples and an entity description per entity, and prompts the evaluated LLM to produce a ~200-word open-ended overview incorporating those specific relational facts. Entity popularity is estimated from six statistics (page views, site links, linked entities, IDs, token count, statements/references) with ML-derived weights, and combined with per-relation complexity scores via a sigmoid-based formula (inspired by Item Response Theory) to produce a unified question difficulty score Q_d. Accuracy is then rescaled as weighted accuracy W_a = Accuracy × (Q_d / Avg(Q_d)) to neutralize entity-popularity bias across benchmark runs. The Response Verification Module applies a two-stage pipeline. An entity-level filter first detects abstentions via a small LLM, then computes a 70/30-weighted combination of cosine embedding similarity (Jina-Embedding-V3, 1024-dim) and Fuzzy Set Ratio (Levenshtein-based) against the entity's Wikipedia description; responses below threshold are classified as breadth-of-knowledge hallucinations (Halu_BOK). Responses passing entity-level screening proceed to a tri-stage fact-level check: (1) RoBERTa-Large-MNLI for NLI entailment (resolves 27.58% of facts in 0.36s average); (2) Llama-3.1-8B for LLM-based entailment on cases the NLI model marks as neutral or contradicted (resolves 71.39% of facts); (3) a Mistral-7B expert decision filter as a fail-safe for the remaining 1.03%. Facts judged incorrect produce depth-of-knowledge hallucination scores (Halu_DOK). Human validation on 900 questions showed 79.19% agreement for entity-level filtering and 87.74% for fact-level checking, outperforming a GPT-3.5-Turbo automated judge by 5.65% and 8.56% respectively. Key Results: 25 frontier LLMs (15 open-source, 10 proprietary) were evaluated across 10 runs of 150 questions each. GPT-5 achieves the highest W_a at 65.60%, with GPT-4.1, Grok-4, and Grok-3 also scoring above 60%. Proprietary models collectively average 55.94% W_a versus 48.32% for open-source models with ≥235B parameters, though GLM-4.5 (open-source, 335B) reaches 54.35% W_a, approaching mid-tier proprietary performance. Halu_BOK drops dramatically from 54.75% (8–32B models) to 11.91% (large proprietary models), confirming that smaller models fail primarily at entity recognition. Halu_DOK decreases more modestly from 24.59% to approximately 23–28% for large proprietaries, indicating that even top models struggle to recall specific factual details. Roughly 39.74% of responses from proficient models still contain at least one hallucinated fact. Models with high abstention rates (e.g., GPT-oss-20B at 55.93%, Claude Sonnet 4 at 49.40%) reduce hallucination counts but at severe cost to utility. Difficulty weighting reduces standard deviation in accuracy across runs from 2.57% to 2.45%, confirming improved benchmark consistency. Strengths: - The decomposition of hallucination into Halu_BOK and Halu_DOK is genuinely novel and interpretable, revealing mechanistically distinct failure modes that aggregate metrics hide — smaller models hallucinate because they misidentify entities, while larger models hallucinate because they lack specific factual depth. - The difficulty-weighted accuracy metric W_a is well-motivated and empirically validated: it reduces run-to-run variance and mitigates entity-popularity confounds that plague prior dynamic KG benchmarks. - The tri-stage fact verification pipeline is practical (average 4.47s per question) and achieves strong human alignment (87.74%) by routing easy, explicitly-stated facts through a cheap NLI model and reserving the more expensive LLM judge for subtle cases. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Wikidata dependence introduces systematic coverage gaps: non-English entities, marginalized topics, and underrepresented cultures are sparsely represented, biasing the benchmark toward anglophone and high-profile subjects regardless of the stratified sampling strategy. - The automated verification pipeline achieves only 79.19% and 87.74% human agreement — meaningful error rates for a benchmark intended to produce reliable comparative rankings, particularly since entity-level misclassifications propagate silently into the fact-level stage without correction. - Questions are limited to open-ended entity overviews centered on three KG relations, which probes factual recall but not reasoning, multi-hop inference, or cross-entity consistency — the very capabilities where frontier model differences may be most diagnostically interesting.
Recommended 2602.19643 PDF
#49 — Innovative (64)

ConFu: Contemplate the Future for Better Speculative Sampling

Zongyue Qin, Raghavv Goel, Mukul Gagrani, Risheek Garrepalli, Mingu Lee, Yizhou Sun · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: ConFu introduces a conceptually appealing idea — conditioning draft models on the target model's latent forward intent via contemplate tokens — that cleanly addresses the error accumulation problem in speculative decoding. The surgery-free design preserving the frozen target model is practical. However, the improvements over the already strong EAGLE-3 baseline are modest (10-15% accept length gains), the MoE component adds architectural complexity for marginal benefit (+0.05), and evaluation is limited to two small Llama models on a single benchmark, leaving the value proposition at commercially relevant scales unclear.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by having a lightweight draft model propose candidate tokens that a larger target model verifies in a single parallel pass. The critical bottleneck is draft quality: existing draft models, including the state-of-the-art EAGLE series, condition token generation solely on the current prefix. This causes error accumulation — the draft model's hidden representations progressively drift from the target model's, lowering token acceptance rates as the sequence grows. The paper argues that draft models need forward-looking signals: knowledge of the target model's current high-level "thought" or semantic trajectory before committing to specific token proposals. Methodology: ConFu introduces three interlocking mechanisms. First, it injects contemplate tokens (pause tokens) and soft prompt tokens into the target model's KV cache at inference time. The contemplate token is appended after each input position, causing the target model to compute additional intermediate representations that encode its forward-looking intent. Critically, the target model is frozen — only the soft prompt embeddings and contemplate token embeddings are learned — so the output distribution is not altered. The hidden state of the contemplate token (a future vector f) is passed as an auxiliary input token to the draft model, which conditions all subsequent token proposals on this fixed signal. During verification, one contemplate token is inserted per draft tree node and processed in parallel via a modified tree attention mask, so the overhead scales as 2T tokens (T draft nodes) rather than requiring additional sequential passes. Second, because a single static contemplate embedding is insufficient across diverse generation contexts (math vs. prose), ConFu replaces it with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture: a router conditions on the hidden state of the last accepted token and produces a weighted combination of K expert embeddings, yielding context-adaptive future signals. Third, the training framework addresses two efficiency problems: anchor token sampling selects a sparse subset of positions for contemplate token insertion, growing sequence length from N to N+K_train instead of 2N; future prediction replication trains the draft model to predict tokens at nearby positions using the same future vector f, implicitly encouraging the contemplate signal to be smooth and robust across local context windows. Key Results: ConFu is evaluated on SpecBench across 11 task categories (writing, QA, summarization, translation, coding, math/reasoning, etc.) using Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as target models, with temperatures {0, 0.7, 1.0} and draft budgets of 30 and 60 nodes. Against EAGLE-3 (the strongest published baseline), ConFu consistently improves: for the 3B model under greedy decoding with 30 nodes, average accept length increases from 4.00 to 4.41 (+10.3%) and speed-up ratio improves from 1.83× to 2.11× (+15.3%). For the 8B model under the same setting, accept length rises from 4.59 to 5.01 (+9.2%) and speed-up from 2.36× to 2.69× (+14.0%). Gains are consistent across all tasks and settings, though most pronounced at lower temperatures where the target distribution is sharper. Ablations show that future prediction replication contributes ~+0.17 accept length and MoE contributes ~+0.05, confirming both components add independently. Strengths: - The core insight — that draft models should be conditioned on the target model's latent forward intent, not just its past hidden states — is conceptually clean and addresses a real, well-characterized failure mode (error accumulation) in a principled way. - The implementation is surgery-free with respect to the target model: no fine-tuning of target weights, no change to output distribution, making ConFu drop-in compatible with any frozen LLM that EAGLE supports. - The overhead analysis is rigorous: processing 2T tokens during verification vs. T is modest at typical draft tree sizes (T=30-60), and the anchor token sampling trick limits training memory growth to N+K rather than 2N. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are restricted to two Llama-3 model sizes (3B and 8B) on a single benchmark suite (SpecBench). Generalization to other model families (Mistral, Qwen, Gemma) and larger target models (70B+) is undemonstrated, which is the regime where inference acceleration matters most commercially. - The 8-11% headline improvement is measured relative to an already highly optimized baseline (EAGLE-3), but absolute wall-clock throughput numbers on real serving hardware are not reported — it is unclear how the 2T-token verification overhead interacts with batched inference or different hardware memory bandwidths beyond a single H100 with batch size 1. - The MoE contribution (+0.05 accept length) is small relative to replication (+0.17), raising the question of whether the additional architectural complexity of MoE is justified in practice versus simpler context-conditioned mechanisms.

#50 — Innovative (64)

oo7G9mheNo

oo7G9mheNo
Why this rank: CHTs24 makes a meaningful contribution by achieving true sparse training under hardware-compatible semi-structured constraints, eliminating the dense master weight requirement of STE-based methods. The theoretical grounding via Fréchet perturbation equivalence to weighted random sampling and automatic annealing through CH score variance is elegant. However, pre-training experiments are limited to 60M–130M parameter models where gains over SR-STE are narrow at 2:4 sparsity, and the absence of wall-clock speedup measurements critically undermines the hardware acceleration motivation.
