#51 — Innovative (64)

Self-Purification Mitigates Backdoors in Multimodal Diffusion Language Models

Guangnian Wan, Qi Li, Gongfan Fang, Xinyin Ma, Xinchao Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: DiSP earns credit for identifying an entirely unexplored vulnerability surface (backdoors in MDLMs) and designing an architecturally motivated defense that exploits the masked-input flexibility unique to diffusion language models—a structural insight with no AR analogue. The Fisher-Jacobian saliency estimator is technically sound. However, the evaluation is limited to projector-only fine-tuning with patch-style triggers, and the absence of adaptive adversary evaluation weakens the security claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) — which generate text via iterative masked-token denoising rather than autoregressive decoding — have emerged as competitive alternatives to AR-based multimodal LLMs, but their vulnerability to backdoor attacks is entirely unexplored. Existing backdoor defenses are designed for AR models and cannot be directly transferred to the MDLM setting, leaving a critical security gap. Adversaries can exploit this by poisoning fine-tuning datasets, causing models to exhibit attacker-controlled behaviors (content insertion, targeted refusal, semantic misclassification) whenever a visual trigger is present while behaving normally on clean inputs. Methodology: The paper introduces Diffusion Self-Purification (DiSP), a two-stage defense that exploits a property unique to MDLMs: their ability to accept partially masked inputs. The core insight is that backdoor activation is dominated by a small subset of high-saliency visual tokens — masking these tokens at inference time suppresses trigger-induced behavior without substantially degrading clean utility. Saliency scores for each visual token are estimated via the Fisher–Jacobian quadratic form, approximated efficiently using a Hutchinson estimator with Monte Carlo probes (avoiding the prohibitively expensive explicit J⊤FJ product). The top-ρ fraction of visual tokens by saliency are replaced with mask embeddings, and the compromised model is run on these masked inputs to generate purified responses. These purified (image, clean-response) pairs replace the poisoned responses in the training set, which is then used to fine-tune the backdoored model — eliminating the embedded backdoor without requiring auxiliary clean models or external reference data. Key Results: Experiments on LLaDA-V and LaViDa across three backdoor targets (content insertion, targeted refusal, semantic misclassification) show that without defense, backdoored models achieve ASR >90% on triggered inputs. DiSP reduces ASR to ≤1.5% in all settings on LLaDA-V (e.g., content insertion: 92.5% → 0.5%; targeted refusal: 98.5% → 1.0%; misclassification: 94.5% → 0.5%), with clean performance (MMMU accuracy) essentially unchanged (49.56 → 49.22, within noise). Baseline defenses — random data drop, model pruning, and the AR-targeted BYE filter — leave ASR between 15% and 100% on LLaDA-V. DiSP also generalizes across four trigger types (noise patch, multi-trigger, blended trigger), reducing ASR to 0% in three of four cases. Ablation confirms both saliency-guided token selection and the masked-input rewriting step are necessary: removing either causes ASR to remain ≥88%. Strengths: - Identifies and exploits a structural property of MDLMs (masked-input flexibility) that has no analogue in AR models, producing a defense that is architecturally motivated rather than heuristic. - Requires no auxiliary clean models, no external reference data, and no knowledge of which samples are poisoned — matching the realistic threat model where defenders only control the training pipeline. - The Fisher–Jacobian saliency estimator is computationally practical: the Hutchinson approximation avoids forming the full Jacobian, making the method scalable to large vision encoders. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments fine-tune only the projector while keeping the vision encoder and LLM backbone frozen; it is unclear how DiSP performs when full model fine-tuning is used, which is a more powerful (and increasingly common) attack surface. - The defense is evaluated on patch-style visual triggers; adaptive attacks that specifically craft triggers to have diffuse, low-saliency visual signatures could potentially evade the saliency-based masking step, and no such adaptive adversary is considered. - Only two MDLMs (LLaDA-V, LaViDa) and relatively small poisoned datasets (1,000 training samples) are tested; scalability to larger pre-training-scale poisoning scenarios remains undemonstrated.
Recommended 2602.22246 PDF
#52 — Innovative (64)

Pancake: Hierarchical Memory System for Multi-Agent LLM Serving

Zhengding Hu, Zaifeng Pan, Prabhleen Kaur, Vibha Murthy, Zhongkai Yu, Yue Guan, Zhen Wang, Steven Swanson, Yufei Ding · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Pancake delivers a well-engineered systems contribution with strong empirical results — reducing memory operation overhead from 82% to 3.2% of execution time is practically significant. The FSM-based access pattern modeling and hybrid-graph multi-agent coordination are technically sound. The work is primarily systems optimization rather than conceptual novelty, applying known indexing and caching techniques to a new workload profile.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLM agents increasingly rely on external memory stores to maintain action histories, user profiles, and knowledge bases, approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search over these stores has become a critical performance bottleneck. Existing memory frameworks like Mem-GPT and A-Mem suffer from memory operations consuming over 82% of total execution time at scale. The root causes are threefold: (1) single-agent workloads exhibit fine-grained interleaved search-insert patterns that scatter inserted vectors across many IVF clusters due to the high-dimensional shell effect, degrading both search efficiency and recall; (2) multi-agent scenarios compound this by requiring each agent to traverse the full coarse index of every other agent, causing coarse search overhead to reach 80% of latency at just 20 agents; and (3) GPU-CPU hybrid platforms are underutilized because existing caching strategies assume static indexes, while agentic workloads require frequent updates that break GPU-resident consistency. Methodology: Pancake introduces a three-pronged multi-tier ANN memory system. First, a pattern-driven multi-level index cache handles single-agent memory: a three-level hierarchy (L0 of Np recently accessed vectors, L1 of intermediate clusters caching top-k' neighbors, L2 of stable coarse clusters) is organized to reflect the agent's intrinsic locality. Crucially, each agent's memory access sequence is modeled as a Finite State Machine (FSM) over semantic cluster states, capturing both intra-request locality and inter-request step-wise locality. These FSMs drive workload-aware search reordering, early termination (skipping lower levels when top-k candidates satisfy a distance threshold α_et · d_agent), and prefetching of likely-next clusters during parallel LLM generation. Second, multi-agent index coordination is achieved via a hybrid graph that unifies all agents' coarse indexes into a single navigable structure with sparse inter-graph connections (probability 1/ef_connect) enabling cross-scope coarse search in a single BFS-like traversal rather than per-agent traversals. Agent-specific profiles associated with static clusters track each agent's local access vectors to improve fine-search recall without modifying the global index. Third, dynamic GPU-CPU index coordination caches hotspot clusters on GPU based on access frequency, maintains a CPU-side per-cluster insertion buffer (size B_insert = 128) to absorb fine-grained updates without synchronous transfers, and uses fully asynchronous GPU-CPU migration with proactive cluster-space allocation and GPU-based K-means for on-GPU cluster splitting. Key Results: Pancake is evaluated on 8× NVIDIA H100 GPUs against agent-framework baselines (A-Mem, MemGPT, LlamaIndex, LangMem) and standalone vector-database baselines (Quake, SpFresh, DiskANN) across six agent datasets (UltraChat, Prm800k, Gsm8k, APIGen, AgentGym, UltraFDBK) and four workload patterns. In single-agent end-to-end throughput, Pancake achieves 1.12× to 26.18× improvements over existing libraries, averaging more than 4.29× speedup, reducing memory operation time to an average of 3.2% of total execution. Memory-only operation speedup exceeds 6.81×. For vector-database throughput under agent workloads, Pancake achieves 1.9× to 4.2× speedup over Quake, SpFresh, and DiskANN baselines; GPU acceleration yields an additional 2.2× gain. In two-agent mixed-workload scenarios, existing frameworks degrade 29.9%–55.9% while Pancake holds degradation to under 9.8%. With up to 20 concurrent agents, end-to-end performance degradation stays below 10.2%, demonstrating near-linear scalability. The multi-level cache reduces scanned vectors by up to 2.23× in latency over long-serving workloads, and the hybrid-graph agent-profile mechanism reduces total vector similarity computations by 11.6% (structure) plus an additional 21.8% (agent profiles) compared to independent per-agent indexes. Strengths: - Thorough empirical motivation grounded in real agent workload traces: the scattered-cluster, step-wise locality, and GPU-CPU cost characterizations are concrete and drive each design choice directly. - Practical design that integrates with existing frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, MemGPT) through a simple Python API, lowering adoption friction and enabling real deployment. - The hybrid-graph approach elegantly eliminates O(N_agents) coarse-index traversals through a single BFS traversal, with inter-graph connection density adaptively calibrated to relative index densities. - Comprehensive ablation study isolates the contribution of each component (multi-level cache, hybrid graph, agent profiles, GPU caching) with clear attribution of speedup sources. Weaknesses / Limitations: - FSM convergence requires a warm-up period: the paper acknowledges that IVF-Split shows a "long pre-convergence phase," and while Pancake's multi-level cache adapts faster, cold-start behavior for new agents or rapidly shifting workloads is not directly quantified. - The hybrid-graph connection density parameter α_ic (empirically set to 4–8) and early-termination threshold α_et (0.6–0.8) require per-deployment tuning and the sensitivity of these choices across memory scales beyond the MS MARCO 8M-passage corpus is not analyzed. - GPU memory competition with LLM inference weights and KV caches is acknowledged but not deeply studied; the 5–15 GB hotspot cache assumed for Pancake may be difficult to sustain on heavily loaded inference servers, and the degradation curve beyond that budget is not characterized.
Recommended 2602.21477 PDF
#53 — Innovative (64)

Deep Dense Exploration for LLM Reinforcement Learning via Pivot-Driven Resampling

Yiran Guo, Zhongjian Qiao, Yingqi Xie, Jie Liu, Dan Ye, Ruiqing Zhang, Shuang Qiu, Lijie Xu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 64 for a well-motivated and cleanly executed approach to exploration sparsity in LLM RL. The pivot-based resampling with dual-stream optimization addresses a real limitation of tree-based methods, and the finding that depth concentration beats breadth is a useful insight. However, the absolute gains over baselines are modest (1-2% on most benchmarks), and restriction to mathematical reasoning with binary rewards leaves generalization unclear.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning for LLMs (e.g., GRPO) suffers from exploration sparsity: sampling exclusively from the root means the policy's probability distribution determines where budget flows, so high-probability trajectories are oversampled while deep, error-prone states remain statistically inaccessible. Simply scaling root rollouts (N=8 to N=64 on GSM8K) yields diminishing returns, saturating at ~66% accuracy. Tree-based branching methods attempt to reach deeper states but disperse budget too broadly, creating sparse per-branch samples that produce unstable advantage estimates and introduce optimization bias via prefix duplication—multiple branches sharing the same prefix accumulate redundant gradient updates, destabilizing training and causing performance regression in later stages. Methodology: The paper introduces Deep Dense Exploration (DDE), instantiated as DEEP-GRPO, which redirects the sampling budget to "pivots"—deep, recoverable states embedded within failed trajectories. The method operates in three integrated components. First, utility-guided pivot selection scores candidate branching points via Q(t) ∝ P_φ(success | s<t) · (t/T)^γ, balancing recoverability (estimated by an online logistic regression model trained on historical trajectory outcomes) against depth bias (controlled by hyperparameter γ). Second, dense local resampling generates K auxiliary completions from the selected pivot, concentrating enough samples at a single point to compute stable local advantage estimates. Third, a dual-stream optimization objective decouples global policy learning (main chain loss over root-sampled trajectories) from local corrective updates (auxiliary chain loss with gradient masking on the shared prefix), combined as J(θ) = main_loss + λ·aux_loss. This separation prevents prefix duplication bias and controls the influence of the auxiliary signal. The success probability estimator P_φ is a lightweight logistic regression (P_φ(success | r_t) = σ(w·r_t + b)) updated online from a replay buffer, requiring no external annotations. Key Results: On GSM8K with Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct, DEEP-GRPO achieves 67.7% accuracy versus GRPO-n64 at 66.2%, TreeRL at 65.5%, and AttnRL at 67.0%—outperforming all baselines while using the same total compute budget as GRPO-n8 (1 pivot, 8 branches). On five harder mathematical benchmarks with 1.5B models, DEEP-GRPO-1.5B achieves 43.3% average accuracy versus 42.1% for Oat-Zero-1.5B (Dr.GRPO), 39.8% for Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B-Instruct, and 33.1% for Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B. At the 7B scale, DEEP-GRPO-7B achieves 54.0% average versus 51.4% for Dr.GRPO-7B, 51.0% for GPG-7B, and 48.0% for PRIME-Zero-7B. Gains are most pronounced on AIME24 (46.7% vs. 43.3% for Dr.GRPO), indicating the approach particularly benefits hard problems requiring deep error recovery. Training dynamics show DEEP-GRPO maintains consistently higher policy entropy and produces longer responses throughout training, attributed to emergent self-correction behavior. Strengths: - The core insight is well-motivated and empirically verified: concentrating budget at a single deep pivot with K=8 branches outperforms dispersing the same budget across P=2 pivots with K=4 branches each, establishing that depth concentration beats breadth. - The dual-stream optimization with gradient masking cleanly addresses the prefix duplication problem that plagues tree-based methods, with Appendix E providing empirical evidence of the instability it prevents. - The online logistic regression estimator for recoverability is lightweight and data-driven, learning pivot utility without external process reward models or human annotations, making the approach practical within standard RL training loops. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The two-stage sampling (root rollouts then sequential pivot branching) introduces a sequential dependency that reduces training throughput compared to GRPO's fully parallelizable root sampling—a real deployment cost the paper acknowledges but does not quantify precisely. - A significant fraction of selected pivots remain "unrecoverable" throughout training (Figure 11 shows ~50 such states persisting at step 2500), meaning the depth bias systematically targets states from which the model cannot recover with rejection sampling alone, wasting auxiliary budget on intractable states. - Experiments are limited to mathematical reasoning with verifiable binary rewards; it is unclear whether pivot recoverability estimation generalizes to tasks with partial rewards, open-ended generation, or non-decomposable objectives.
Recommended 2602.14169 PDF
#54 — Innovative (63)

MoEless: Efficient MoE LLM Serving via Serverless Computing

Hanfei Yu, Bei Ouyang, Shwai He, Ang Li, Hao Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The serverless expert decoupling is a clean architectural insight that maps well to the elastic scaling problem in MoE serving, and the gate-network reuse for load prediction is a practical and clever design choice. The cost reductions are dramatic (92-95% over baselines). That said, the core contribution is primarily systems engineering—applying established serverless patterns and greedy heuristics to a new domain—rather than a fundamental algorithmic advance, and the single 8-GPU NVLink testbed leaves open questions about multi-node scalability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs are widely adopted to scale model capacity without proportional compute costs, but their sparse activation mechanism creates severe expert load imbalance during serving under expert parallelism (EP). A small subset of "hot" experts receive disproportionately high token loads while others sit idle, causing the straggler problem: overloaded GPUs delay the all-to-all gather synchronization that all other GPUs must wait on, inflating inference latency and serving cost. Existing load balancing solutions (e.g., EPLB from DeepSeek) rely on static resource configurations on serverful infrastructure, limiting scalability and elasticity, and must choose between costly real-time expert swapping or lossy expert re-routing that degrades generation quality. Methodology: MoEless is the first serverless MoE serving framework that decouples experts from the MoE model and packages them as independent serverless functions, enabling fine-grained elastic scaling without touching the non-expert modules in data parallelism. The system has three main components: (1) Expert Load Predictor: lightweight neural networks that reuse and fine-tune the MoE model's own gate networks to speculate expert load distributions d layers ahead, leveraging the high cosine similarity between residual-connected hidden states across layers. A layer-aware fine-tuning strategy selectively fine-tunes only low-accuracy (early) layers, keeping overhead minimal (≤80K parameters per predictor, full fine-tuning in under 5 minutes on one GPU). Prediction runs asynchronously on a dedicated CUDA stream with zero latency impact. (2) Expert Scaler: a greedy heuristic that iteratively identifies straggler experts using a max-heap, adds replicas, and splits their load until the coefficient of variation (CV) of loads drops below a threshold (CV ≤ 0.2) or a per-layer memory cap is reached. The resulting scaling plan targets per-layer balanced load distributions within a memory budget. (3) Expert Placer: assigns replicas to GPUs using a Join-the-Shortest-Queue strategy that reuses warm replica instances to avoid serverless cold-start overhead, while balancing aggregate GPU loads to minimize straggler effects and all-to-all communication overhead. All three components operate asynchronously with a configurable prediction distance (default d=1 layer) to fully overlap with ongoing inference. Key Results: MoEless is evaluated on three MoE models — Mixtral-8×7B, Phi-3.5-MoE, and Llama-4-Scout — across two real-world LLM serving traces (LMSYS-Chat-1M and ShareGPT) on an 8-GPU NVIDIA A6000 testbed. Against Megatron-LM and EPLB as serverful baselines: MoEless reduces average MoE layer forward latency by 43.19% vs. Megatron-LM and 21.89% vs. EPLB across all models and datasets. On total inference cost (GPU memory × latency), MoEless reduces cost by 92.68% vs. Megatron-LM, 84.06% vs. Oracle, and 95.11% vs. EPLB. Expert load prediction accuracy improves by up to 18% over Mixtral-offloading and 15% over ProMoE baselines. MoEless's performance tracks closely to the Oracle upper bound (which achieves perfect load balance but at the cost of generation quality), demonstrating near-optimal load balancing without sacrificing output fidelity. Predictor memory footprint is ≤2% of ProMoE's (e.g., 1.92 MB vs. 128.32 MB for Mixtral-8×7B). Ablation confirms all three components are essential — removing any one degrades latency. Strengths: - Elegant co-design insight: reusing the model's own gate networks as load predictors (with lightweight fine-tuning) avoids large external predictor overhead while achieving superior accuracy, making the approach both practical and deployable - The serverless expert decoupling design cleanly separates scaling concerns — non-expert modules stay in static data parallelism while only experts scale elastically — yielding dramatic cost reductions without generation quality loss - Layer-aware fine-tuning is well-motivated by empirical observation that early layers have lower input similarity and need more tuning, whereas later layers are naturally more predictable; this targeted approach is both efficient and principled Weaknesses / Limitations: - System parameters (prediction distance, CV threshold, memory cap) are determined via offline profiling and remain fixed at runtime; no adaptive or learned mechanism is provided, which may limit generalization to highly non-stationary workloads - The evaluation is confined to a single 8-GPU NVLink-connected testbed; scalability to larger clusters with multi-node all-to-all communication — where communication costs dominate differently — is not validated - Standard serverless management schemes (pre-warming, fixed keep-alive periods) are borrowed wholesale to handle cold starts; the paper explicitly defers more advanced runtime optimizations, leaving open whether the approach remains effective under extreme cold-start or rapid workload shifts
Recommended 2603.06350 PDF
#55 — Innovative (63)

An Efficient Memoization Engine for Concurrent Graph Query Processing (complete version)

gaosen.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: KGraph demonstrates meaningful innovation through its partition-based memoization approach that elegantly recovers small-graph memoization benefits on large graphs, combined with the decision-tree-based optimizer that removes manual tuning across diverse graph topologies. The Grab production deployment validates practical applicability, though the core ideas of memoization and graph partitioning are individually well-established.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Concurrent Graph Query (CGQ) processing—executing many homogeneous graph queries simultaneously—is a fundamental bottleneck in applications ranging from DeepWalk and Personalized PageRank to real-time taxi-hailing services (Grab processes 1.5K SSSP queries/minute at peak). Prior work on CGQ optimization (e.g., ForkGraph) focused on cache sharing via graph partitioning, but ignored computation sharing: the authors' profiling reveals that ~90% of edges appearing in one query's result also appear in another's, and >10% of edges appear in at least 90 queries' results. Naive memoization fails on large graphs because the operation reduction ratio is low while the overhead of scanning memoized results is high—producing 20% slowdowns on 100M-edge graphs even while delivering 2.3× speedups on 10K-edge graphs. Methodology: KGraph is a CGQ memoization engine built on two principal innovations. First, partition-based memoization with adaptation: rather than memoizing query results over the entire graph, KGraph partitions the graph and memoizes results only within each partition. This localizes memory accesses, raises the operation reduction ratio (emulating small-graph behavior), and fits data within LLC using the constraint |E|+|V|/|P| + k/|P|×Mem_max ≤ Size_LLC. A cost model estimates optimal partition size by balancing intra-partition processing cost against inter-partition coordination overhead. Second, strategic pivot query selection: KGraph pre-selects a small set of "pivot" queries whose results are memoized and made available to all CGQs. Six pivot selection strategies (S1–S6) spanning random, degree-sorted, and boundary-vertex criteria are evaluated; a decision tree trained on graph features (average degree, max degree, |V|) automatically selects the best strategy and pivot count for a given graph. The optimizer combines the cost model and decision tree. The execution engine extends ForkGraph's APIs with two new primitives—INITMEMOIZATIONSTORAGE and MEMOIZATIONFUNCTOR—that users implement to define memoization logic, with parallelism across pivot queries being embarrassingly parallel. Five application types are supported: SSSP, SSWP, SSR (traversal-based), DeepWalk, and PPR (random walk-based). Key Results: On 8 real-world graphs (4.6M–133.6M vertices, road networks to social/web graphs) against ForkGraph, KGraph achieves up to 12.5× speedup with an average of 4.1×. For traversal-based queries (SSSP, SSWP, SSR), KGraph outperforms ForkGraph in all cases, with SSSP showing up to 9.35× on Friendster. KGraph significantly outperforms Glign (a traversal-specialized baseline) on road networks while matching it on social networks. For random walk queries (DeepWalk, PPR), KGraph nearly doubles speeds on road networks but shows only ~20% improvement on social networks due to large boundary-vertex counts and the small-world topology. On synthetic R-MAT graphs scaling to 100M edges with densities up to 64, KGraph achieves 49.4× speedup over Ligra (memoization-augmented Ligra) with operation reduction ratios reaching 47.8–61.3% vs. Ligra's 15–22% at density 64. In the Grab Jakarta case study, applying KGraph reduced total query processing time from 6,519ms to 725ms (with only 8ms for memoization overhead), well within acceptable latency for real-time ride-hailing. Memory overhead for traversal-based pivot memoization remains modest (0.6–53.5% of graph size) but grows substantially for random walk applications (up to 384% for DeepWalk at α=2.2). Strengths: - Rigorous empirical motivation: the profiling study (operation reduction ratio distributions, query result overlapping distributions) provides principled justification for why and when memoization helps, distinguishing this from ad hoc engineering. - The partition-based approach elegantly recovers small-graph memoization benefits on large graphs by exploiting locality, with a theoretically grounded cost model and LLC-fit constraint. - The decision tree-based optimizer removes manual tuning, generalizing across road networks, social networks, and web graphs with a single trained model; the real Grab case study validates practical applicability at production scale. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The memory overhead for random walk applications (DeepWalk, PPR) is prohibitive at higher pivot counts—exceeding the graph size itself—limiting practical applicability for walk-based workloads on memory-constrained systems. - The performance gains on social networks are marginal for random walks (~20%), and the system's benefit is highly graph-topology-dependent; the decision tree mitigates but does not fully resolve the risk of poor configuration selection (e.g., wrong partition count can degrade performance by 2×). - The approach requires users to implement custom MEMOIZATIONFUNCTOR and INITMEMOIZATIONSTORAGE logic, introducing programming burden and potential for incorrect implementations that undermine memoization correctness or efficiency.
Citations
#56 — Innovative (63)

Towards Controllable and Interpretable Latent Modeling for Vision and Beyond

ses.library.usyd.edu.au · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This PhD thesis introduces a coherent selective modeling framework applied across four diverse domains, with SAVA's formal recovery of the minimal Markov blanket being a rare theoretical contribution in multimodal generation. The OCP-CL and CoF modules are plug-and-play and demonstrate consistent improvements, though as a thesis compilation the individual contributions are meaningful rather than paradigm-shifting.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep learning models for vision and multimodal tasks rely on high-dimensional latent representations that are difficult to interpret and control. Task-relevant semantic factors become entangled with irrelevant variation, latent dimensions lack clear functional roles, and standard learning objectives (e.g., contrastive learning with aggressive augmentations) can suppress the very information needed for structured prediction tasks. This PhD thesis investigates how latent representations and latent structures can be organized to support both interpretability and controllability across four problem domains: ordinal visual learning, structured reasoning in large vision models, multimodal generation, and agentic workflow design. The unifying principle is selective modeling: preserving information that is causally relevant to a task while suppressing or filtering out irrelevant variation, rather than treating latent spaces as monolithic vectors. Methodology: The thesis proposes four distinct technical contributions. (1) OCP-CL (Ordinal Content Preserving Contrastive Learning, ICLR 2024): Ordinal regression is harmed by standard contrastive augmentations (blur, color jitter) that destroy fine-grained semantic cues like wrinkles or retinal lesions. The method trains a GAN (StyleGAN2-based) with a learnable binary mask enforcing a minimal change principle via L1 sparsity, disentangling latent space into ordinal (z_o) and non-ordinal (z_n) factors. Content-preserving augmentations are generated by fixing z_o and resampling z_n, then used as strong views in a supervised contrastive loss that can be plugged into any ordinal regression backbone. (2) Chain-of-Focus (CoF) Prompting (ICLR 2025): Large Autoregressive Vision Models (LAVMs) lack structured reasoning analogous to chain-of-thought in LLMs. CoF introduces saliency-based intermediate visual reasoning steps, progressively annotating regions in descending saliency order (via U²-Net) to mimic human perceptual attention. Prompt pairs are selected by two criteria: query relevance via Jaccard similarity of VQGAN codebook indices, and annotation diversity (codebook cardinality of targets). No model finetuning is required. (3) SAVA (Selective Audio-Visual Alignment, NeurIPS 2025): Text-to-audio-video (T2AV) generation suffers because audio and visual streams have only partial correspondence—roads don't produce sound, wind lacks visual presence. SAVA learns binary masks M_V and M_A (conditioned on both modalities) via cross-modal reconstruction with L1 sparsity, provably recovering the minimal Markov blanket of shared latent factors. Fine-tuning uses LoRA on conditioning layers of pretrained T2V and T2A diffusion models; inference proceeds in a cascaded fashion. (4) MedDCR (arXiv 2025): Agentic workflows are treated as learnable latent computational graphs. A meta-agent loop with three roles—Designer (proposes workflows), Coder (executes and self-fixes), Reflector (evaluates and gives feedback)—searches for effective ICD-10 medical coding workflows over a memory archive initialized with seed strategies. Key Results: OCP-CL consistently improves all five ordinal regression baselines (OR-CNN, CNNPOR, SORD, POE, MWR) across three datasets: on Adience (age estimation) accuracy improves by 1.0–5.3% and MAE reduces by 2–8.5%; on Diabetic Retinopathy by 0.8–1.8% accuracy; on SkyFinder by 1.1–3.8%. In the transfer learning evaluation OCP-CL achieves 65.2%/53.1%/57.5% accuracy on DR/Adience/SkyFinder, outperforming SupMoCo, SupCon, S-LooC, and SupCReg. CoF prompting on image segmentation with LLaMA-7B achieves IoU 52.53% and P-ACC 67.05% vs. 49.41%/65.04% for the best baseline (SupPR); failure rates drop by 7.7–13.9% relative to random prompting on LLaMA-1B and LLaMA-7B. SAVA on VGGSound achieves FVD 662.9, FAD 5.49, AVHScore 0.206, CAVPSIM 0.165, versus the best prior T2AV baseline (CoDi: FVD 709.4, FAD 6.17, AVHScore 0.153); relative reductions of 6.5% in FVD and 16% in FAD. On AudioCaps, SAVA achieves the highest CAVPSIM (0.230 bidirectional) among all tested methods. V2A generation also outperforms SpecVQGAN and SeeHear on KL divergence and FAD. Strengths: - The unifying selective modeling framework is conceptually coherent and applied convincingly across four diverse problem settings (ordinal regression, visual ICL, multimodal generation, agentic AI), demonstrating broad generality - SAVA includes a formal theoretical result (recovery of the minimal Markov blanket under four standard assumptions) that distinguishes the work from purely empirical alignment approaches; this is a rare rigor in multimodal generation papers - OCP-CL and CoF are plug-and-play modules that improve existing architectures without requiring architectural redesign, making them practically adoptable - Strong empirical validation with ablations: each chapter systematically isolates contribution of individual components (mask sparsity, cognitive reasoning steps, query relevance, annotation diversity) Weaknesses / Limitations: - OCP-CL relies on a GAN for content-preserving augmentation, introducing additional training complexity and the well-known instability of GAN training; the approach has not been demonstrated with modern diffusion-based generators - CoF prompting depends on an off-the-shelf saliency detector (U²-Net) and VQGAN tokenizer, creating a pipeline dependency that may fail when saliency is ambiguous or the tokenizer's codebook poorly represents the target task; performance degrades from two to more intermediate steps in smaller LAVMs, suggesting sensitivity to model capacity - The theoretical guarantees for SAVA rely on block-wise reparameterization and decoder universality assumptions that, while standard, may not hold tightly in practice with finite-capacity networks trained on limited data - MedDCR's workflow search is computationally expensive (token costs reported per 100 inference samples) and is evaluated only on medical coding; generalization to other agentic domains is asserted but not demonstrated empirically
Citations
#57 — Innovative (62)

Multimodal multi-instance learning for cardiopulmonary exercise testing performance prediction

nature.com · Fei Wang, FAMIA, FIAHSI, FACMI - new articles
Why this rank: Scores 62 for the principled integration of multi-instance learning with multimodal fusion for CPET prediction, directly mirroring clinical workflow where physicians review variable-length image sets collectively. The use of TabPFN as a tabular foundation model for EHR encoding within a fully differentiable pipeline and the patient embedding regularization technique are meaningful technical contributions. The clinical motivation is strong — democratizing functional capacity assessment beyond specialized centers. However, the dataset is modest (n=1,000), the age subgroup disparity (R²=0.571 vs 0.249) undermines clinical readiness, and the individual architectural components are standard.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) is the gold standard for assessing functional capacity and predicting survival in heart failure (HF), with peak oxygen consumption (peak VO₂) being the critical metric that determines eligibility for advanced therapies such as heart transplantation or LVAD implantation. However, CPET adoption is severely constrained by requirements for specialized equipment, trained personnel, and dedicated infrastructure — limiting its use especially in community and rural settings. Despite affecting ~7 million Americans (projected to exceed 10 million by 2040), advanced HF has <50% one-year survival yet only ~6,000 patients annually receive lifesaving interventions. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) and EHR data are universally available but have demonstrated limited predictive value for survival outcomes when used with traditional approaches. Prior AI work (Alishetti et al.) demonstrated feasibility of predicting peak VO₂ from TTE+EHR but used a naive ensemble of independently trained unimodal models under a single-instance learning paradigm, failing to model cross-modal interactions or the inherent multi-instance structure of echocardiographic studies. Methodology: The proposed framework introduces two key technical innovations over prior work. First, multi-instance learning (MIL) replaces single-instance learning for each TTE imaging modality. Each TTE study contains variable numbers of images per modality (1–29 2D cine loops, 1–9 M-Mode recordings, 1–37 Spectral Doppler images); rather than treating each image independently, MIL treats the full set per modality as a "bag" with a single patient-level label. Separate MIL modules are constructed for each modality branch: Video Swin Transformer-T encodes 2D cine loops (resized to 112×112 per frame), and Swin Transformer-T encodes M-Mode and Spectral Doppler images (resized to 224×224). Attention pooling aggregates instance-level feature vectors (dimensionality 250) into a modality-specific patient representation via learned attention weights. Second, multimodal fusion replaces simple score averaging: modality-specific representations from TTE branches are fused with EHR features encoded via TabPFN — a tabular foundation model pre-trained on 100M+ synthetic datasets via 12 Transformer layers — enabling differentiable joint representation learning across all modalities. A novel patient embedding regularization term (inspired by proximal regularization) stabilizes training. The two-step training strategy (modality-specific branches trained and frozen first, then the fusion module trained) outperforms end-to-end training given the limited dataset size (n=1,000). Training/validation/test split is 64%/16%/20% across five predefined random splits. The dataset spans four NewYork-Presbyterian hospitals, with Site 1 (n=1,000) as development cohort and Sites 2–4 (n=127) as external validation, never seen during training. Key Results: On the held-out internal test set, the multimodal MIL model achieves R²=0.603 for peak VO₂ regression (vs. R²=0.529 for prior work; RMSE: 4.853 vs. 5.280; MAE: 3.667 vs. 4.021) and AUROC=0.849 for high-risk patient identification (peak VO₂ ≤14 mL/kg/min threshold per Mancini criteria; vs. AUROC=0.836). Critically, on the external validation cohort, the model achieves R²=0.541 (vs. 0.395) and AUROC=0.870 (vs. 0.797), demonstrating substantially better generalization. The value of multimodal fusion is confirmed via ablations: 2D cine loop alone gives R²=0.396/AUROC=0.780; adding M-Mode and Spectral Doppler improves to R²=0.469/AUROC=0.804; EHR alone yields R²=0.567/AUROC=0.794; full fusion achieves R²=0.603/AUROC=0.849. MIL consistently outperforms single-instance learning across all individual modality branches (e.g., 2D cine loop R² improves from 0.384 to 0.396; AUROC from 0.762 to 0.780). Patient embedding regularization improves internal R² from 0.585 to 0.603 and external R² from 0.493 to 0.541. KNN evaluation in embedding space achieves AUROC 0.784–0.847, confirming the learned representations encode clinically meaningful patient similarity. Subgroup analysis reveals age disparity: R²=0.571 for patients 18–59 vs. R²=0.249 for age ≥60. Spectral Doppler generalizes poorly cross-site (R² drops 0.513 externally) while M-Mode and EHR generalize well. Strengths: - The MIL formulation directly mirrors clinical workflow (clinicians review all images collectively), providing principled justification over single-instance approaches and yielding consistent performance gains across all modalities. - Using TabPFN for EHR encoding elegantly bridges the tree-based vs. neural network divide for tabular data, enabling a fully differentiable multimodal fusion pipeline with meaningful patient-level embeddings — a capability absent in prior ensemble approaches. - External multicenter validation without any fine-tuning, demonstrating genuine generalizability; the patient embedding regularization technique specifically improves external cohort performance, suggesting it reduces overfitting to site-specific distributional artifacts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Dataset scale is modest (n=1,000 development, n=127 external), all from a single metropolitan area's academic hospital network, limiting the ability to adopt advanced training strategies (pretraining, domain adaptation) and constraining evaluation of geographic/institutional generalizability; the external cohort is particularly small for definitive cross-site assessment. - Significant age subgroup disparity (R²=0.571 for <60 vs. 0.249 for ≥60) indicates the model is poorly calibrated for older patients who carry the highest HF burden, and racial disparities in high-risk identification (AUROC 0.903 white vs. 0.790 non-white) raise fairness concerns for clinical deployment — both remain unaddressed by the current approach. - The retrospective study design introduces confounding by indication (only patients already referred for CPET are included), TTE and CPET are not simultaneous (up to 6 months apart), and the model does not disentangle physiologic components of VO₂ (cardiac output, peripheral extraction, ventilatory efficiency), limiting mechanistic interpretability despite HiResCAM saliency analysis.
Recommended
#58 — Innovative (62)

Investigation of Bcl-2 3'UTR-driven Plant Stress Tolerance Through MicroRNA and RNA Interference

eprints.qut.edu.au · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This thesis scores 62 for demonstrating the first cross-kingdom biogenesis of human Bcl-2 3'UTR-derived small RNAs in plants, a genuinely novel finding that bridges non-coding RNA biology across kingdoms. The mechanistic link from UTR-derived sRNAs to a coherent pro-survival transcriptomic signature (ERQC→UPR→autophagy) is creative and advances beyond two decades of purely phenomenological observations. However, the absence of direct causal validation between the 197 identified sRNAs and stress tolerance, reliance on only two transgenic lines, and restriction to a non-crop model species temper the novelty with significant uncertainty about the proposed mechanism and practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Abiotic stresses such as drought and salinity are accelerating threats to global food security, with climate change projected to require a 56–110% increase in food production by 2050. Genetic engineering of stress-resilience traits offers a faster route than conventional breeding. Earlier observations established that transgenic plants expressing the human Bcl-2 3' Untranslated Region (3'UTR) — a non-protein-coding sequence — exhibit enhanced tolerance to a range of abiotic and biotic stresses, yet the underlying molecular mechanism has remained entirely unknown for over two decades. Because UTRs lack coding potential, the regulatory effect must arise via RNA-based mechanisms such as miRNA or RNAi pathways. This thesis investigates whether the Bcl-2 3'UTR generates small RNAs (sRNAs) in transgenic plants and how these modulate endogenous gene expression to confer stress tolerance, filling a critical mechanistic gap in cross-kingdom non-coding RNA biology. Methodology: The first 713 bp of the human Bcl-2 3'UTR was cloned downstream of the CaMV35S constitutive promoter in the binary vector pOPT-EBX and introduced into Nicotiana benthamiana by Agrobacterium-mediated leaf disc transformation. Thirteen T0 transgenic lines were confirmed by PCR; Southern blot analysis identified four single-copy events, and two of these (lines #23 and #26) were selected for detailed study. Transgenic expression was verified by RT-PCR. Stress phenotyping used (1) a floating leaf disc assay at 150 mM NaCl with chlorophyll quantification, and (2) a whole-plant water deficit protocol in which soil VWC was held at 10% for two weeks, with measurements of leaf relative water content (RWC), CO2 assimilation, electrolyte leakage rate, and Evans Blue dye uptake. For mechanistic studies, a custom in silico miRNA prediction pipeline was developed: the UTR sequence was fragmented, putative stem-loops identified via the Vienna RNA Package (MFE algorithm), candidates aligned to miRBase v22.1, and targets predicted using psRNATarget against the N. benthamiana transcriptome. This was cross-validated by NGS (Illumina NovaSeq 6000 sRNA and mRNA libraries) from well-watered and water-deficit-stressed transgenic and non-transgenic plants. Differential gene expression (DESeq2), GO enrichment (GOEnrichment), and pathway analysis (MapMan/Mercator4) were performed using Galaxy Australia and QUT HPC. Additionally, Chapter 6 tested biotechnology applications: Bcl-2 3'UTR as a transcriptional terminator and as a co-infiltration agent to boost transient GFP expression via Agrobacterium leaf infiltration. Key Results: All four transgenic lines showed significantly greater chlorophyll retention than wild-type under 150 mM NaCl (P<0.05), with transgenic lines retaining ~56–58 µg total chlorophyll/well versus ~54 µg in WT. Under whole-plant water deficit, line #26 maintained leaf RWC of approximately 83% versus ~68% in WT (P<0.05). Membrane integrity was significantly better in transgenic plants: line #26 exhibited electrolyte leakage rates approximately half that of WT (P<0.05), and Evans Blue dye uptake of 2.65 nmol/g dry weight versus 3.00 nmol/g in WT (P<0.05). Photosynthetic rate (CO2 assimilation) showed no significant difference between transgenic and WT under stress. NGS confirmed 197 distinct sRNAs with 100% sequence identity to the Bcl-2 3'UTR in transgenic plants (with ≤25 reads mapping in non-transgenics), demonstrating in planta processing of the human sequence. The in silico pipeline predicted 34 candidate miRNAs; 25 of these were directly confirmed by NGS (16.5% coverage of the total NGS-derived sRNA set). Bcl-2 3'UTR transcripts did not show canonical endogenous target mimicry (eTM) activity. Transcriptomic analysis (DESeq2/Mapman) revealed that transgenic line #26 under water deficit upregulated 44 ERQC genes (chaperones, calnexin, calreticulin, BiP), 36 UPR genes (IRE1, bZIP60, GAAP, PDI), and core autophagy genes (Atg1, Atg5, Atg7, Atg8, Atg9, Atg13, Vps34), while pro-death executors (VPE vacuolar processing enzymes, metacaspases MCP1/2) were expressed at lower levels than in non-transgenics. In biotechnology applications, Bcl-2 3'UTR functioned as a weak transcriptional terminator in isolation; co-infiltration with Bcl-2 3'UTR-expressing constructs significantly elevated transient GFP expression (P<0.05), likely by attenuating Agrobacterium-induced cell death stress. Strengths: - First demonstration of cross-kingdom in planta biogenesis of human Bcl-2 3'UTR-derived sRNAs, validated by both custom in silico prediction (34 candidates, 25 confirmed) and NGS with 100% sequence identity filtering. - Multi-endpoint phenotypic characterization (chlorophyll retention, RWC, electrolyte leakage, Evans Blue) with rigorous Latin Square experimental design for water deficit trials, using well-characterized single-copy insertion lines. - Pathway-level transcriptomic analysis linking Bcl-2 3'UTR expression to a mechanistically coherent pro-survival signature (ERQC → UPR → autophagy upregulation, pro-death gene suppression), advancing beyond phenomenological observations in prior studies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Only two single-copy lines (#23 and #26) used for full molecular characterization and NGS, limiting statistical power; line #23 showed weaker or non-significant phenotypes in several assays, raising questions about penetrance and reproducibility. - The causal link between the 197 Bcl-2-derived sRNAs and the stress-tolerance phenotype is not directly established — no target knockdown validation (e.g., RACE, reporter assays, or CRISPR-based target confirmation) was performed, leaving the miRNA-mRNA connection at the level of prediction. - CO2 assimilation was not improved in transgenics under water deficit (both WT and transgenics reduced equally), meaning enhanced membrane integrity and water retention do not translate to maintained photosynthetic productivity — a critical limitation for agricultural relevance. - The study is conducted exclusively in N. benthamiana, a model laboratory species with no direct agricultural value; generalizability to crop plants (wheat, rice, maize) under field conditions remains entirely untested.
Citations
#59 — Innovative (62)

SafeNet: A Neural - Symbolic Network for Safe Planning in Robotic Systems using Formal Method-Guided LLM Fine-Tuning

par.nsf.gov · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: SafeNet scores 62 for its well-motivated integration of Signal Temporal Logic formal verification with LLM fine-tuning via an automated reward machine. The SFT→DPO pipeline with STL-based reward signals is a meaningful contribution, though evaluation is limited to simple simulated environments with no real-hardware demonstration or comparison against formal-method planners.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Robotic systems operating in human environments require safety guarantees that neither traditional control methods nor pure machine learning approaches can reliably provide. Conventional methods (model predictive control, potential fields) struggle with modern robotic complexity and dynamism, while ML approaches lack formal safety guarantees and suffer from data bias and generalization issues. Pre-trained LLMs offer broad reasoning capabilities but lack the domain-specific knowledge required for safety-critical robotic planning. The paper addresses the gap between formal verification rigor and the adaptability of neural networks for trajectory generation and task planning in robotics. Methodology: SafeNet introduces a neural-symbolic architecture operating in the Neuro→Neuro paradigm, where symbolic rules guide neural network learning. The framework has three core components. First, a formal method-based reward machine automatically evaluates whether generated trajectories satisfy Signal Temporal Logic (STL) constraints — for combinatorial (discrete) state spaces it checks constraint adherence directly; for continuous spaces it extends the Argus library with a time-constrained "Finally" operator to handle temporal reach requirements. Rewards decompose along STL "And" sub-constraints: violating any sub-constraint receives a penalty lower bound, while full satisfaction receives an upper-bound reward. Second, prompt optimization using a structured Claude-based prompt generator encodes task descriptions, STL notation, and few-shot examples in HTML-tagged templates. Third, a two-phase fine-tuning pipeline applies Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with LoRA to enforce format compliance and constraint adherence on golden trajectories, followed by Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) using paired safe/unsafe trajectories ranked by the reward machine to align the model toward safety-compliant outputs. Both phases use LoRA (rank 16) applied to all projection layers, with β=0.2 for DPO deviation control. Key Results: On the block manipulation task using Mistral-7B, the pre-trained model achieved only a 1.17% safe rate (6/512 safe trajectories, 475 invalid). After SFT, performance jumped to 88.67% (454/512 safe), and SFT+DPO reached 91.60% (469/512 safe, 29 unsafe, 14 invalid). On path planning (small maps, 5 models), all pre-trained baselines scored between 0.78–7.23% safe rate. SFT improved all models substantially (43.95–72.07%), and SFT+DPO pushed further to 47.46–90.63%, with Mistral-Small achieving the best result. Performance degrades with map complexity: Mistral-7B dropped from 85.74% (small) to 65.2% (medium) to 55.9% (large). LoRA rank sensitivity analysis shows safe rate increasing from 45.61% at rank 4 to 58.40% at rank 32, with diminishing returns beyond rank 8. Strengths: - The automated STL-based reward machine avoids manual trajectory labeling and provides consistent, quantitative training signal — a meaningful engineering contribution over prior work requiring manual state decomposition. - The two-phase SFT→DPO pipeline is well-motivated and the ablation (Fig. 6, Table I) cleanly demonstrates additive benefit of each phase across multiple model families. - The extended "Finally" operator for continuous-space STL with timing constraints is a practical and underappreciated contribution enabling temporally precise path planning beyond binary goal-reaching. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to two relatively simple, simulated environments (block world and grid path planning); there is no demonstration on real robotic hardware or higher-dimensional continuous systems (e.g., 6-DOF manipulators), making deployment readiness unclear. - The Llama-3B model performs poorly even after fine-tuning (47.46% safe rate), yet the paper does not investigate why smaller models fail to benefit as much — the claim that smaller models achieve "competitive performance" is misleading given this outlier. - There is no comparison against baseline approaches such as direct formal-method planners, LLM+STL prompt-only methods, or prior fine-tuning work (e.g., Yang et al. [20]), making it difficult to contextualize the actual advantage of SafeNet beyond an untuned LLM baseline.
Search Results
#60 — Innovative (61)

{AdaCheck}: An Adaptive Checkpointing System for Efficient {LLM} Training with Redundancy Utilization

usenix.org · 2 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The tensor redundancy abstraction is a clean generalization that works across diverse parallelism strategies, and the gradient-based incremental checkpointing insight — exploiting Adam's mathematical structure to reduce checkpoint size by 7× — is technically elegant. The system achieves dramatic checkpoint size reductions (up to 896×) with near-zero throughput overhead. However, it remains primarily a well-engineered systems optimization rather than a conceptual departure, and evaluation at only 128-GPU scale leaves open questions about frontier-scale applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large-scale LLM training runs for weeks or months across thousands of GPUs, with failures occurring frequently — LLaMA 3.1 on 16K GPUs averaged a failure every three hours, wasting roughly 2M GPU hours. Checkpointing is the standard recovery mechanism, but existing systems are designed offline for specific parallelism strategies (e.g., ZeRO, pipeline parallelism) and fail to exploit state redundancy — the fact that many model states are replicated across workers by virtue of the parallelism structure itself. These systems therefore store at least one complete model replica on every checkpoint, missing substantial optimization opportunities. The core challenge is identifying precise state redundancy across diverse and evolving parallelism strategies (including auto-generated irregular ones) and model architectures (dense and sparse MoE). Methodology: AdaCheck introduces the abstraction of tensor redundancy — a per-tensor descriptor of which workers hold replicas — to formally characterize state redundancy across any combination of parallelisms and model architectures. The system operates in two complementary phases. First, offline redundancy utilization analyzes tensor redundancy at the parameter and optimizer state level, computes the intersection of their redundancy types (full, partial, or none), and excludes redundant states from the checkpoint, keeping only the minimal non-redundant set. Second, online redundancy utilization via gradient-based incremental checkpointing stores only the half-precision gradients (size 2M) instead of full model states and optimizer states (size 14M for Adam), exploiting the mathematical relationship between adjacent optimizer states to reconstruct the full state from gradients — reducing per-checkpoint size by up to 1/7 in mixed-precision training. Identifying tensor redundancy at scale is non-trivial: naively comparing tensors across 1000+ workers would require over 30 days. AdaCheck addresses this with a hash-based consistency check (using BLAKE2s) and a ring-based communication algorithm that achieves O(C_n × (M_h + M_c)/2) complexity and completes in under 3 minutes across 128 workers. The system runs the detector for two iterations at startup to filter hash collisions and accidental equality. A failure tolerance factor k allows users to tune how many simultaneous failures AdaCheck must survive, controlling group size for gradient-based incremental checkpointing. Non-blocking full checkpointing via a background thread handles rare catastrophic failures. Key Results: Evaluated on DCN (32×A800 80G GPUs) and CMD (128×3090 24G GPUs) clusters across LLaMA-7B, LLaMA-30B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, GPT-1.4B, GPT-7B, and GPT-MoE, AdaCheck achieves: (1) Checkpoint size reduction of 6–896× compared to CheckFreq across all tested model and parallelism configurations; (2) Checkpoint frequency increase of 1.46–111× compared to CheckFreq and 1.46–3.64× compared to GEMINI, enabling one-step-one-checkpoint (1S1C) in most scenarios; (3) Wasted time per failure reduced by 12.1–88.93× vs. CheckFreq and 1.73–4.51× vs. GEMINI; (4) Near-zero training throughput overhead, as gradient-based incremental checkpointing overlaps with training communication and the non-blocking approach uses separate bandwidth; (5) Reliability analysis shows recovery probability exceeds 90% in worst-case scenarios (k=3, n=32) given that simultaneous failures of 4 workers in 32 are rare events (OPT-175B reports only 1.5% of workers fail within 24 hours). The ablation study confirms that each component — hash-based check, ring communication, offline utilization, and online utilization — contributes substantially, with online utilization achieving a further 7.09× reduction over offline alone. Strengths: - The tensor redundancy abstraction cleanly generalizes across an unusually wide range of parallelism strategies — including irregular auto-generated parallelisms from tools like nnScaler — and both dense and sparse MoE architectures, making the approach broadly applicable without system-specific engineering. - The redundancy detector design is practically efficient: completing in under 3 minutes for 128 workers (vs. 30+ minutes for naive approaches) through the combination of hashing, communication group scoping, and ring-based pipelining. - The gradient-based incremental checkpointing insight is technically elegant — exploiting the mathematical structure of Adam optimizer updates to reduce checkpoint size by 1/7 without any loss of recovery fidelity, while the remote CPU optimizer backup makes recovery overhead comparable to full checkpoint loading. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The gradient-based incremental checkpointing has meaningful reliability constraints: recovery probability drops below 90% for worst-case simultaneous failures (k=3, n=32 with 4 simultaneous failures), requiring users to carefully choose the failure tolerance factor k and periodically take non-blocking full checkpoints as a safety net — adding operational complexity. - Evaluation is limited to relatively small cluster scales (up to 128 GPUs), and it remains unclear how the redundancy detector's 3-minute initialization overhead scales to training runs at 1K–16K GPU scale, where communication group topology may be significantly more complex and variable.
Citations
#61 — Innovative (61)

Analyzing the Effects of a Human-in-the-Loop Candidate Recommendation Algorithm at Jobindex

dl.acm.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The 17-month longitudinal deployment study across 41,390 real recruiting sessions is a rare and valuable contribution that goes well beyond typical offline evaluations, providing compelling evidence of HITL recommender adoption dynamics in practice. The identification of three recruiter adoption profiles and the analysis of design interventions (term highlighting, UI pop-ups) that drove engagement offer actionable insights. The SPBERT architecture itself is a straightforward BERT fine-tuning approach, limiting the methodological novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Recruiting — matching CVs to job postings — is a complex, time-consuming information-seeking task. While many algorithmic approaches to talent search exist, they nearly universally treat it as a pure machine learning problem, ignoring how such systems fit into real recruiter workflows and whether human professionals actually adopt and benefit from them. This paper fills that gap by chronicling the full lifecycle of a candidate recommender deployed at Jobindex, one of Scandinavia's largest job portals, and conducting the first large-scale longitudinal analysis of human-in-the-loop (HITL) adoption in algorithmic recruiting. Methodology: The core algorithm, SPBERT (Sentence-Pair BERT), treats job-resume matching as a sentence-pair classification task. A job posting and resume are concatenated with special [JOB]/[CV] segment tokens, tokenized to 512 tokens, passed through fine-tuned BERT, and scored via an MLP classifier. The full BERT model is fine-tuned end-to-end on 2.4M historical job-resume pairs (138K job postings, 120K CVs) using binary cross-entropy. The system is integrated into the recruiter workflow by replacing the default broad query (Query 0) with a ranked recommendation list shown before manual search begins, preserving recruiter decision autonomy. A skill and ESCO-entity highlighting module (Danish BERT NER + classification) was added after A/B tests revealed a ~35% engagement drop without term highlighting. SPBERT was evaluated offline against SBERT, JTIH, PJFNN, and BPJFNN, and validated via a 25-day online A/B test before production deployment. The adoption analysis covers 17 months (April 2023–September 2024) across 41,390 jobs, classifying each as CHECK (recruiter shortlisted ≥1 recommended CV) or SKIP, and examining outcomes via positive response rate (PRR), number of contacted candidates, and query effort. Key Results: Offline, SPBERT dramatically outperforms all baselines: AUC 0.946 vs. 0.703 for the next-best (SBERT), and NDCG@20 of 0.186 vs. 0.017 for SBERT (p < 0.001). In the online A/B test against JTIH, SPBERT achieves a 13.5% vs. 12.7% positive response rate (+10.4%) and a 41.1% vs. 24.75% candidate-from-recommendations rate (+66%). Over 17 months of deployment, recruiters who engaged with recommendations (CHECK jobs, 41.5% of all jobs) outperformed those who skipped on every metric: PRR 15.78% vs. 13.99% (+12.7%), contacted candidates 27.18 vs. 23.44 (+15.9%), positively responding candidates 4.22 vs. 3.26 (+29.4%), and 22.7% fewer queries submitted — all statistically significant (p < 0.001). Candidates selected from recommendations showed consistently higher PRR than those from manual queries across all 17 months. Benefits hold for both complex and standard jobs, and for 35 of 37 industry sectors. A multiple-regression model confirms engagement with recommendations as a significant predictor of PRR (β = 0.0123, p < .001), though the overall model explains only 6% of variance (R² = 0.059). Recruiter clustering reveals three adoption profiles: early adopters, forced adopters (responded only to a UI pop-up intervention added in October 2023), and persistent non-adopters. Strengths: - Rare 17-month longitudinal deployment study on 41,390 real recruiting sessions provides compelling evidence of real-world HITL recommender value, going far beyond offline benchmark evaluations. - SPBERT achieves substantially superior offline performance (AUC 0.946 vs. 0.703 for SBERT), and the sentence-pair architecture elegantly handles vocabulary mismatch between job postings and resumes without requiring pre-structured input fields. - The paper rigorously examines adoption barriers — algorithm aversion, fear of job loss, explainability gaps, time pressure — and connects them to concrete system design interventions (term highlighting, UI pop-up reminders, recruiter workshops). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The binary CHECK/SKIP classification of recruiter engagement is admittedly coarse; a recruiter who views 6 CVs and shortlists none counts the same as one who actively uses the list, potentially conflating meaningfully different behaviors. - The observational study design cannot rule out self-selection confounding: recruiters who choose to engage with recommendations may systematically differ from those who skip them in ways that independently drive better outcomes, despite the paper's attempts to control for seniority and activity level. - The regression model explains only 6% of PRR variance, and the proxy metric (candidate positive response rate) captures candidate interest rather than actual hiring outcomes, limiting the strength of claims about true recruitment success.

#62 — Innovative (61)

Graph-of-Mark: Promote Spatial Reasoning in Multimodal Language Models with Graph-Based Visual Prompting

Giacomo Frisoni, Lorenzo Molfetta, Mattia Buzzoni, Gianluca Moro · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Graph-of-Mark is a natural and well-executed extension of Set-of-Mark that addresses a genuine gap — the lack of relational structure in visual prompting. The training-free, plug-and-play design and consistent improvements across four datasets and three architectures demonstrate practical value. However, the conceptual step from overlaying numbered masks to overlaying a scene graph is relatively incremental, and the reliance on heuristic geometry thresholds and monocular depth for relation estimation introduces fragility.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal language models (MLMs) persistently struggle with spatial reasoning — distinguishing concepts like left, above, in front of, and behind in visual scenes. Existing pixel-level visual prompting methods, most notably Set-of-Mark (SoM), improve grounding by overlaying numbered region masks on images, but treat detected objects as isolated entities. This leaves the relational structure governing scene geometry entirely unexploited, since no explicit encoding of how objects relate to one another is passed to the model. The gap is significant: even state-of-the-art closed-source models frequently hallucinate spatial relationships, reducing reliability in applications from robotic navigation to medical image interpretation. Methodology: Graph-of-Mark (GoM) is a training-free, plug-and-play pixel-level visual prompting technique that constructs a scene graph (SG) over the input image and renders it directly as a visual overlay before feeding the augmented image to any frozen MLM. The pipeline has five stages: (1) Object detection and segmentation — an ensemble of three complementary detectors (OWL-V2, YOLOv8-X, Mask R-CNN) fused via Weighted Boxes Fusion, then refined to pixel masks via SAM-HQ; (2) Spatial relation estimation — pairwise directed edges computed across three ontological categories: directional (above, below, left_of, right_of), depth-based (in_front_of, behind) using MiDaS monocular depth, and proximity-based (near, close, very_close, touching); (3) Query-based graph filtering — objects and relations irrelevant to the task prompt T are pruned via cascaded lexical matching and FastText cosine similarity, retaining only the top-k relations per object; (4) Scene graph rendering — nodes drawn as class-colored segmentation masks with numeric or textual IDs, edges rendered as directed arrows with relation labels, with a collision-resolving mark allocation strategy to prevent visual occlusion; (5) Prompting — the augmented image I^SG is passed to the MLM either alone (Visual SG) or paired with a verbalized triplet representation of the graph (Visual + Textual SG). No model weights are modified at any stage. Key Results: GoM is evaluated on GQA, VQAv1, VQAv2, and RefCOCOg across three open-source MLMs: Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Gemma-3-4B, and LlamaV-o1-11B. GoM consistently outperforms raw image and SoM baselines across all three models and all four datasets. On Qwen2.5-VL-7B, the best GoM variant achieves 63.8% on GQA (+2.2 pp over SoM), 82.5% on VQAv1 (+17.3 pp over SoM), 80.5% on VQAv2 (+8.3 pp over SoM), and 57.4% on RefCOCOg (+1.9 pp over SoM). LlamaV-o1-11B shows the largest absolute gains on VQA tasks, reaching 83.4% on VQAv1. The visual SG representation consistently outperforms textual-only SG encodings by up to +10 percentage points, demonstrating that pixel-level graph rendering provides signal that verbalized triplets cannot replace. Optimal graph density is 3–10 objects and 4–16 relations; beyond this, surplus annotations introduce noise. GoM adds an average latency of 1.13 seconds per image versus 0.77 s for segmentation-only and 0.92 s for SoM, a modest overhead attributable primarily to relation estimation. Strengths: - Addresses a real and persistent failure mode of MLMs (spatial reasoning) with a clean, training-free approach that is immediately deployable on any frozen vision-language model without architectural changes - Rigorous empirical evaluation across 3 architecturally diverse open-source MLMs, 4 public datasets, and 27 decoding seed/temperature/top-p combinations, with ablations isolating contributions of edge labels, ID type (numeric vs. textual), and visual vs. textual SG modality - Practical efficiency: the overhead is small (≈0.2–0.4 s over SoM) and the code, preprocessed datasets, and evaluation scripts are released under MIT license, satisfying the software imperative for reproducible research Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to open-source models with ≤11B parameters; performance on larger or closed-source models (GPT-4V, Gemini) is not reported, leaving open the question of whether GoM adds value when the base model is already stronger at spatial reasoning - The relation estimation pipeline relies on monocular depth (MiDaS) and heuristic geometry thresholds, which can fail on cluttered scenes, unusual viewpoints, or objects without clear bounding box separation — errors here propagate to incorrect relational annotations and can degrade rather than improve model outputs, as evidenced by occasional REC failures when textual labels add noise

#63 — Innovative (61)

ReTabSyn: Realistic Tabular Data Synthesis via Reinforcement Learning

Xiaofeng Lin, Seungbae Kim, Zhuoya Li, Zachary DeSoto, Charles Fleming, Guang Cheng · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: ReTabSyn's oracle-free DPO fine-tuning for tabular synthesis is a well-motivated application that directly targets the utility gap decomposition, and the perturbation-based preference pair construction is a clever schema-native alternative to classifier-guided RL. The breadth of evaluation across three challenging regimes is commendable. The contribution is meaningful but builds heavily on established DPO and GREAT frameworks rather than introducing fundamentally new ideas.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard deep generative models (DGMs) for tabular data optimize the full joint distribution P(X, y), which spreads limited statistical budget equally across all feature marginals and the decision boundary. In low-data, class-imbalanced, or distribution-shifted regimes, this leads to synthetic data that hallucinates unrealistic feature co-occurrences and degrades downstream ML utility. The authors ground this observation in a formal utility decomposition theorem (Xu et al., 2023) showing that the utility gap is bounded by two terms: feature mismatch and regression mismatch (i.e., error in the conditional P(y|X)). This motivates prioritizing the conditional distribution over generic joint fidelity — a gap that existing methods, including RL-guided approaches like PTA and SynRL that rely on external oracle classifiers, do not cleanly address. Methodology: ReTabSyn (Reinforced Tabular Synthesis) fine-tunes a pre-trained LM-based tabular generator (GREAT/GPT-2 backbone) using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) without any external reward model or human labels. The pipeline has two stages: (1) supervised pre-training on SMOTE-augmented tabular data serialized as natural language strings, with adaptive augmentation ratios M(N) ranging from 30x for N≤128 to 1x for N>1000; (2) DPO fine-tuning on oracle-free preference pairs. Preference pairs (chosen C, rejected R) are constructed via two tabular-native perturbation strategies: Target Perturbation — keeping features X fixed and replacing label y with a marginal sample ỹ, directly targeting the regression mismatch term; and Feature Perturbation — perturbing one feature in a strongly correlated pair (identified by Pearson's r or Cramér's V) while keeping its correlated partner fixed, penalizing unrealistic feature co-occurrences. A 70/30 mix of target vs. feature perturbations is used. The DPO-Positive objective enlarges the log-likelihood margin between chosen and rejected completions while a regularization term (weight λ=0.1) prevents drifting from the pre-trained feature manifold. The entire preference construction is schema-validated and label-free, eliminating oracle classifier bias. Key Results: Evaluated across 10 benchmark tabular classification datasets under three challenging regimes — small data (N∈{32,64,128,256,512}), imbalanced targets (1% minority prevalence), and demographic distribution shift — ReTabSyn consistently outperforms all baselines including GReaT, TVAE, TabSyn, SMOTE, and the classifier-guided RL methods PTA and SynRL. In the small data regime (32–128 rows), ReTabSyn achieves the highest synthetic AUROC across datasets and in augmentation settings outperforms real-data baselines at very low N. On imbalanced data (PR-AUC at 1% prevalence), it reaches 0.906 on Adult vs. 0.891 for the next-best baseline (SMOTE). Under distribution shift, ReTabSyn leads by 1–4 AUC points over all synthetic baselines on Adult, Churn, Liver, and Titanic. On statistical fidelity, ReTabSyn achieves the highest or tied-highest β-Recall (0.33), Shape (0.83), and Corr. (0.83) among all generators. Privacy analysis using 13 membership inference attacks shows ReTabSyn leakage (AUC≈0.62) is substantially lower than interpolation-based SMOTE (0.83) and comparable to deep generator baselines. On domain constraint satisfaction (Adult), ReTabSyn achieves 0.00% violation rate vs. 1–3% for all other deep generators. Ablations confirm both training stages are necessary and that oracle-based preference labeling underperforms the schema-native perturbation approach. Strengths: - Theoretically grounded: the method directly minimizes the utility gap decomposition rather than heuristically applying RL signals, providing a principled justification for targeting P(y|X) - Oracle-free and label-free design eliminates guidance-model bias, reduces compute cost, and avoids privacy leakage from external classifiers — a genuine practical advantage over PTA/SynRL - Strong empirical breadth: 10 datasets, 3 evaluation regimes, 10 random seeds, comprehensive fidelity + privacy + utility metrics, and thorough ablations covering training stages, perturbation types, augmentation scale, and DPO budget allocation Weaknesses / Limitations: - The base generator is exclusively GPT-2/GREAT (autoregressive LM); generalization to diffusion-based or GAN/VAE backbones is acknowledged as future work, limiting conclusions about architectural independence - Regression task performance lags SMOTE on two of three regression datasets (Abalone, Shoppers), suggesting the conditional alignment strategy is less effective when the decision boundary is continuous and noisy — a non-trivial limitation given many real-world tabular tasks are regression - The DPO fine-tuning and augmentation pipeline adds meaningful implementation complexity and an additional ~3 minutes of fine-tuning per dataset (on an H100 GPU), which may be non-trivial for practitioners without access to high-memory accelerators despite the lightweight framing
