#64 — Innovative (61)

ChartEditBench: Evaluating Grounded Multi-Turn Chart Editing in Multimodal Language Models

Manav Nitin Kapadnis, Lawanya Baghel, Atharva Naik, Carolyn Rosé · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The multi-turn stateful evaluation protocol revealing 20-33% performance degradation across sequential edits is a meaningful methodological contribution that exposes failure modes invisible to single-turn benchmarks. The hybrid evaluation combining programmatic assertions with visual difference enumeration is more principled than generic LLM-as-a-judge approaches. The restriction to synthetic matplotlib-only tasks and dependence on an unpublished Chart-R1 model limit both ecological validity and reproducibility.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing benchmarks for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) focus on single-turn chart generation or question answering — tasks where models either synthesize a chart from scratch or answer a factual query about a static image. This framing misses how real-world visualization workflows actually operate: analysts and developers iteratively refine existing chart specifications across multiple turns, maintaining shared context, tracking prior edits, and adapting to evolving requirements. No benchmark existed to measure this incremental, stateful editing capability. Additionally, standard evaluation approaches (LLM-as-a-judge, CLIP-based embeddings) suffer from narrow score distributions, high variance, and opacity about which aspects of an output succeeded or failed — making them ill-suited for diagnosing multi-turn failure modes. Methodology: The authors introduce ChartEditBench, a synthetic benchmark with 4,142 instances (20,710 total modification samples) spanning 37 chart types and 35 modification categories across two tasks: (1) Code-to-Code Chart Modification, where given original code and a target image the model must produce modified code, and (2) Natural Language Delta Modification, where the model receives a natural language instruction instead. Each instance undergoes N=5 sequential turns of progressively harder edits — from simple styling changes (colors, titles) at turn 1 to complex transformations (chart type conversion, dual axes) at turns 4–5. A fully automated three-stage pipeline generates charts, modification chains, and VQA pairs using LLMs against 37 matplotlib chart types, with a UniformDistributionManager ensuring balanced coverage. A 430-instance subset (2,400 turns) was human-verified by two expert annotators. Crucially, the benchmark uses a conversational evaluation protocol where each turn receives the model's own previous prediction as input, creating error-accumulation chains. Evaluation employs a hybrid composite metric combining: execution-based rendering success, programmatic assertion pass rates for instruction following and code quality, and a visually grounded similarity score where Chart-R1 (a fine-tuned VLM) enumerates explicit visual differences between predicted and ground-truth charts. Six models are evaluated: GPT-5-mini, Claude Haiku 4.5, Qwen3-VL (2B/8B/30B-A3B), and InternVL3-1B. Key Results: Claude Haiku 4.5 achieves the highest overall score (2.187/5.0) with particularly strong visual similarity (1.937). Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B is competitive (2.139 overall) using only 3B active parameters via MoE. GPT-5-mini leads on instruction following (1.623) but lags on visual similarity (1.521). InternVL3-1B scores only 1.345 overall with instruction following of 0.480. All models exhibit monotonic performance degradation across turns: Claude Haiku 4.5 drops from 2.46 to 1.97 (20% decline) while InternVL3-1B drops from 1.62 to 1.09 (33%). Visual similarity degrades most steeply (~35% average across models), while code quality drops only ~8% and instruction following remains roughly stable across turns — indicating accumulated errors corrupt visual output more than code structure. Style modifications (axis_style, series_label, axis_limit) yield scores of 2.2–2.8 for top models, while data-centric modifications (rolling_average, data_transformation) drop to 1.3–1.5. Rolling average failures exceed 80% low-score rates across all models except Claude Haiku 4.5 (58%). Proprietary models maintain 85–90% rendering success vs. 46–90% for open-source models. Strengths: - The multi-turn stateful evaluation protocol is a genuine methodological advance over one-shot benchmarks, revealing capability gaps (20–33% degradation) that single-turn evaluation completely masks. - The hybrid evaluation framework combining programmatic assertions, execution checks, and LLM-based visual difference enumeration is more interpretable and reliable than generic LLM-as-a-judge or CLIP-based metrics, with a principled separation between programmatic and LLM-judged instructions. - The dataset construction pipeline is rigorous: four-step code validation (AST parsing, sandboxed execution, render validation, structure validation), balanced modification type distribution via round-robin, and human verification with inter-annotator agreement via Cohen's κ. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is entirely synthetic — all charts and modification chains are LLM-generated, which may introduce systematic biases from the generating model's preferences and could fail to capture the diversity and messiness of real analyst workflows. - The evaluation relies on Chart-R1 (an anonymous, unpublished model) as the primary visual similarity judge, creating a dependency on an external artifact that cannot be independently verified or reproduced by the community. - The task formulation is restricted to matplotlib/Python code editing; it does not generalize to other visualization toolchains (Vega-Lite, D3, Plotly) that are equally common in practice, limiting ecological validity.

#65 — Moderately Innovative (58)

CacheSolidarity: Preventing Prefix Caching Side Channels in Multi-tenant LLM Serving Systems

Panagiotis Georgios Pennas, Konstantinos Papaioannou, Marco Guarnieri, Thaleia Dimitra Doudali · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: CacheSolidarity addresses a real and timely security concern in multi-tenant LLM serving with a well-engineered selective isolation mechanism. The KDE-based conditional activation is a sensible design that avoids the over-protection of blunt isolation, and the negligible overhead is impressive. However, the core idea—isolating suspicious cache entries rather than entire users—is a relatively standard refinement of access-control granularity, and the acknowledged gaps (first-entry vulnerability, manipulable activation threshold) limit the security contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Automatic Prefix Caching (APC) is a widely deployed optimization in LLM serving systems (OpenAI, DeepSeek, Google Gemini, vLLM) that reuses previously computed KV tensors for shared prompt prefixes. While APC dramatically reduces latency for repeated or multi-turn queries, it introduces a timing side channel: cache hits are faster than misses, and this latency difference—observable via time-to-first-token (TTFT)—leaks whether another user's prompt shares a prefix with the current request. In multi-tenant deployments, adversaries can exploit this iteratively to reconstruct sensitive prompt content (names, medical conditions, private identifiers) word-by-word through "prompt-stealing attacks." The paper demonstrates through empirical TTFT analysis across 9 LLMs and 5 load levels that the channel's exploitability is conditional: it depends on prefix length, model size, and system load (RPS). Existing defenses—full user-level cache isolation, timing obfuscation, and LLM-based semantic classification—all introduce unnecessary overhead for scenarios where the side channel is not actually exploitable, penalizing benign users. Methodology: CacheSolidarity introduces a lightweight, condition-adaptive defense built on a key insight: it is sufficient to isolate only the specific prefixes that enable attacks, not entire users. The system extends vLLM's KV cache with two metadata fields per entry: OwnerID (immutable, set at cache allocation) and AttackFlag (a marker set when cross-user reuse is detected). Three components work together: (1) the KV Cache Extension tracks ownership and flags suspicious entries with only 32 bytes of overhead per entry; (2) the Detector runs per-request on cache hits—if a hit belongs to a different user's unflagged entry, it flags that entry and isolates subsequent reuse; if a hit belongs to a flagged entry owned by another user, reuse is blocked and a new cache path is created ("partial hit"); (3) the Activator uses Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) overlap between observed TTFT distributions of hits and misses over a sliding window to dynamically enable or disable selective isolation only when the timing side channel is statistically exploitable. When KDE overlap exceeds a configurable threshold θ (hits and misses are indistinguishable), the detector is deactivated and full prefix sharing resumes. This cooperative-efficiency design allows benign shared prefixes to remain accessible while blocking adversarial reuse paths. CacheSolidarity is implemented on top of vLLM 0.8.5 and will be open-sourced. Key Results: Evaluation across 5 multi-user workloads (10 users × 100 requests = 1,000 requests each) and 9 LLMs (0.5B–13B parameters) on an NVIDIA A100 40GB shows: (1) CacheSolidarity achieves up to 70% higher cache hit rate and 30% lower TTFT compared to User Cache Isolation (the strongest security baseline), while staying within 5–10% of unprotected Prefix Caching performance. (2) Across all 9 LLMs, CacheSolidarity closes the performance gap to within 6% of Prefix Caching while significantly outperforming User Cache Isolation. (3) In an attacker-perceived performance experiment with a 20-probe reconstruction attack, the unprotected system shows a clear TTFT and hit-rate spike at the 9th request (the correct secret guess), which the attacker can trivially identify; CacheSolidarity eliminates this spike entirely—hit rate and TTFT are uniform across all 20 attacker requests, empirically validating that the timing leak is closed. (4) System overhead is negligible: 0.004 ms per request for the Detector, 0.003 ms for the Activator, and 32 bytes per cache entry for metadata. Strengths: - The conditional activation via KDE overlap is well-motivated by a thorough empirical characterization of when the side channel is actually exploitable—avoiding the over-protection that plagues prior work. - The selective prefix isolation mechanism is semantically clean: it isolates paths of flagged prefixes rather than users, preserving intra-user reuse even past flagged entries, which is a meaningful improvement over blunt user isolation. - Overhead is genuinely negligible (sub-millisecond per request, 32 bytes per cache entry), and the implementation on production vLLM with open-source commitment enhances credibility and deployability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The security guarantees have two explicit gaps: CacheSolidarity does not protect against attacks targeting the very first prefix entry (no parent to flag), and it does not protect against an attacker who correctly guesses the secret on the first attempt—both acknowledged but potentially meaningful in low-entropy secret spaces. - The KDE threshold θ is left to the system administrator with limited guidance on how to set it safely; an adversary who can manipulate traffic patterns (burst requests to inflate KDE overlap) can potentially force the Activator to deactivate protection, though the paper argues sliding-window aggregation mitigates this. - Evaluation is limited to a single hardware platform (A100) with synthetic workloads derived from ShareGPT; real-world deployments with heterogeneous hardware, mixed model serving, or disaggregated prefill/decode architectures may exhibit different TTFT dynamics that could affect the KDE-based activation logic.
Search Results 2603.10726 PDF
#66 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Robust Deep Knowledge Tracing with Out-of-Distribution Detection

mdpi.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: EB-OOD DKT presents a clean integration of energy-based OOD detection into the knowledge tracing pipeline, which is a sensible and previously unaddressed combination. However, the core techniques—energy-based OOD scoring and InfoNCE contrastive learning—are well-established in the broader ML literature, and the transfer to KT, while useful, is methodologically straightforward. The artificial OOD construction using cross-skill negatives rather than genuine distribution shifts weakens the practical claims considerably.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep Knowledge Tracing (DKT) models are highly sensitive to out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs arising from curriculum changes, new assessment formats, or atypical student behaviors. Existing DKT approaches—including attention-based models like SAKT—silently produce high-confidence predictions on OOD sequences, degrading reliability in adaptive learning systems. No prior framework integrates OOD detection directly into the KT pipeline, leaving deployed systems unable to flag when their predictions are untrustworthy. Methodology: The paper proposes EB-OOD DKT, which grafts energy-based OOD detection onto a Transformer-based DKT backbone. Student interactions are encoded via four features—Skill ID (embedding), Response Correctness (binary), normalized Time Interval (min-max scaled), and log-transformed Cumulative Attempts—fused into a single embedding vector. A Transformer encoder produces hidden states from which two parallel heads operate: a prediction head outputs next-step correctness probability via sigmoid, and an energy head computes E(x) = −T·log∑exp(z_i/T) over output logits. At inference, normalized energy Ê(x) = (E(x)−μ_E)/σ_E is compared to a threshold τ (calibrated on validation data via grid search on AUROC); sequences exceeding τ are flagged as OOD. Training uses a joint loss L_total = L_BCE + λ·L_contrastive, where the InfoNCE contrastive loss treats successive same-skill interactions as positive pairs and cross-skill pairs as negatives, pulling ID embeddings together and pushing OOD embeddings apart in latent space. This dual-objective design is parameter-free at inference (energy requires only a log-sum-exp over existing logits, adding O(1) overhead) and requires no separate OOD model. Key Results: On in-distribution EdNet-KT1 (1M+ interactions), EB-OOD DKT achieves Accuracy 0.762 and AUC 0.847, versus SAKT's 0.741/0.824 and DKT's 0.734/0.812—consistent gains of ~2 accuracy points and ~2.3 AUC points. On OOD detection across four held-out datasets (ASSIST2009, ASSIST2015, Algebra 2005–2006, Khan Academy), the model achieves AUROC of 0.812–0.826 and FPR@95 of 0.26–0.29, compared to SAKT's best of 0.743/0.42 on ASSIST2015—a 0.049 AUROC improvement and a 0.13 FPR@95 reduction. Calibration ECE improves substantially: 0.064 versus SAKT's 0.103. The ablation study (Table 6) confirms each component is load-bearing: removing contrastive loss drops AUROC from 0.94 to 0.88; removing temporal/behavioral features drops it to 0.85; removing energy normalization drops it to 0.89. Mean energy separation between ID and OOD samples ranges from 0.43 (ASSIST2015) to 0.67 (Algebra 2005–2006), with t-SNE visualizations confirming clear geometric separation in latent space. Strengths: - Clean unification of OOD detection and performance prediction in a single forward pass with negligible inference overhead (O(T·d²), identical to baseline SAKT) - Thorough empirical evaluation across five datasets spanning meaningfully different domain shifts (single-skill algebra vs. multi-subject K-12 vs. multi-skill Khan Academy), with ablation, calibration, threshold sensitivity, and energy distribution analyses all reported - The contrastive InfoNCE objective is well-motivated for the KT setting: same-skill consecutive interactions are semantically coherent positive pairs, while cross-skill pairs are natural negatives without requiring external OOD data during training Weaknesses / Limitations: - The OOD construction is artificial: negative contrastive pairs are randomly sampled cross-skill interactions from the training set, not true distribution-shifted sequences. This may overstate real-world OOD detection capability when the shift is subtle (e.g., curriculum drift within the same skill domain) rather than a wholesale platform change. - The static threshold τ requires target-domain validation data for calibration and will degrade under gradual distributional drift; the paper acknowledges but does not address continual/adaptive recalibration, which is arguably the most practically important scenario for deployed educational platforms. - Evaluation is limited to mathematics-focused, discrete-response datasets; generalization to open-ended tasks, natural language responses, or cross-lingual settings is unexamined and left as future work.
Search Results
#67 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Spherical double k-means: a co-clustering approach for textual data analysis

art.torvergata.it · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Integrates cosine similarity into the double k-means co-clustering framework with a proven monotone convergence guarantee, addressing a genuine geometric mismatch in text clustering. The contribution is principled and the shared centroid matrix coupling rows and columns is well-motivated, but the core components (spherical k-means, co-clustering, cosine similarity) are all well-established, making this a competent synthesis rather than a conceptual advance. Experiments on real corpora show interpretable results but only marginal differentiation from existing methods like vMF mixtures.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Text clustering of high-dimensional, sparse term-document matrices (typically TF-IDF weighted) is challenged by the curse of dimensionality, variable document lengths, and pervasive sparsity. Standard k-means using Euclidean distance overemphasizes document magnitude rather than thematic content, tends to produce one or two dominant clusters, and conflates co-occurring terms from different topics. Researchers often want to cluster both documents and terms simultaneously to enable richer topic modeling, keyword extraction, and discovery of latent thematic structure—yet existing methods either cluster only one dimension (spherical k-means, SKM) or use Euclidean geometry (double k-means, DKM). The paper fills this gap by introducing Spherical Double K-Means (SDKM), which integrates cosine similarity into a co-clustering framework. Methodology: SDKM simultaneously partitions the N terms (rows) of a term-document matrix X into K clusters and the J documents (columns) into Q clusters, producing membership matrices U (N×K) and V (J×Q) and a shared centroid matrix Ȳ (K×Q). The objective is to maximize tr(X'UȲV'), normalized to lie in [0,1], which is the cosine-similarity analog of the DKM least-squares objective. Block-coordinate ascent alternates between (i) assigning each term i to the cluster k maximizing cosine similarity between xᵢ and the row-cluster centroid cₖ = Ȳₖ·V', and (ii) assigning each document j to the cluster q maximizing cosine similarity between x·ⱼ and the column-cluster centroid dq = UȲq. The centroid matrix Ȳ is updated in closed form and normalized by rows only (not columns), a deliberate choice that avoids over-constraining the optimization while empirically keeping column norms near 1. The algorithm is guaranteed to monotonically non-decrease the objective at each step (proved in Appendix 1) and converges to a local maximum; 20 random starts are recommended based on simulation evidence that this eliminates local-maxima trapping even at high noise. Optimal (K, Q) is selected via a spherical adaptation of the pseudo-F index. MATLAB implementations run 100 simulation runs on an Apple M1 in ~87 seconds total, indicating sub-second per-run cost. Key Results: Simulation studies with known ground truth (K=4, Q=3) show 100% correct cluster recovery at low noise (ε=0.1), declining to 13% at ε=0.5 and 0% at ε=0.9—consistent with the NP-hard nature of the problem. At ε=0.1 both U and V are perfectly recovered (ARI=1); ARI degrades gracefully to ~0.35 (U) and ~0.35 (V) at ε=2.0. Centroid recovery (NRMSE) remains better than partition recovery at high noise. Comparison with vMF mixtures on the same simulated data shows near-identical row (U) recovery, but SDKM matches or exceeds vMF on column (V) recovery for ε≤1.35 (mean ARI_V: 0.933 vs. 0.951 at ε=0.90; 0.840 vs. 0.911 at ε=1.10), with vMF pulling ahead only at very high noise (ε≥1.50). On the U.S. presidential inaugural addresses corpus (59 documents, 1206 terms after TF-IDF preprocessing), SDKM with K=3, Q=2 identifies two historically coherent document clusters (pre-/post-Civil War) and three interpretable word clusters labeled "American Dream," "Law and Order," and "Politics, Economics, and Secession." Compared to DKM on the same data, the only document-level disagreement is Lincoln's 1865 address—assigned to the older cluster by DKM (magnitude-sensitive) and to the modern cluster by SDKM (angle-sensitive, correctly capturing its reconciliation rhetoric). Word-cluster ARI between DKM and SDKM is 0.225, reflecting finer-grained proportional distinctions captured by cosine. On the 20 Newsgroups corpus (1278 terms × 2834 documents after stratified sampling), SDKM with K=3, Q=2 cleanly recovers religion/politics/sports versus computing/classifieds document groups, with interpretable specialized vocabulary clusters. Strengths: - Principled integration of cosine similarity into a co-clustering framework with a proven monotone convergence guarantee, directly addressing the geometric mismatch that plagues Euclidean-based methods on sparse TF-IDF data. - Joint clustering of terms and documents via a shared centroid matrix Ȳ couples the two spaces, meaning column (document) cluster centroids exploit row (term) cluster structure and vice versa—a coherent bilinear factorization rather than two independent clusterings. - Computationally efficient matrix-formulation implementation: 100 simulation runs on an Apple M1 in ~87 seconds total, substantially faster than standard SKM implementations, with empirically near-linear scaling in Q and sub-linear in K for sparse matrices. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Hard (crisp) cluster assignments cannot represent documents or terms that legitimately belong to multiple topics; a fuzzy or mixture-model extension (acknowledged by the authors) would be more realistic for real corpora where thematic overlap is the norm rather than the exception. - The pseudo-F cluster selection criterion systematically underestimates the true cluster count as noise increases (e.g., 0% correct identification at ε=0.5), requiring analysts to manually inspect multiple (K,Q) candidates and rely on interpretability heuristics rather than a fully automated selection rule. - The normalization analysis is incomplete: only rows of Ȳ are explicitly normalized, and while column norms empirically stay near 1 in the tested corpora, no theoretical bound is provided. The robustness of this approximation for corpora with very different document-length distributions or extreme sparsity remains uncharacterized.
Search Results
#68 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Sparsity Induction for Accurate Post-Training Pruning of Large Language Models

Minhao Jiang, Zhikai Li, Xuewen Liu, Jing Zhang, Mengjuan Chen, Qingyi Gu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The insight that distributional disruption rather than importance scoring is the binding constraint in post-training pruning is conceptually appealing, and the mathematically equivalent reparameterization that adds zero inference overhead is a clean design. The Fast Hessian Update (22.65× speedup) is a useful engineering contribution. However, gains on strong baselines like Wanda and SparseGPT are marginal at practical sparsity levels, with occasional regressions on zero-shot accuracy, and evaluation is limited to older architectures at ≤13B scale.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Post-training sparsity (PTS) — pruning LLM weights without full retraining — is attractive for reducing inference cost, but existing methods focus almost exclusively on designing better importance scores (magnitude, Hessian-based sensitivity, activation norms). The paper argues this direction has hit diminishing returns: the real problem is that dense LLM weight matrices are not inherently sparse, and pruning disrupts the distributional and channel-scale structure established during training. This imbalance propagates through attention and FFN paths and is the primary driver of accuracy loss, not inadequate importance scoring. Methodology: The authors propose Sparsity Induction (SI), a pre-pruning preparation stage that reshapes weight distributions and feature structure to be more sparsity-friendly before any mask is applied. SI operates along two complementary axes. At the distributional level, it introduces per-channel scaling (s) and shifting (δ) as mathematically equivalent reparameterizations of linear layers and attention projections. These transformations enlarge the gap between important and unimportant weights without altering model functionality — the scale factors are folded back into the weight matrices after training, adding zero inference overhead or extra parameters. The optimization minimizes expected output distortion under the target mask over a small calibration set (128 samples), updating only the lightweight transformation parameters Θ = {s, δ, s_a}. At the feature level, SI adds a Spectral Norm Loss regularizer (an exponential of the p-norm of layer weights across the sparse set) to encourage low-rank feature structure and promote feature sparsity. A robust channel-wise initialization derived from median/mean output statistics stabilizes convergence. A Fast Hessian Update avoids repeated forward passes by using a diagonal Gauss–Newton proxy (ΣX = E[XX⊤]) that transforms under distributional scaling as diag(H') = s² ∘ diag(H), enabling O(d_in) metric refresh from cached diagonals. SI is designed as a plug-and-play wrapper compatible with any existing PTS method (Magnitude, Wanda, SparseGPT) and runs for only 1–5 epochs on calibration data. Key Results: Experiments cover OPT-125M/350M/1.3B/2.7B and LLaMA-1/2 at 7B and 13B, evaluated on WikiText-2/C4 perplexity and six zero-shot benchmarks (ARC-E/C, HellaSwag, BoolQ, Winogrande, OpenBookQA). At 50% unstructured sparsity on LLaMA-1-7B, SI+Wanda reduces perplexity from 7.26 to 7.04 and SI+SparseGPT from 7.17 to 7.13. Gains are substantially larger for magnitude pruning (LLaMA-1-7B: 17.28 → 12.08) and at aggressive structured sparsity (2:4 pattern, OPT-1.3B magnitude: 427.18 → 40.73). Zero-shot accuracy improvements are consistent but modest for stronger baselines — e.g., LLaMA-1-7B at 50% sparsity: Wanda 51.47% → 52.50%, SparseGPT 52.37% → 52.04% (slight regression). The Fast Hessian Update achieves a 22.65× speedup in metric refresh time on LLaMA-7B (345.91s → 15.27s). End-to-end 2:4 inference latency is identical to Wanda (251ms) since SI parameters are fully absorbed. Strengths: - The core insight — that distributional disruption, not importance scoring, is the binding constraint in PTS — is well-motivated and supported by the visualization showing sharpened importance landscapes after SI. - The mathematically equivalent reparameterization is elegant: it adds zero inference overhead, requires no architectural changes, and is compatible with any existing pruning criterion as a drop-in preprocessing step. - The Fast Hessian Update (22.65× speedup) is a practically significant engineering contribution that makes SI tractable at scale without repeated forward passes. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Results on strong baselines (Wanda, SparseGPT) show only marginal improvements at 50% unstructured sparsity, and SI+SparseGPT occasionally regresses slightly on zero-shot accuracy (LLaMA-1-7B: 52.37 → 52.04), undermining the claim of consistent improvement. - Evaluation is limited to models ≤13B parameters (OPT and LLaMA-1/2 generations); no experiments on more recent architectures (Mistral, LLaMA-3, DeepSeek) or models at 70B+ scale where PTS is most practically relevant. - The spectral norm regularizer (R_p) introduces three hyperparameters (λ, α, p) with no systematic ablation showing their individual contribution or sensitivity; it is unclear whether feature-level induction adds meaningful benefit beyond distributional reparameterization alone. - The calibration requirement (1–5 epochs on 128 samples) adds non-trivial wall-clock time not fully accounted for in the efficiency analysis, and the interaction between calibration data distribution and pruning performance is not studied.
Recommended 2602.21652 PDF
#69 — Moderately Innovative (57)

DyLLM: Efficient Diffusion LLM Inference via Saliency-based Token Selection and Partial Attention

Younjoo Lee, Junghoo Lee, Seungkyun Dan, Jaiyoung Park, Jung Ho Ahn · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: DyLLM applies the well-established principle of temporal sparsity—skipping computation for representations that change minimally between steps—to masked diffusion LLMs, achieving impressive speedups (7-9×) with maintained accuracy. The theoretical error bounds under RMSNorm are a nice touch. However, the core idea of caching and selectively recomputing based on similarity is a standard acceleration technique applied to a new setting, and the threshold τ still requires per-model tuning, limiting the claimed generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) like LLaDA and Dream enable parallel token decoding by iteratively denoising a fully masked response sequence, offering a promising alternative to autoregressive generation. However, unlike autoregressive models that benefit from incremental KV caching, MDLMs use bidirectional attention and must reprocess the entire sequence (prompt + response) at every denoising step. This "repeated prefill" pattern creates prohibitive computational cost—dominated by FFN and attention operations—that largely offsets the throughput benefits of parallel decoding. Prior acceleration methods (Fast-dLLM, dKV-Cache, dLLM-Cache, Elastic-Cache) address this with fixed refresh schedules or block-level heuristics, but these rely on rigid a priori strategies that do not account for layer-wise dynamics and often require extensive per-dataset hyperparameter tuning. Methodology: DyLLM is a training-free inference framework that exploits temporal sparsity: across consecutive denoising steps, the majority of token representations remain stable, with only a small salient subset undergoing meaningful semantic transitions. Saliency at layer l and timestep t for token i is quantified by the cosine similarity of attention context vectors between adjacent steps: s_{t,l}^{(i)} = (C_{t,l} · C_{t-1,l}) / (‖C_{t,l}‖ ‖C_{t-1,l}‖). Tokens with similarity below a threshold τ are marked salient; only these undergo full FFN recomputation and exact attention updates, while non-salient tokens reuse cached activations. DyLLM operates in two phases: an initial T_full=4 warm-up of full forward passes to populate caches, followed by sparse steps where SparseStep selectively recomputes salient tokens. The saliency threshold is applied per-layer, naturally adapting to the empirical observation that early layers are highly stable while deeper layers show broader similarity distributions. For non-salient tokens, DyLLM further introduces saliency-aware approximate attention: since semantic deltas are localized to salient positions, the attention context update decomposes into a sparse column-row product involving only salient value deltas (ΔV) and salient attention columns, reducing attention complexity from O(N²d) to O(N·|A_{t-1,l}|·d). Additionally, 75% of sparse steps process only response tokens (response-only steps), since salient tokens are concentrated in the unmasked response region. Two formal propositions ground the approximation: scale invariance of the cosine similarity metric under RMSNorm, and an error bound δ ≤ κ(W_o)√(2(1−s_{t,l})) showing that skipping FFN for near-stationary tokens (s→1) introduces near-zero output error. Key Results: Evaluated on LLaDA 8B Instruct and Dream 7B Instruct across GSM8K, MBPP, MATH, and MMLU-Pro benchmarks on a single H100 80GB GPU. With n_u=1 parallel decoding: DyLLM (τ=0.99) achieves 7.60× throughput over the LLaDA baseline (87.21 vs 11.47 tok/s on GSM8K) while improving accuracy from 77.79% to 79.08%; on Dream (τ=0.9975), DyLLM achieves 9.60× speedup (120.62 vs 12.57 tok/s) with accuracy maintained at 78.09% vs 75.59% baseline. Against Fast-dLLM (PrefixCache+DualCache), DyLLM consistently preserves higher accuracy while delivering competitive or superior throughput. On Dream MBPP, DyLLM achieves 56.80% accuracy (vs 54.60% baseline) at 7.12× speedup. A critical scalability advantage emerges at higher n_u: Fast-dLLM's mandatory full-sequence refresh steps become increasingly dominant as parallel decoding degree grows, whereas DyLLM maintains sparsity at every step—enabling it to surpass Fast-dLLM DualCache in throughput on Dream at n_u=4. The number of computed tokens adapts flexibly across layers, averaging substantially fewer tokens than fixed-schedule competitors without sacrificing generality. Strengths: - Training-free and model-agnostic: applies directly to existing MDLMs (LLaDA, Dream) without any fine-tuning, making adoption straightforward - Layer-adaptive saliency mechanism avoids the need for per-dataset hyperparameter tuning that plagues fixed-schedule methods like dLLM-Cache, which requires separate K_P and K_R values per model and dataset - Theoretically grounded: formal error bounds via Propositions 3.1 and 3.2 establish that the cosine similarity metric directly controls approximation error under RMSNorm, providing principled justification for the threshold-based selection policy - Superior scalability with increasing parallel decoding degree (n_u): unlike Fast-dLLM whose refresh overhead grows proportionally with n_u, DyLLM's sparsity-based approach maintains efficiency without refresh bottlenecks Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single GPU evaluation only (H100 80GB): no experiments on distributed inference or multi-GPU settings, limiting assessment of scalability claims for very large models or long sequences - The threshold τ still varies by model family (0.99 for LLaDA vs 0.9975 for Dream), requiring some tuning; the paper does not provide a principled automatic method for selecting τ without benchmark sweeps - The warm-up phase (T_full=4 full steps) adds overhead for short generation sequences; the framework's benefit is most pronounced for moderate-to-large numbers of denoising steps - No evaluation on latency for single-sequence interactive settings—all throughput numbers are batch inference (batch size 16 for GSM8K), which may overstate practical gains for single-request use cases
Search Results 2603.08026 PDF
#70 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Discrete Tokenization Unlocks Transformers for Calibrated Tabular Forecasting

Yael S. Elmatad · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The core insight — that discrete tokenization lets Transformers implicitly recover tree-like piecewise-constant decision boundaries — is appealing, and the adaptive Gaussian smoothing for calibration is a principled design. However, the entire evaluation is confined to a single domain (marathon pace prediction), making it impossible to judge whether the recipe generalizes to standard tabular benchmarks. The contribution reads more as a strong domain-specific application than a general tabular learning advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Transformers consistently underperform gradient boosted trees (XGBoost) on tabular data benchmarks. The prevailing explanation is architectural: trees naturally produce piecewise-constant decision boundaries via axis-aligned splits, capturing the discrete regime structure prevalent in tabular features, while neural networks are smooth function approximators that struggle with these irregular patterns. Existing tabular Transformer efforts (FT-Transformer, TabTransformer, TabNet) narrow but do not close the gap. A secondary gap is that all tree-based methods and most neural approaches produce point estimates rather than calibrated probability distributions, which limits their utility for uncertainty-aware decision-making. Methodology: The paper presents RunTime, a decoder-only causal Transformer (6 layers, 8 heads, 512-dimensional embeddings) applied to marathon running pace prediction, built around three interlocking design choices. First, discrete tokenization: continuous environmental features and target pace are quantized into 270+ bins via balanced (quantile-based) binning, so the vocabulary explicitly represents the discrete regimes that tree splits discover implicitly. Temporal gaps (weeks_since_last, weeks_to_target) and race distance are encoded as raw numeric tokens rather than quantized. The token sequence per race event follows a fixed grammar: [features+demographics][pace][d_next][d_fin]. Second, Gaussian-integrated soft targets replace hard one-hot labels: the target distribution T_i integrates a Gaussian centered at the true pace over each bin's interval, preserving ordinal structure. An adaptive smoothing formula σ_i = sqrt(σ_floor² + (k·w_i)²) scales the smoothing width proportionally to bin width w_i, keeping narrow bins sharp and giving broad tail bins more mass — automatically adapting to heterogeneous bin widths without post-hoc temperature scaling. Third, entity-disjoint evaluation splits 600K NYRR runners (2.25M/250K/500K examples) with no runner overlap across train/validation/test, preventing individual memorization. The adaptive-σ best-KS checkpoint is selected to minimize the Kolmogorov-Smirnov calibration statistic rather than MAE, explicitly trading a small accuracy cost for distributional fidelity. Key Results: On the 60K-runner, 500K-prediction test set, RunTime achieves median MAE of 35.94s versus 40.31s for tuned XGBoost — a 10.8% improvement — and roughly 30% better than the physics-based Riegel formula (49.74s). Median RMSE is 71.83s versus 73.15s for XGBoost. The adaptive-σ best-KS checkpoint achieves KS=0.0045, indicating near-uniform calibration across percentiles without any post-training temperature scaling. Ablations quantify each component: removing time-delta tokens raises median MAE from 35.94s to 36.58s (≈1.8% degradation) and increases wall-clock convergence from 60h to 107h; shuffling race order raises MAE to 36.65s (≈2.0%). Calibration sweeps show that very small σ (hard targets) produces overconfident distributions concentrated at extremes, while very large σ (σ=35) produces underconfident distributions (KS=0.119); the adaptive-σ variant achieves the best calibration (KS=0.0045 at best-KS checkpoint vs. 0.0264 at MAE-optimal checkpoint). Attention diagnostics confirm cadence tokens dominate when prediction entropy is high. Strengths: - The adaptive Gaussian smoothing formula is a principled, tunable solution to the bin-width heterogeneity problem that implicitly functions as an in-training temperature schedule, eliminating the need for post-hoc calibration methods like temperature scaling. - The entity-disjoint evaluation protocol is methodologically sound and prevents the memorization leakage that plagues many tabular benchmarks; the 5M example dataset drawn from NYRR's 9+1 program ensures consistent race coverage per athlete. - The ablation design is clean: time-delta tokenization and sequential ordering are tested as binary on/off switches, and the calibration sweep independently varies σ while holding training budget constant, isolating the effect of each design choice. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The entire experimental evaluation uses a single domain (road running pace), making it impossible to assess how well the discretization-plus-smoothing recipe generalizes to tabular datasets without natural sequential structure or with different target distributions. External validation on standard tabular benchmarks (e.g., UCI, OpenML) is absent. - The discretized vocabulary has no overflow bins, so paces outside the training distribution are clipped — a practical limitation for tail predictions (unusually fast or slow runners) that the authors acknowledge but do not address in this version. - The stride grammar repeats fixed runner attributes (gender, age) at every event block, introducing token redundancy that wastes context window capacity; the authors note this inefficiency but retain it for implementation simplicity rather than correctness.
Citations 2603.07448 PDF
#71 — Moderately Innovative (56)

GP2F: Cross-Domain Graph Prompting with Adaptive Fusion of Pre-trained Graph Neural Networks

Dongxiao He, Wenxuan Sun, Yongqi Huang, Jitao Zhao, Di Jin · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: GP2F's theoretical contribution — the control-variate framing proving that combining frozen and adapted branches reduces estimation error — is clean and provides principled motivation for the architecture. However, the dual-branch frozen-plus-adapted design pattern is increasingly common in NLP adapter literature, and the practical improvements over strong baselines are modest (1-3%), with linear probing alone competitive on several datasets.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph Prompt Learning (GPL) has emerged as a way to adapt pre-trained GNNs to downstream tasks without full fine-tuning. The field has shifted toward cross-domain settings where the pre-training source and downstream target differ substantially in data distribution (e.g., a model trained on citation graphs applied to co-purchase networks). A key open question is why existing GPL methods remain effective under such distribution shift. The authors observe that two simple baselines — Linear Probing (LP, frozen encoder + linear classifier) and full Fine-Tuning (FT) — are surprisingly competitive with, or even superior to, existing GPL methods in cross-domain settings. This motivates a principled investigation into what actually drives GPL effectiveness when domain gaps are large. Methodology: The paper makes both a theoretical and algorithmic contribution. Theoretically, the authors model the frozen encoder output and an adapted encoder output as two noisy, unbiased estimators of the same latent representation, each with different error statistics. Under a second-order error model, they prove (Lemma 3.3, Theorem 3.4) that an affine combination of the two branches achieves strictly lower MSE than either branch alone, with the optimal mixing weight λ* derivable in closed form. This provides a principled explanation for why combining frozen pre-trained knowledge with task-specific adaptation works in cross-domain transfer. Guided by this theory, the authors propose GP2F, a dual-branch architecture. The frozen branch passes the pre-trained GNN unchanged (H_pre). The adapted branch introduces lightweight layer-wise residual adapters — bottleneck projections DOWN ∈ ℝ^(d×r) → UP ∈ ℝ^(r×d) with r ≪ d and learnable per-layer scaling factors β — applied inside the frozen backbone to produce H_adp. The two branches are fused via a learnable scalar α: H_mix = α·H_pre + (1−α)·H_adp. Two auxiliary losses regularize training: (1) a cross-branch contrastive loss L_ctr that treats the two branch outputs as two views of the same graph and applies InfoNCE with graph-neighborhood positive pairs, aligning the adapted branch to the pre-trained representation space; and (2) a topology-consistent fusion loss L_fus formulated as binary cross-entropy between the fused self-similarity matrix S_mix and the target adjacency matrix A, ensuring topologically adjacent nodes remain similar in the fused representation. The total objective is L = L_cls + λ_1·L_ctr + λ_2·L_fus. Key Results: GP2F is evaluated on 7 node classification datasets (including ogbn-arxiv with 169K nodes and ogbn-products with 2.4M nodes) and 6 graph classification datasets in cross-domain few-shot settings (1-shot, 3-shot, 5-shot), with encoders pre-trained using GRACE, DGI, and GraphMAE. Against GPL baselines (GPPT, GPF, GraphPrompt, EdgePrompt, DAGPromT) and cross-domain methods (GraphLoRA), GP2F achieves best or second-best performance in the large majority of settings. On 1-shot node classification (GRACE pre-training), GP2F reaches 57.80% on Cora, 51.55% on CiteSeer, 54.89% on Computers, 67.60% on Photo, and 68.33% on CS — improving over the best baseline by 1.66% on average, with larger gains of 3.45% on CiteSeer and 3.49% on CS. On 50-shot graph classification (PROTEINS pre-training), GP2F achieves best or second-best AUROC across COX2 (66.87%), BZR (69.18%), PROTEINS (66.78%), and MUTAG (73.19%). On large-scale datasets, GP2F reaches 68.76% on ogbn-arxiv and 76.91% on ogbn-products, outperforming all compared baselines. Ablation studies confirm that both L_ctr and L_fus contribute meaningfully; removing either loss degrades performance across all datasets, and removing both (direct merge without constraints) performs worst. Strengths: - The theoretical analysis is a genuine contribution: the control-variate framing that formalizes why combining frozen and adapted branches reduces estimation error is clean, non-trivial, and directly motivates the architecture design. - The dual-branch design is parameter-efficient: the bottleneck adapters add very few parameters (rank r ≪ d), and the pre-trained backbone is never modified, making the method compatible with any pre-training objective (contrastive or generative) without hyperparameter changes. - Experimental scope is thorough: 15 datasets spanning citation, co-purchase, co-authorship, web, molecular, and protein graphs; multiple pre-training strategies; 1/3/5-shot settings; and large-scale OGB benchmarks up to 2.4M nodes. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The theoretical guarantees rest on a second-order error model that assumes unbiased, uncorrelated noise between the two branches. In practice, both branches share the same backbone and data, making this independence assumption optimistic and difficult to verify empirically. - The hyperparameter sensitivity analysis (adapter rank r, temperature parameters τ_ctr and τ_fus, loss weights λ_1 and λ_2) shows dataset-dependent optimal settings with no clear principled selection criterion, requiring per-dataset tuning that limits plug-and-play usability. - The performance advantage over strong baselines is modest (∼1–3% on most benchmarks), and LP alone achieves competitive or best results on several datasets (e.g., best on PubMed, runner-up on Computers and WikiCS), suggesting the dual-branch machinery may not be strictly necessary in all cross-domain scenarios.
Recommended 2602.11629 PDF
#72 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Regression Models Meet Foundation Models: A Hybrid-AI Approach to Practical Electricity Price Forecasting

Yunzhong Qiu, Binzhu Li, Hao Wei, Shenglin Weng, Chen Wang, Zhongyi Pei, Mingsheng Long, Jianmin Wang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The insight of using frozen TSFMs as feature generators for downstream regression is practically sound and computationally elegant, especially the cacheability enabling CPU-only inference. However, the improvement over direct regression with rich exogenous features is modest (1.8-4.6% MAE), raising questions about how much the TSFM forecasts truly contribute versus the domain-engineered features.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Electricity price forecasting (EPF) is critical for energy market participants making bidding, risk management, and resource allocation decisions. Electricity prices exhibit extreme volatility, non-Gaussianity, heavy tails, and non-stationarity — characteristics that make accurate forecasting genuinely hard. The Shanxi spot market data used in this paper shows tail ratios (p99/p50) of ~5, excess kurtosis of ~5, and extreme price events (p > 1000) occurring ~5.5% of the time. Two dominant paradigms each have complementary weaknesses: time-series foundation models (TSFMs) excel at capturing temporal dependencies via large-scale pretraining but underutilize cross-variate correlations and non-periodic patterns essential for price forecasting; regression models capture feature interactions well but are limited to future-available inputs, ignoring crucial historical drivers unavailable at forecast time. No prior work bridges these two paradigms in a principled way for EPF. Methodology: The paper proposes FutureBoosting, a two-stage hybrid paradigm. Stage 1 uses a frozen pretrained TSFM (e.g., Chronos2, TimerXL, Sundial) in zero-shot mode to forecast future-unavailable exogenous variables — supply-demand drivers like system load and renewable generation — over the day-ahead or real-time horizon. These TSFM-generated forecasts serve as augmented features encoding temporal structure and forward-looking expectations. Stage 2 concatenates these TSFM forecasts with future-available exogenous variables (grid production plans, weather forecasts, calendar features) and domain-knowledge-constructed factors (thermal auction space, renewable ratio) into an enriched feature set, on which a lightweight tree-based regressor (LightGBM) is trained to forecast final electricity prices. The TSFM is kept frozen — no fine-tuning — making TSFM inference cacheable and the entire pipeline CPU-runnable after caching. The framework is model-agnostic: any TSFM and any regressor can be plugged in. The design insight is that TSFMs provide generalizable temporal priors while regression captures domain-specific cross-variate structure, and these capabilities are complementary rather than competing. Key Results: On the Shanxi real-world dataset (15-minute resolution, full year 2025, rolling monthly evaluation), FutureBoosting reduces MSE/MAE by 45.43%/32.40% for day-ahead and 39.27%/24.98% for real-time forecasting relative to corresponding zero-shot TSFM baselines. Compared to direct regression on exogenous variables (LightGBM baseline), improvements are 1.80%/4.56% for day-ahead and 6.17%/2.97% for real-time. Best overall: TimerXL + FutureBoosting achieves 33,978 MSE / 94.78 MAE day-ahead and 44,522 MSE / 117.10 MAE real-time. On the RealE benchmark (France and Germany, hourly), FutureBoosting improves over zero-shot TSFMs by 16.92%/8.41% on FR and 36.52%/18.52% on DE (MSE/MAE). Compared to fine-tuning Chronos2 with LoRA, FutureBoosting achieves competitive MSE (34,344 vs. 35,333) at a fraction of the computational cost: 151s end-to-end per monthly window on a single GPU vs. 8,867s using 4× GPUs for LoRA fine-tuning. With TSFM inference cached, the pipeline becomes CPU-only at 59s per monthly window. Strengths: - The core insight — use TSFMs as frozen feature generators rather than end-to-end forecasters — is practically sound and avoids expensive fine-tuning while still harnessing pretrained temporal knowledge. - Computational efficiency is excellent: a single RTX 4090 suffices, TSFM inference is cacheable making repeated runs CPU-only, and the downstream regressor trains in ~29s per monthly window. - The paper includes a real deployed system evaluation on Xingzhixun's IoTDB server (December 2025), with SHAP-based instance-level explanations demonstrating that the enriched feature set meaningfully corrects TSFM errors in both low-price and high-price regimes. - Experimental design is rigorous: rolling monthly evaluation on a full year of real market data, multiple TSFM backbones tested, ablations over regressor choices, and comparison against LoRA fine-tuning. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The improvement over direct regression (LightGBM on exogenous variables alone) is modest — 1.80%/4.56% MAE day-ahead — raising the question of how much the TSFM forecasts actually contribute versus the rich exogenous feature set itself; the ablation in Table 4 does not isolate this clearly. - The framework requires careful domain-knowledge engineering (thermal auction space, renewable ratio factors) for the Shanxi market; generalization to markets with fewer available exogenous variables or different regulatory structures is not demonstrated and may require significant manual adaptation per market. - The paper does not address how FutureBoosting handles distribution shift between TSFM pretraining domains and target electricity markets, nor does it analyze failure modes when TSFM forecasts of supply-demand drivers are themselves badly wrong.
Search Results 2603.06726 PDF
#73 — Moderately Innovative (55)

EmbTracker: Traceable Black-box Watermarking for Federated Language Models

Haodong Zhao, Jinming Hu, Yijie Bai, Tian Dong, Wei Du, Zhuosheng Zhang, Yanjiao Chen, Haojin Zhu, Gongshen Liu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: EmbTracker makes a clever observation that frozen embedding layers in PEFT-based federated learning provide a durable, low-interference carrier for per-client watermarks, and the resulting system achieves strong empirical results across an impressively broad evaluation. However, the core technique — embedding poisoning to create backdoor triggers — builds directly on well-established backdoor watermarking concepts, with the primary contribution being their application to the federated LM setting with PEFT compatibility rather than a fundamentally new mechanism.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Federated Language Model (FedLM) training enables collaborative fine-tuning of large language models without sharing raw data, but introduces a critical IP vulnerability: any participating client can obtain the global model snapshot during training and redistribute it without authorization. Existing watermarking schemes for federated learning fail to address this threat adequately — they either embed a single shared watermark that proves only group-level ownership (not which client leaked), require white-box access to model parameters for verification, or demand client-side cooperation to embed watermarks (which adversarial clients can refuse). Furthermore, existing schemes are largely designed for vision models and are incompatible with PEFT methods like LoRA and prefix tuning that dominate FedLM deployments. The core gap is the absence of a server-side, black-box verifiable, client-traceable watermarking framework for federated language models. Methodology: EmbTracker addresses both challenges — black-box verifiability and client-level traceability — through an embedding-poisoning framework that operates entirely on the server side. The key insight is that word embedding matrices in LLMs provide a high-capacity, low-interference carrier: modifying individual token embeddings affects only sentences containing those tokens, leaving unrelated vocabulary untouched, and the embedding layer is typically frozen during PEFT-based client local training, making injected signals durable. The pipeline has three phases. First, trigger generation: each client $c_k$ generates a digital signature using RSA public-key cryptography; the signature is hashed (SHA256) to obtain a token index, which maps to a client-specific trigger word $Tr_k$ from the model vocabulary. Second, watermark injection: the server trains a universal watermark embedding $W_w$ for a universal trigger $Tr_u$ using embedding poisoning on an auxiliary dataset — only the embedding vector of $Tr_u$ is updated, a one-time cost. Before distributing the global model to each client, the server replaces $Tr_k$'s embedding with $W_w$, linking $Tr_k$ to the same backdoor behavior as $Tr_u$. This substitution is performed per-client with no additional training per client and no client-side modifications. Third, watermark verification: when a suspicious model is found, a Certification Authority queries it with each client's trigger set; a client is identified as the leaker if their trigger achieves verification rate (VR) ≥ γ while all other clients' triggers yield VR < γ. Watermark enhancement training is run server-side after each aggregation round to reinforce the signal. The scheme extends naturally to Vision-Language Models by targeting the LLM component's word embedding layer. Key Results: Evaluated across binary classification (SST-2, Enron, Twitter), multi-class classification (AGNews, DBpedia, Yahoo), question answering (FreebaseQA, CoQA, NQ), and visual QA (OK-VQA, OCR-VQA) tasks under both IID and Non-IID settings with BERT and Llama-2-7B: - EmbTracker achieves VR near 100% on almost all tasks and datasets under both IID and Non-IID conditions, meeting the ≥90% satisfactory threshold. The closest traceable black-box baseline, TraMark, fails or is inapplicable on NLP tasks. - Task accuracy (ACC) drops are typically within 1–2% relative to unwatermarked FedAvg, with some datasets showing slight ACC improvements due to regularization effects from watermark training. - Robustness to fine-tuning attacks: VR remains above or near 90% after adversarial fine-tuning on held-out data across most configurations. - Robustness to pruning: VR stays high with up to 30% parameter pruning; beyond this the model degrades to the point of being functionally invalid regardless. - Robustness to quantization: VR remains above 95% under INT8 and FP16 quantization even when ACC has degraded. - Robustness to overwriting attacks: original watermark VR stays at 98.43% after an adversarial client embeds a competing watermark, with timestamp-based mitigation resolving ambiguity. - Generalization across PEFT methods (LoRA, Prefix Tuning), FL algorithms (FedAvg, FedAvgM, FedProx, SCAFFOLD), and model architectures (Llama-3.2-3B, Vicuna-7B, Mistral-7B) consistently exceeds 95% VR. - Only 5 watermark training samples suffice to achieve >90% VR; a 5% poison ratio achieves 96.43% VR on NQ. - Time overhead is marginal: EmbTracker's per-round cost is a lightweight embedding replacement, substantially lower than TraMark (which retrains per-client models each round) and competitive with or better than WAFFLE and FedTracker. Strengths: - The embedding-space carrier is a genuinely clever technical choice: it is naturally compatible with PEFT (embedding layers are frozen on clients), provides high capacity for distinct per-client signals in large vocabularies (30,533 tokens for BERT; 32,000 for Llama-2-7B), and modifying a single token embedding has negligible parameter footprint (~6×10⁻⁷ of total model parameters for Llama-2-7B). - The scheme achieves both black-box verifiability and client-level traceability simultaneously from the server side, without any client-side cooperation — a combination no prior FL watermarking method achieved for LMs. - Thorough empirical evaluation spans 11 datasets, 3 base models, 4 FL algorithms, 2 PEFT methods, and 4 attack types, providing strong evidence of practical deployability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The trigger words are derived deterministically from client signatures via a hash-to-vocabulary-index mapping, which could allow a sophisticated adversary who reverse-engineers the scheme to predict their own trigger and selectively remove or corrupt only that embedding vector before leaking the model — a targeted attack not evaluated. - The scheme assumes the server is fully trusted and that clients faithfully follow the FL protocol (they are only modeled as passive leakers, not active watermark-removal adversaries during local training beyond fine-tuning and pruning). The threat model excludes colluding clients who pool their models to average out individual watermarks, a realistic concern in practice. - Verification confidence degrades somewhat as client count grows to 50 (Fig. 6 shows VR declining on NQ from ~97% at 10 clients to ~90% at 50), suggesting scalability limits in large federations that are acknowledged but not fully resolved.
Recommended 2603.12089 PDF
#74 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Vision-Language Models Encode Clinical Guidelines for Concept-Based Medical Reasoning

Mohamed Harmanani, Bining Long, Zhuoxin Guo, Paul F. R. Wilson, Amirhossein Sabour, Minh Nguyen Nhat To, Gabor Fichtinger, Purang Abolmaesumi, Parvin Mousavi · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: MedCBR's guideline-driven concept enrichment is a practical and well-motivated strategy for overcoming noisy binary concept annotations in medical CBMs. The multi-task contrastive formulation unifying alignment, concept grounding, and classification is competently designed. However, the core mechanism — using an LLM to generate richer training signal from sparse annotations — is a natural application of existing VLM capabilities rather than a methodological departure, and the 20-case radiologist evaluation is insufficient to support clinical reliability claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have gained traction as interpretable AI systems for medical imaging by mapping visual features to human-interpretable concepts before making diagnostic predictions. However, standard CBMs suffer from two critical limitations in clinical deployment: (1) they treat concept annotations as binary indicators, ignoring the relational and contextual meaning encoded in clinical guidelines (e.g., BI-RADS standards) that specify how combinations of findings map to diagnostic risk; and (2) concept annotations in real medical datasets are noisy and incomplete due to inter-observer variability, weakening the visual-concept correspondence. The result is a system that fails on complex cases where individual features appear ambiguous and broader clinical context is required to reconcile competing evidence. Methodology: The paper introduces MedCBR, a three-stage framework. First, a Guideline-Driven Concept Enrichment stage uses a large vision-language model (LVLM) to generate structured clinical reports conditioned on the image, its observed concepts, and the relevant BI-RADS guideline text. This transforms sparse binary concept vectors into rich, guideline-grounded textual representations that capture relational and diagnostic meaning. Second, a Vision-Language Concept Modelling stage fine-tunes a CLIP backbone using a multi-task objective combining contrastive alignment (InfoNCE loss between image and generated report embeddings), concept prediction (lightweight two-layer MLP adapters per concept), and diagnostic classification. This jointly enforces cross-modal alignment, concept-level interpretability, and discriminative power. Third, a Concept-Based Clinical Reasoning stage employs a frozen large reasoning model (LRM) that receives the model's predicted concepts, diagnostic output, and the relevant guideline section to synthesize a structured clinical narrative — explicitly interpreting each concept's contribution, cross-checking against the guideline, and producing a step-by-step justification grounded in verifiable clinical rules. The framework is evaluated on BUS-BRA (1,875 breast ultrasound images), CBIS-DDSM (mammography, ~3,500 ROI patches), and CUB-200-2011 (11,788 bird images, as a natural-image generalization test). Key Results: MedCBR achieves AUROC of 94.2% on BUS-BRA ultrasound (vs. 93.5% for CLIP ViT-L/14 and 91.8% for CLIP CBM) and balanced accuracy of 89.0% (vs. 88.1% for CLIP ViT-L/14, +6.3% vs. AdaCBM). On CBIS-DDSM mammography, AUROC reaches 84.0% and balanced accuracy 76.4%, outperforming all CBM variants. On CUB-200-2011, MedCBR achieves 86.1% accuracy, surpassing label-free CBM by over 10 percentage points. In per-concept AUROC, MedCBR consistently leads across clinically critical descriptors (spiculated margins, microlobulated margins, posterior shadowing) on both imaging modalities. In radiologist-evaluated reasoning quality, MedCBR attains the highest F1 among all VLM baselines on both medical datasets (84.3% on BUS-BRA, 74.4% on CBIS-DDSM), with Concept Interpretation Score of 95.4%/95.2% and Concept Integration Score of 98.3%/87.5%, while maintaining specificity of 94.3%/76.0%. Ablations confirm that each component contributes: guideline-conditioned multi-task training (MTL+Guideline) consistently outperforms the concept bottleneck layer alone (CBL) and the CBL augmented with guidelines. Strengths: - The guideline-enrichment strategy is a principled and practically motivated solution to noisy concept supervision — using the guideline itself to generate richer training signal rather than requiring additional human annotation. - The multi-task contrastive formulation elegantly unifies vision-language alignment, concept grounding, and diagnostic classification under a single differentiable objective, and the ablations cleanly isolate each component's contribution. - Generalization to CUB-200-2011 (non-medical, Sibley field-guide conditioned) demonstrates that the framework is domain-agnostic and not merely overfit to BI-RADS structure, a meaningful test of the approach's breadth. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Dependence on annotated concept labels at training time remains a hard requirement; the paper acknowledges this cost barrier but defers label-free extension to future work, leaving a significant scalability gap for new clinical domains without curated concept banks. - The reasoning quality evaluation is conducted on only 20 radiologist-reviewed cases — too small a sample to draw statistically robust conclusions about clinical reliability, particularly for the tail of challenging or atypical presentations. - The LRM operates as a frozen post-hoc reasoning module with no feedback into the vision encoder; errors in predicted concepts propagate directly into the reasoning narrative, and the system lacks any mechanism to flag or recover from concept prediction failures at inference time beyond the LRM noting inconsistencies qualitatively.
Search Results 2603.08921 PDF
#75 — Moderately Innovative (55)

DenoiseFlow: Uncertainty-Aware Denoising for Reliable LLM Agentic Workflows

Yandong Yan, Junwei Peng, Shijie Li, Chenxi Li, Yifei Shang, Can Deng, Ruiting Dai, Yongqiang Zhao, Jiaqi Zhu, Yu Huang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: DenoiseFlow provides a clean theoretical framing (Noisy MDP) that unifies several individually known techniques — uncertainty estimation via semantic entropy, adaptive branching, and root-cause tracing. The 40-56% cost reduction over fixed branching is the most compelling result. However, the absolute accuracy gains over the best baseline are modest (+1.3% over JudgeFlow), and each component draws heavily from established methods (Monte Carlo sampling, temperature scaling, influence propagation).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-horizon LLM agentic workflows decompose complex tasks into multi-step reasoning chains, but reliability degrades as sequence length grows. The core failure mode—termed accumulated semantic ambiguity—occurs when minor interpretation errors in natural language instructions compound silently across steps, progressively drifting execution from the intended specification. Existing approaches are fundamentally reactive: static workflow optimizers (AFlow, MermaidFlow) fix execution structure offline with no runtime adaptivity; error-recovery methods (Reflexion, Self-Refine) detect failures only post-hoc via explicit signals; uniform branching strategies (CoT-SC, Tree-of-Thoughts) allocate exploration budget wastefully regardless of per-step uncertainty; and single-path methods ignore uncertainty entirely. No existing method integrates all five capabilities needed for robust long-horizon execution: uncertainty sensing, adaptive branching, root-cause tracing, online calibration, and targeted correction. Methodology: DenoiseFlow formalizes multi-step agent interaction as a Noisy MDP, where state transitions are perturbed by a latent semantic noise process ξ representing LLM-induced ambiguity. Accumulated Semantic Divergence Δ_t grows with graph depth and width via a recurrence over predecessor couplings, motivating a closed-loop policy that maximizes task reliability under a fixed inference budget. The framework operates through three coordinated stages. Stage 1 (Sensing) estimates per-step uncertainty u_t via Monte Carlo sampling: N=5 parallel LLM calls are clustered by cosine-similarity into semantic interpretations C_t, and Normalized Semantic Entropy over the cluster distribution yields a bounded uncertainty proxy ∈ [0,1]. Uncertainty propagates through a Probabilistic Dependency Graph G via a dual-channel recurrence combining a Bottleneck channel (λ=0.5, capturing single high-risk predecessors) and an Aggregation channel (β=0.3, capturing cumulative mild degradation). Online self-calibration via temperature scaling continuously corrects overconfidence/underconfidence using verifier feedback—without ground-truth labels. Stage 2 (Regulating) synthesizes a unified risk metric combining propagated systemic risk and local slot-level uncertainty, then routes each step to one of three execution modes: Direct (high confidence, single greedy path), Branch (medium confidence, K_t parallel paths with consensus scoring by validity + cohesion + cluster size), or Refine/Escalate (low confidence, trigger Stage 3). Thresholds θ_high and θ_low are set adaptively from the running confidence distribution (Q3 and Q1–0.5×IQR). Stage 3 (Correcting) performs influence-based root-cause localization over G by backpropagating failure influence to identify k = argmax_k I_k, applies asymmetric calibration (boosting uncertainty at k to 1.0), and re-executes from the corrected state—preserving valid intermediate results rather than performing global restarts. Key Results: Evaluated on six benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning (GSM8K: 1,319 problems; MATH: 500 competition problems), code generation (MBPP: 500 problems; HumanEval: 164 problems), and multi-hop QA (HotpotQA: 7,405; DROP: 9,536), all using GPT-4o-mini as the backbone LLM under a unified evaluation protocol. DenoiseFlow achieves 83.3% average accuracy, outperforming the strongest baseline JudgeFlow (82.0%) by +1.3% and AFlow (78.1%) by +5.2%—the best on all six benchmarks simultaneously. On competitive MATH, the gain is +2.9% over JudgeFlow (+8.6% over AFlow), where multiple valid solution strategies create genuine interpretation ambiguity that adaptive branching resolves. On code generation (MBPP 84.9%, HumanEval 93.9%), closed-loop refinement with binary test execution feedback enables rapid confidence calibration. On multi-hop QA, root-cause tracing outperforms full restart by +2.64 F1 and local retry by +3.11 F1 on DROP while reducing cost by 13%. Critically, adaptive branching reduces computational cost by 40–56% compared to fixed K=5 or K=7 branching (average cost $0.194 on GSM8K vs. $0.32 for fixed K=5; $1.012 on MATH vs. $1.69). Cross-model experiments confirm generalization: on GPT-4o, DenoiseFlow achieves 99.5% GSM8K and 68.5% MATH; on DeepSeek-V2.5, 88.8% GSM8K and 55.5% MATH—with the largest relative gains on weaker models (+12.5% MATH on DeepSeek-V2.5). Ablation identifies Adaptive Branching as the most critical component (−3.87% average when removed, −5.81% on MBPP, −5.79 F1 on HotpotQA), with Online Calibration second (−2.20% average). The uncertainty estimates are rank-consistent with actual difficulty (binned Spearman ρ = −0.782 on GSM8K and MATH), validating the routing signal. Stage 3 refinement recovers 33–44% of triggered failures. Strengths: - Clean theoretical grounding: the Noisy MDP formulation with a formal propagation bound (Proposition M.1) motivates the architecture rather than engineering it ad hoc; errors at early steps compound exponentially, justifying front-loaded uncertainty sensing. - Label-free online calibration via temperature scaling with verifier feedback is practically important—most calibration methods require held-out labeled data, making them inapplicable during autonomous execution. - Adaptive routing achieves cost reduction (40–56%) while matching or exceeding fixed-branching accuracy, addressing the core efficiency-robustness tradeoff; branching is concentrated where it is genuinely needed. - Comprehensive experimental design: 13 baselines reproduced under a unified protocol with the same backbone LLM, three random seeds, and sensitivity analysis across all key hyperparameters. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are primarily conducted with GPT-4o-mini as backbone; validation on fully open-source models (Llama, Mistral) is absent, and different models may exhibit different uncertainty characteristics requiring threshold recalibration. - Online calibration requires approximately 20 warm-up observations to stabilize—during cold start, uncertainty estimates are unreliable, and temperature may not transfer across task domains when task distribution shifts. - Stage 3 refinement effectiveness is coupled to verification signal quality: for tasks without clear success criteria (open-ended generation, creative writing), the refinement loop degrades to blind re-execution; the 33–44% recovery rate is modest even on well-defined tasks. - The Monte Carlo sampling overhead in Stage 1 (N=5 concurrent LLM calls per step) adds non-trivial cost for uniformly simple problems, and the proposed mitigation (a meta-controller to skip uncertainty estimation) is left as future work.
Recommended 2603.00532 PDF
#76 — Moderately Innovative (55)

LLM-WikiRace: Benchmarking Long-term Planning and Reasoning over Real-World Knowledge Graphs

Juliusz Ziomek, William Bankes, Lorenz Wolf, Shyam Sundhar Ramesh, Xiaohang Tang, Ilija Bogunovic · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: LLM-WikiRace is a well-designed benchmark that cleanly disentangles world knowledge from planning ability via the binary link-classification experiment, producing the concrete and falsifiable 'planning gap' finding. The behavioral analysis of loop frequency as the dominant failure mode is unusually thorough. However, as a benchmark paper it introduces no new methods, and the oracle action-space filtering substantially constrains the problem, limiting ecological validity.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Most LLM planning benchmarks operate in synthetic, structured environments with limited action spaces, leaving open the question of how well LLMs can leverage pretrained world knowledge for planning in large, real-world knowledge graphs. This paper addresses that gap by introducing LLM-WikiRace, a benchmark based on the WikiRace game in which an agent must navigate from a source Wikipedia page to a target page by following hyperlinks step by step. The task requires long-horizon planning, semantic reasoning over open-domain concepts, and operational use of world knowledge under partial observability — the agent sees only the current page and its outgoing links, not the global graph structure. Methodology: LLM-WikiRace is built on a snapshot of the Wikipedia hyperlink graph (549,232 pages in the largest strongly connected component, taken June 2025). Three difficulty levels are defined by shortest-path length between source and target: easy (path length 3–4, 200 pairs), medium (5–6, 150 pairs), and hard (7–8, 100 pairs). At each step, the agent receives a prompt with the current page, the target page, traversal history, and 50 candidate outgoing links (filtered using precomputed shortest-path distances, hidden from the model). A maximum of 30 steps is allowed. The benchmark evaluates over 20 open- and closed-source models including Gemini 3, GPT-5, Claude Opus 4.5, DeepSeek R1, Kimi K2, LLaMA 3 (1B–70B), and Gemma 3 (4B–27B). Two key metrics are reported: success rate (fraction of episodes reaching the target within 30 steps) and suboptimal steps (excess steps beyond the shortest path on successful runs). To disentangle world knowledge from planning ability, the authors construct a separate binary classification task probing whether models know which Wikipedia pages are directly hyperlinked — an experiment that isolates graph knowledge from sequential decision-making. A preliminary fine-tuning experiment using DAPO (Decoupled clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization) on Qwen-2.5-7B is also conducted to assess whether RL-based training can improve performance. Key Results: Performance stratifies sharply by difficulty. On easy instances, leading models (Gemini 3: 95%, GPT-5: 92.5%, Claude Opus 4.5: 91.5%, Grok 4.1-F: 90%) exceed 90% success, with Gemini 3 also achieving superhuman path quality (0.9 suboptimal steps vs. 2.3 for humans). On medium difficulty, success drops to 44–66% for top models. On hard instances, Gemini 3 achieves only 23% — the best of any model — while most models fall below 20% and smaller open-source models (LLaMA, Gemma families) reach near-zero. The world-knowledge classification experiment reveals a "planning gap": models with comparable world knowledge scores diverge substantially on game performance, with reasoning-tuned models outperforming instruction-tuned models at the same knowledge level. Trajectory analysis shows that looping is the dominant failure mode — Gemini 3 enters loops in 66% of hard tasks and Claude Opus 4.5 in 92%, with recovery rates near zero on hard instances. Loop frequency correlates strongly and negatively with success rate (regression coefficient −1.02, 95% CI [−1.13, −0.91]). DAPO fine-tuning on Qwen-2.5-7B improves easy success from 22.5% to 67.5% after 300 steps and medium from 1.3% to 4.6%, but hard performance remains 0% across all checkpoints. Strengths: - The benchmark is cleanly designed: difficulty is grounded in a principled, graph-theoretic measure (shortest-path distance), the environment is fixed to a specific Wikipedia snapshot ensuring reproducibility, and the action-space filtering using oracle shortest-path distances is transparently disclosed and kept hidden from the model. - The disentangling of world knowledge from planning ability via the binary link-classification experiment is methodologically sound and produces the concrete, falsifiable "planning gap" finding — a genuinely useful diagnostic insight. - The behavioral analysis is unusually thorough for a benchmark paper: loop frequency, recovery rate, and average maximum visitation metrics, combined with qualitative trajectory traces, give mechanistic insight into failure modes rather than just aggregate numbers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The action-space filtering (providing the 50 links closest to the target by shortest-path distance) is a substantial oracle intervention that does not reflect true open-ended navigation; it artificially constrains the search space and may inflate success rates on easy tasks while understating the full difficulty of the problem. - The fine-tuning experiment (Section 5.3) reduces the task to a one-step supervised problem during training (choosing the optimal link at each step), which is a different problem than multi-step sequential planning; the authors acknowledge this but it limits the generalizability of the RL training conclusions. - Hard instances remain nearly unsolved by all models (best: 23%), which is useful as a ceiling signal but limits the benchmark's ability to differentiate among top-tier models on the hardest split — effectively, the hard split is currently a binary pass/fail for most systems.
Recommended 2602.16902 PDF
