#77 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Improving Parametric Knowledge Access in Reasoning Language Models

Melody Ma, John Hewitt · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: A well-executed empirical study that asks a timely question—whether RLVR-trained reasoning transfers to parametric knowledge recall—and provides a clear answer with strong ablation structure. However, the contribution is self-admittedly incremental: applying existing RLVR machinery to a new domain with limited mechanistic insight into what the model actually learns, and the authors' own analysis suggests gains may reflect improved answer calibration rather than deeper associative reasoning.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reasoning language models trained via Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) excel at mathematics and coding, but it is unclear whether their reasoning capabilities transfer to accessing world knowledge stored in their own parameters. Unlike deductive reasoning, parametric knowledge retrieval requires a different kind of associative, spreading-activation-style cognition—recalling that Canberra is Australia's capital may benefit from reasoning through related concepts like purpose-built capitals or major cities. The gap this paper fills is both empirical (do reasoning models use their reasoning budget effectively for knowledge recall?) and methodological (can RLVR teach models to reason better over their own parameters?). Methodology: The paper proceeds in two stages. First, it evaluates four reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B, Olmo-3-7B-Think, GPT-OSS-20B, GPT-5.2) on TriviaQA and Natural Questions under two prompt conditions—baseline (answer only) and +Cue (think step-by-step)—plus MATH as a control where RLVR training already saturates reasoning. This isolates whether models spontaneously use reasoning well for knowledge recall. Second, the authors apply RLVR to GPT-OSS-20B using TriviaQA as the training domain. The reward function combines an answer correctness signal (1.0 for exact match, 0.5 for recall, 0 otherwise) with a small format penalty. Optimization uses a GRPO-style importance-sampling policy gradient with LoRA (rank=32), group size 8, and KL penalty 0.01. Two ablations—Reasoning-SFT (offline imitation of correct reasoning traces) and standard SFT (no reasoning tokens)—isolate the contribution of on-policy RL versus supervised fine-tuning. Key Results: Adding the think step-by-step cue yields modest but statistically significant improvements on knowledge recall (average +0.8% Ex-Recall across models and datasets), while degrading MATH performance for three of four models, confirming that math reasoning is already well-optimized by prior RLVR but knowledge recall is not. After RLVR training on TriviaQA, GPT-OSS-20B improves substantially: +27.1% EM and +9.9% Ex-Recall on TriviaQA, +12.2% EM and +4.2% Ex-Recall on Natural Questions (out-of-domain), +9.5% EM on HotpotQA, +1.5% EM on SimpleQA, and +3.0% EM on StrategyQA. These gains far exceed those from Reasoning-SFT (e.g., +2.3% EM on TriviaQA vs. +27.1% for RL). Notably, the RL-trained model also improves on MATH in the no-cue setting (+3.0%), suggesting incidental transfer. Reasoning traces grow modestly in length (94→107 tokens average on TriviaQA), but qualitative inspection shows the model often simply arrives at the correct answer earlier in the trace rather than performing deeper associative reasoning. Strengths: - Clean experimental design with a strong ablation structure (base vs. +Cue vs. Reasoning-SFT vs. RL) that isolates the contribution of on-policy RL from supervised imitation and from reasoning tokens themselves. - Broad out-of-domain evaluation across five QA benchmarks (NQ, HotpotQA, SimpleQA, StrategyQA) despite training only on TriviaQA, demonstrating genuine generalization rather than benchmark overfitting. - Honest and self-critical analysis of reasoning traces: the authors acknowledge that performance gains may reflect improved answer calibration (outputting the right answer sooner) rather than richer associative reasoning, and frame this as a spectrum rather than a binary. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Training is confined to a single model (GPT-OSS-20B) and a single RL training domain (TriviaQA); it is unclear whether findings generalize across model families, scales, or different knowledge domains. - The nature of the improvement in reasoning traces remains opaque and not mechanistically explained. Gains on harder benchmarks like SimpleQA (+0.6% Ex-Recall) are marginal, suggesting the approach has limited headroom for genuinely challenging factual recall. - The paper is self-described as a "short contribution" and accordingly lacks deeper analysis of what reasoning strategies the RL-trained model actually learns, leaving the spreading-activation hypothesis motivating the work largely unvalidated.
Recommended 2602.22193 PDF
#78 — Moderately Innovative (53)

LLMGreenRec: LLM-Based Multi-Agent Recommender System for Sustainable E-Commerce

Hao N. Nguyen, Hieu M. Nguyen, Son Van Nguyen, Nguyen Thi Hanh · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The closed-loop multi-agent architecture with UCB-based prompt selection is a moderately creative extension of prior LLM-based recommendation work, and consistent gains across three datasets suggest genuine utility. However, the sustainability claims — the paper's distinguishing angle — are almost entirely unsupported: no baselines for green recommendation, no rigorous definition of 'sustainable' products, and no energy measurements despite carbon footprint being a motivating claim.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional session-based recommender systems are optimized for short-term conversion rates using popularity signals, making them poor at capturing nuanced user intent for eco-friendly products. A well-documented "say-do gap" exists where up to 80% of consumers claim sustainability matters to them but only 1–7% alter purchasing behavior. The paper argues that recommender systems inadvertently perpetuate this gap by burying sustainable (often niche) products beneath dominant brands. A secondary motivation is energy efficiency: by resolving user intent faster with fewer interactions, the system reduces the digital carbon footprint of e-commerce sessions. Methodology: LLMGreenRec is a two-stage pipeline. Stage 1 deploys a Cross-encoder reranker (Sentence-BERT + classifier) that scores each candidate item against each session item pairwise, averages the scores across the session, and retains the top 20 of an initial 100-item candidate pool. Stage 2 is a six-agent LLM orchestration loop built on Meta-Llama-3. The agents operate sequentially: (1) Evaluate ranks the filtered 20 candidates via an initial prompt; (2) DetectError flags sessions where the ground-truth target falls outside the top-10; (3) InferReason uses LLM self-reflection to hypothesize why the prompt failed; (4) RefinePrompt rewrites the prompt based on those hypotheses; (5) Augment generates 3–5 paraphrase variants of the refined prompt; and (6) Select applies the UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) algorithm to choose the best-performing prompt across exploration/exploitation tradeoffs. The chosen prompt is fed back to the Evaluate agent, closing the feedback loop. The sustainability dimension is introduced by biasing the candidate pool and prompt framing toward eco-friendly product attributes. Key Results: On general recommendation (RQ1, multi-agent module only), LLMGreenRec substantially outperforms all baselines across three datasets (ML-1M, Amazon Games, Bundle). Against the strongest LLM baseline PO4ISR, gains are: +27.5% HR@1 on ML-1M (0.2550 vs. 0.2000); +76.9% HR@1 on Games (0.4580 vs. 0.2588); +26.6% HR@5 on Games (0.7430 vs. 0.5866); +40.7% NDCG@5 on Bundle (0.4279 vs. 0.3040). For sustainable product recommendation (RQ2), evaluated only on the Bundle dataset, LLMGreenRec achieves HR@1 of 0.3950 and HR@5 of 0.5504, meaning the target sustainable item is ranked first in ~40% of sessions and in the top five in ~55% of sessions. No baseline comparison is provided for RQ2. Strengths: - The closed-loop, self-correcting multi-agent architecture with UCB-based prompt selection is a principled approach to automated prompt optimization, extending PO4ISR in a meaningful direction. - Large and consistent gains over strong LLM-based baselines on three heterogeneous datasets suggest the multi-agent framework is genuinely capturing richer session intent. - The two-stage design (reranker then LLM reasoning) is computationally sensible: it bounds LLM context length by pruning candidates early. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The sustainable recommendation evaluation (RQ2) is entirely without baselines — Table 2 reports absolute numbers for one dataset only, making it impossible to judge whether the "green" component adds value beyond general recommendation improvement. - The operationalization of "green" or "sustainable" products is never rigorously defined. The Bundle dataset has intent annotations for Electronics, Clothing, and Food — not sustainability labels — so it is unclear how eco-friendliness is actually encoded or measured. - The energy-efficiency motivation is rhetorical: no empirical measurements of energy consumption, interaction counts, or carbon emissions are provided, undermining a central claim of the paper. - No ablation study is presented to isolate the contribution of individual agents (Evaluate, DetectError, InferReason, etc.), leaving it ambiguous which components drive the observed gains.
Citations 2603.11025 PDF
#79 — Moderately Innovative (52)

LUMAX: A LUT-Based Mixed-Precision Accelerator for LLM Inference on the Edge

dspace.lib.ntua.gr · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The quarter-size LUT strategy elegantly sidesteps the exponential memory growth that has limited prior LUT-based accelerators, and the tight RoCC/RISC-V integration with runtime reconfigurability is a practical contribution. However, evaluation is confined to a 15M-parameter toy model with no end-to-end inference benchmarks, and the comparison against a Gemmini baseline that lacks native sub-4-bit support inflates the reported advantages.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) on edge devices requires efficient mixed-precision inference, where activations remain at higher precision (INT8/INT16) while weights are quantized to low bitwidths (INT1–INT8). This asymmetric quantization regime is poorly served by both general-purpose hardware (GPUs, CPUs) and existing systolic array accelerators like Gemmini, which lack native support for sub-4-bit weights or runtime precision reconfiguration. Lookup Table (LUT)-based GeMM is a promising alternative—replacing multiplications with table lookups of precomputed products—but prior LUT accelerators suffer from exponentially growing memory requirements, limited mixed-precision support, and poor hardware-software integration into standard processor ecosystems. LUMAX addresses these gaps with a fully integrated, reconfigurable LUT-based GeMM accelerator tightly coupled to a RISC-V RocketChip core. Methodology: LUMAX centers on a quarter-size LUT (¼-LUT) strategy: rather than precomputing all possible products for a full activation vector (which grows exponentially with bitwidth), the design precomputes products only for a limited activation window, organizes them into synchronous read memories (MEMs), and uses weights as memory indices during inference. This reduces storage from 2^(N-1) to N × 2^(W_width-2) entries, enabling practical mixed-precision operation. The hardware comprises three main units: (1) the Product Generator, which generates and stores precomputed activation–weight products row-by-row into synchronous MEMs during each activation window; (2) the Select Module, which uses each weight as an index into MEMs to retrieve the correct product; and (3) the Accumulator, which sums products via an adder tree to produce output activations. The design is implemented in Chisel within the Chipyard ecosystem and exposed as a RocketChip Co-processor (RoCC), communicating with the host RISC-V core via custom instructions and a DMA engine over TileLink. Key micro-architectural optimizations include a pipelined Select-and-Accumulate stage (reducing 2n cycles to n+1 with no extra hardware), ping-pong weight buffers to overlap DMA weight loading with computation, and a configurable cache mode that reuses activation–weight pair lookups when activations have lower precision than weights. A generator-based design supports runtime-configurable parameters including input/weight bitwidths, activation slicing factors (XS, YS), memory block count (MEM), row factor (RF), and DMA IDs. A cycle-accurate analytical performance model is derived and validated against Verilator simulation. Key Results: LUMAX is prototyped and synthesized on a Xilinx ZCU106 FPGA (Zynq UltraScale+) using Vivado 2022.1, and evaluated on LLaMA2 (TinyStories 15M parameter model) vector-matrix GEMM workloads. Compared to an iso-resource Gemmini 4×4 PE systolic array baseline: LUMAX reduces LUT utilization by up to 33% and DSP usage by up to 96% (using only 8 DSPs versus Gemmini's 197). Cycle counts improve by up to 79% in the best configurations. Speedups for integer GeMM reach up to 4.73× for (8-bit activation, 2-bit weight) configurations and up to 4.64× for (8-bit activation, 4-bit weight). Power consumption is 0.3× to 0.6× of Gemmini across configurations, translating to approximately 70% improvement in energy efficiency. The cache mode, which adds only ~4% more LUTs and ~1% more flip-flops, substantially reduces cycles for small activation window configurations. Design space analysis shows performance is jointly determined by weight bitwidth, row factor, and memory block count, with memory-bound saturation occurring beyond certain DMA ID thresholds. Strengths: - Deep RoCC/RISC-V integration with a clean C software API (preload/start/fence) enables genuine hardware-software co-design and easy deployment into standard processor pipelines, unlike prior standalone LUT accelerators. - The quarter-size LUT approach avoids exponential memory growth, enabling practical mixed-precision operation (INT1–INT8 weights with INT8/INT16 activations) with a hardware-efficient output-stationary dataflow and no multipliers. - Runtime reconfigurability—bitwidths, tiling factors, and DMA parameters can all be adjusted per inference call—enables flexible deployment across heterogeneous quantization schemes without hardware recompilation. - The developed cycle-accurate analytical model and systematic sensitivity analysis provide principled guidelines for configuration selection, which is unusual for accelerator theses. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is confined to a single tiny model (LLaMA2 TinyStories 15M) using only vector-matrix GEMM; no end-to-end inference benchmarks on larger or more realistic LLMs are presented, making it difficult to assess actual deployment impact. - The comparison baseline (Gemmini 4×4 systolic array) does not natively support sub-4-bit inputs or mixed-precision, making the cycle-count comparison somewhat unfair; no direct comparison against LUT-based peers (FIGLUT, LUT Tensor Core) with aligned resource budgets is provided. - Performance evaluation relies on Verilator simulation and analytical cycle models rather than measured FPGA inference latency and throughput, so real-world results may differ due to routing delays, clock frequency limits, and system-level integration effects. - Activation precision is currently limited to INT8 and INT16; floating-point formats (FP16, FP8) used in many modern LLMs are not supported, restricting near-term applicability.
Search Results
#80 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation of Language Models

Woogyeol Jin, Taywon Min, Yongjin Yang, Swanand Ravindra Kadhe, Yi Zhou, Dennis Wei, Nathalie Baracaldo, Kimin Lee · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The diagnosis of diversity collapse at high-entropy teacher tokens is sharp and well-supported, but the intervention — a conditionally-gated forward KL term — is a relatively straightforward combination of existing loss functions (reverse KL + forward KL) with an entropy-based switch. Gains are consistent but modest in absolute terms, particularly on the hardest benchmarks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) of language models trains a student by matching a teacher's token-level distributions over the student's own generated trajectories, using reverse KL divergence as the objective. While effective at transferring dominant behaviors, reverse KL is mode-seeking: it concentrates the student's probability mass on the teacher's most likely tokens and ignores low-probability modes. This causes diversity collapse at positions where the teacher assigns significant probability to multiple plausible continuations — precisely the high-entropy tokens that represent ambiguous decision points in complex reasoning tasks. Empirically, distilled students retain only 6.8% of high-entropy tokens (entropy ≥ 1.0) compared to 18.5% in the teacher, and reverse KL produces unstable gradient signals (top-1 predictions fluctuate with a change count of 84.0 ± 16.7) when the teacher distribution is broad. The gap matters most for mathematical reasoning, where multiple valid solution paths exist and preserving the teacher's uncertainty is critical for exploration at test time. Methodology: The paper proposes Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation (EOPD), which augments the standard clipped reverse KL objective (OPD/PPO-style) with a forward KL term that is selectively activated at token positions where the teacher's entropy exceeds a threshold τ. The per-token loss is: L_EOPD(θ; c_t) = L_OPD(θ; c_t) + 𝟙[H_t^te > τ] · L_FKL(θ; c_t), where the forward KL is approximated using only the teacher's top-k tokens (k=16) to avoid the memory cost of computing over the full vocabulary. Forward KL is mode-covering: it penalizes the student for assigning low probability to any token the teacher deems plausible, directly preserving multimodal structure. By restricting forward KL to high-entropy positions (roughly 15–20% of tokens at convergence with τ=0.8), EOPD avoids the efficiency cost and over-diffusion pathologies of naively applying forward KL everywhere. The full training loop is standard on-policy rollout with PPO-style clipping; the only addition is querying the teacher for its entropy at each token position and conditionally adding the top-k forward KL term. Key Results: Evaluated on six math reasoning benchmarks (MATH500, AMC23, Minerva, OlympiadBench, AIME24, AIME25) across three Qwen3 student sizes (0.6B, 1.7B, 4B), EOPD consistently outperforms OPD. For Qwen3-0.6B-Base, EOPD improves Avg@8 by +1.16 and Pass@8 by +1.37 averaged across benchmarks. For 1.7B: +0.99 Avg@8 and +2.39 Pass@8. For 4B: +1.80 Avg@8 and +5.05 Pass@8. Pass@k curves on AIME show the EOPD advantage widens as k increases (from 8 to 128), indicating more diverse exploration. On out-of-domain benchmarks (GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, AlpacaEval 2.0), EOPD achieves higher average accuracy than OPD on general reasoning tasks, suggesting the diversity benefit transfers beyond the math training distribution. Token-level entropy histograms confirm EOPD preserves substantially more probability mass in the high-entropy region (≥1.0 nats) compared to OPD, staying closer to the teacher. EOPD also outperforms entropy-promoting baselines (OPD + Entropy Bonus, OPD + Advantage Shaping) on forward KL at high-entropy positions, showing that generic exploration incentives are insufficient — teacher-guided KL selection is key. Results generalize to Llama-3.2-3B / Llama-3.1-8B pairs. Strengths: - The core diagnosis is sharp and well-supported: the paper isolates the diversity collapse problem with both a controlled toy experiment and a careful token-level entropy analysis on real AIME prompts, making the motivation concrete rather than hand-wavy. - The intervention is minimal and practical — a single indicator-gated forward KL term with top-k truncation adds negligible computational overhead while addressing the root cause, requiring no architectural changes or additional teacher rollouts. - Ablations are thorough: the paper tests sensitivity to τ (stable over 0.6–1.6), compares full vs. random vs. entropy-gated forward KL placement (entropy-gating wins), and benchmarks against two entropy-promotion baselines, isolating the contribution of teacher-guided selection. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to mathematical reasoning; it is unclear whether the same diversity-collapse problem and EOPD's remedy generalize to domains like code generation or instruction following, where the teacher's high-entropy tokens may carry different semantic significance. - The method introduces a hyperparameter τ that requires tuning, and while the paper shows stability across a range, the optimal τ=0.8 was selected empirically; no principled criterion for setting τ given a new teacher/student pair is provided. - Gains on the hardest benchmark (AIME24/25) are modest in absolute terms for smaller models (e.g., Pass@8 +0 for 0.6B on AIME25), and the student-teacher gap remains large overall, leaving open the question of whether entropy-awareness is sufficient or whether architectural capacity is the binding constraint at these difficulty levels.
Recommended 2603.07079 PDF
#81 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Addressing Zoning Abbreviation Inconsistencies in the Greater Toronto Area (GTA) through a Universal Zoning Ontology

yorkspace.library.yorku.ca · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Zonology addresses a genuine and previously unmodeled gap—cross-jurisdictional zoning abbreviation harmonization—with a well-engineered OWL 2 DL ontology featuring principled design choices (Value With Provenance pattern, modular alignment). The evaluation is honest and instructive, reporting failures alongside successes. However, coverage of only 2 of 25 municipalities substantially limits the 'universal' claim, and the fundamental tension between formal semantics and deliberately ambiguous legal text remains unresolved, keeping this in solid-but-incremental applied ontology territory.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The Greater Toronto Area's 25 municipalities each maintain independent zoning by-laws with incompatible abbreviation systems, definitions, and regulatory structures. A zoning code like "R2" in Toronto carries different permitted uses and development standards than "R2-S" in Markham, despite superficial similarity—creating semantic fragmentation that forces developers, engineers, and planners to manually reconcile terminology across jurisdictions. This inefficiency compounds Ontario's housing crisis: against a provincial target of 125,000 new units per year, only ~81,300 were projected for 2024. Planning approval timelines of 2–4 years are partly attributable to this terminological fragmentation, which also impedes GIS integration, automated compliance tools, and AI-assisted planning systems. No prior ontology (LBCS-OWL2, iCity, OntoURBAN, CityGML, LandInfra) addressed municipal-level zoning abbreviation harmonization with reasoning support across jurisdictions. Methodology: The thesis introduces Zonology, an OWL 2 DL ontology developed using the Linked Open Terms (LOT) methodology and adhering to FAIR principles. The development followed five phases: (1) ontology specification via an Ontology Requirements Specification Document (ORSD) and 13 competency questions; (2) conceptualization with a hybrid top-down/bottom-up approach identifying core classes (ZoningDesignation, Zone, ZoningAbbreviation, LandUse, Authority, SpatialEntity, ZoningIndex, ZoningCharacteristic); (3) refinement and alignment reusing elements from LBCS-OWL2 and iCity; (4) implementation in Protégé 5.6 using OWL 2 DL with SPARQL query support, SWRL rules, and property chains; and (5) evaluation via DL reasoning (HermiT), competency question testing, and domain expert workshops. Cross-municipal alignment uses owl:equivalentClass and SKOS relations (skos:closeMatch, skos:broader) in a separate alignment module to avoid disrupting the core schema. Quantitative zoning constraints (FSI, max height, min frontage) are encoded using a "Value With Provenance" pattern—each numeric value is a named individual with hasUnit and hasValue properties traceable to authoritative by-law clauses. The two proof-of-concept municipalities—Toronto and Markham—were chosen because they represent contrasting approaches: Toronto uses a consolidated by-law (569-2013) with systematic codes, while Markham embeds density and site-specific conditions into legacy abbreviations. Key Results: Zonology harmonizes over 60 zoning categories and aligns over 150 land use permissions across Toronto and Markham. Evaluated against 13 competency question queries, the ontology achieved precision of 0.83, recall of 0.91, and F1 score of 0.87. Execution accuracy was ~0.77 (3 of 13 queries initially required manual corrections due to axiomatization errors discovered through testing—over-strict equivalence between Industrial and Employment classes causing inconsistency, a property range misspecification where hasPermittedLandUse was incorrectly declared as a datatype property, and missing conjunctive restrictions on Mixed-Use classes). All three were diagnosed and corrected. DL reasoning via HermiT confirmed global consistency: no unsatisfiable named classes, no property constraint violations, and no hierarchy collapse from equivalence axioms—including the Markham_TR ≡ Toronto_R1 alignment. Qualitatively, the ontology replaces multi-document manual cross-referencing with single SPARQL queries (e.g., retrieving all zones permitting daycare use across Toronto and Markham with no manual filtering). Planning staff confirmed usability with limited training during structured workshops. Strengths: - Addresses a concrete, practical gap: no prior ontology modeled municipal zoning abbreviations with cross-jurisdictional reasoning support, and the work directly connects to Ontario's housing supply bottleneck - The "Value With Provenance" pattern and modular alignment architecture are well-engineered design choices that ensure legal traceability, extensibility, and avoid tight coupling between the core schema and jurisdiction-specific mappings - The evaluation methodology is honest and instructive—three query failures were reported, analyzed, and fixed rather than suppressed, revealing real ontology engineering pitfalls (equivalence axiom misuse, property type errors, under-specified conjunctive restrictions) with transferable lessons Weaknesses / Limitations: - Coverage is limited to two of the GTA's 25 municipalities; the ontology cannot yet support region-wide queries, and unique practices in Vaughan, Oakville, Mississauga, and others remain unrepresented—substantially constraining the claim of a "universal" ontology in the title - Zoning by-laws contain pervasive conditional and discretionary language ("except where abutting a major arterial road," "subject to Committee of Adjustment approval") that cannot be faithfully encoded in OWL without oversimplification; the ontology is explicitly positioned as decision-support rather than authoritative, but this tension between formal semantics and deliberately ambiguous legal text is a fundamental, unresolved challenge
Search Results
#82 — Moderately Innovative (51)

Improving TabPFN's Synthetic Data Generation by Integrating Causal Structure

Davide Tugnoli, Andrea De Lorenzo, Marco Virgolin, Giovanni Cinà · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The paper cleanly diagnoses a real and previously underappreciated failure mode of autoregressive foundation models — spurious correlations from arbitrary column ordering in the presence of colliders. The DAG-aware conditioning fix is elegant and requires no retraining. However, the solution is conceptually straightforward once the problem is identified, and the practically important discovered-CPDAG setting yields largely negative results, significantly limiting real-world applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: TabPFN (Tabular Prior-Data Fitted Network) is a foundation model for tabular data that generates synthetic samples autoregressively — sequentially conditioning each feature on previously generated features in input column order. This ordering is arbitrary with respect to the true causal structure of the data. When the generation order conflicts with causal dependencies (e.g., a collider is generated before its parents), the model learns and propagates spurious conditional dependencies into the synthetic marginal distribution. This matters most in high-stakes domains like healthcare and drug development, where synthetic data must faithfully preserve causal effects to support valid downstream analyses such as treatment effect estimation. Prior causal generative models (DECAF, Causal-TGAN, DATGAN) address this by training from scratch on each dataset and explicitly using DAG structure, but no prior work had adapted a pre-trained foundation model for causally faithful generation. Methodology: The authors propose two complementary causal conditioning strategies layered on top of TabPFN's existing autoregressive generation mechanism. (1) DAG-aware conditioning: when a full DAG is known, each variable is conditioned exclusively on its causal parents (rather than all predecessors), and variables are generated in topological order. Formally, the conditioning set for variable x_{σ(i)} becomes C_DAG = {x_j : x_j → x_{σ(i)} in G}. (2) CPDAG-based conditioning: for the realistic partial-knowledge setting, a Completed Partially Directed Acyclic Graph (CPDAG) is used. Variables with at least one directed adjacent edge are conditioned on their known causal parents; remaining variables fall back to sequential conditioning on all predecessors. The minimal CPDAG is constructed by orienting only V-structures from the true DAG; the discovered CPDAG is learned from data via the PC-stable constraint-based algorithm (α=0.05), using KCI for continuous pairs, G² for discrete, and k-NN CMI for mixed-type pairs. Evaluation uses three metrics — Correlation Matrix Difference (CMD, Frobenius norm of correlation matrix discrepancy), k-Marginal Total Variation Distance (kMTVD, k=2), and Nearest-Neighbor Adversarial Accuracy (NNAA) — plus Average Treatment Effect (ATE) preservation. Experiments span a custom 4-variable collider SCM, six Microsoft CSuite benchmark datasets (4–12 nodes, diverse causal structures), and the 38-variable UVA/Padova T1DM glucose simulator (partial causal knowledge). Training sizes range from N=20 to N=1000 with 100 resampling iterations per configuration. Key Results: DAG-aware conditioning significantly improves CMD in 24 of 35 dataset×training-size configurations versus vanilla TabPFN with original ordering, with only 2 degradations (both on CSS at small N). When both methods use topological ordering (isolating the contribution of causal parent conditioning from column ordering alone), DAG-aware generation shows 20 significant improvements and no degradations in CMD. For ATE preservation, DAG-aware generation achieves 26 significant improvements across 42 configurations with only 2 degradations; on the custom collider SCM, it reduces absolute ATE error by approximately 1.23 units at N=20. Vanilla TabPFN with topological (vs. original) ordering yields 18 significant ATE improvements and 26 CMD improvements, confirming ordering alone matters substantially. Minimal CPDAG shows 15 CMD improvements and 3 degradations versus vanilla original ordering, and 23 ATE improvements with 2 degradations — the effectiveness depends on V-structure placement. Discovered CPDAG via PC-stable performs poorly overall (4 CMD degradations, 0 improvements), because PC leaves most edges undirected (direction recall ≤ 0.32 across all datasets and sizes), causing fallback to vanilla sequential conditioning and, on CMC where PC over-orients with low precision (~0.19), active degradation. On the 38-variable SGL dataset with partial causal information, improvements in distributional quality increase with training size, unlike the smaller SCMs. Strengths: - Cleanly diagnoses a fundamental and previously underappreciated failure mode of autoregressive foundation models: collider-induced spurious correlations from arbitrary column ordering, validated both theoretically and empirically with correlation coefficients of |ρ|≈0.15 in the vanilla model versus ≈0 for DAG-aware conditioning. - The DAG-aware and CPDAG-based strategies require no retraining or fine-tuning — they are pure inference-time modifications to TabPFN's conditioning context, making them immediately applicable to any TabPFN-based workflow. - Rigorous experimental design with 100 resampling iterations, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with Holm-Bonferroni correction, and Hodges-Lehmann effect size estimation across a diverse benchmark suite spanning controlled SCMs and realistic clinical simulators. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The CPDAG-based strategy is brittle in practice: PC-stable recovers useful orientations only under favorable conditions (sufficient sample size, correct variable types, no latent confounders), and incorrect orientations actively degrade performance — the discovered CPDAG results are largely negative, limiting the no-DAG-knowledge use case. - Evaluation of causal estimands is restricted to the ATE of a single pre-specified treatment-outcome pair per dataset; other causal quantities (ATT, CATE, interventional distributions over non-outcome variables) are not studied, leaving unclear how broadly the improvements generalize. - The CMD metric assumes monotonic dependence structure and is explicitly noted to be uninformative for datasets with multiplicative functional forms (e.g., CSS), creating a gap between the metric and the underlying causal fidelity being targeted.
Search Results 2603.10254 PDF
#83 — Moderately Innovative (51)

PromptTuner: SLO-Aware Elastic System for LLM Prompt Tuning

Wei Gao, Peng Sun, Dmitrii Ustiugov, Tianwei Zhang, Yonggang Wen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: PromptTuner addresses a real systems gap for prompt-tuning-as-a-service workloads with a well-engineered two-layer Prompt Bank and elastic scheduler. The characterization study clearly motivates why existing training and inference systems fail for this workload. However, the core techniques — K-medoid clustering for prompt retrieval, warm/cold GPU pooling, SLO-deadline-ordered scheduling — are standard systems building blocks assembled for a new workload rather than fundamentally new methods.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM Prompt Tuning-as-a-Service (LPT) has emerged as a widely adopted commercial offering, handling tens of thousands of daily requests that require GPU clusters to optimize soft prompts for downstream tasks. The core challenge is satisfying users' Service Level Objectives (SLOs)—latency deadlines for job completion—while minimizing GPU resource costs for providers. Prior systems designed for DL training (e.g., ElasticFlow) or inference (e.g., INFless) are ill-suited for LPT workloads for two reasons: training systems statically provision fixed-size GPU clusters with ~56% utilization, nearly doubling costs; inference systems incur 37–41% GPU allocation overhead from per-instance initialization, and are limited to single-GPU-per-instance designs that cannot support the synchronous cross-GPU communication LPT requires. A third problem unique to LPT is the high sensitivity of convergence speed to the choice of initial prompt—a well-chosen initial prompt can reduce the required tuning iterations by 1.7–4.5×, dramatically affecting SLO attainment and cost. Methodology: PromptTuner introduces two coordinated innovations built atop Knative and deployed on physical GPU clusters. First, the Prompt Bank is a query engine that automatically identifies effective initial prompts for incoming LPT jobs via prompt reusing. It assembles thousands of public prompt candidates and organizes them using a two-layer K-medoid clustering data structure indexed by LLM activation features. Lookup finds the best-matching cluster representative in the first layer, then scores candidates in that cluster using a metric score(p) = average loss on a small evaluation set (16 samples) without any tuning, reducing metric computations from C to K + C/K (optimally 2√C). The two-layer structure reduces lookup overhead by up to 40× while preserving near-ideal ITA (Iterations-to-Accuracy) performance. Second, the Workload Scheduler implements runtime reusing by maintaining per-LLM warm GPU pools with pre-loaded CUDA/DL runtimes and model weights, plus a shared cold GPU pool for elastic scaling. Two algorithms govern allocation: (1) a warm-pool allocator that progressively assigns GPUs to jobs in SLO-deadline order, and (2) a cold-pool allocator that adds/removes GPUs from warm pools using a DelaySchedulable function that delays execution of deferrable jobs to maximize warm-GPU reuse. A latency budget caps Prompt Bank overhead at 20% of each job's SLO. The system is implemented in ~2000 lines of Python (scheduler) and ~1000 lines (Prompt Bank), deployed on Knative with Memcached for cross-GPU communication. Key Results: Evaluated on a 32-node cluster of NVIDIA A100-80GB GPUs with GPT2-Base, GPT2-Large, and Vicuna-7B across 12 downstream tasks, PromptTuner reduces SLO violations by 4.0× vs. INFless and 7.9× vs. ElasticFlow, while lowering resource costs by 1.6× vs. INFless and 4.5× vs. ElasticFlow. Under varying SLO emergence (S=0.5 to 1.5), cost savings over INFless reach 38%, 23%, and 17%, and savings over ElasticFlow reach up to 70%. For heavy workloads with LLaMA-30B, PromptTuner reduces SLO violations by 1.36–2.90× and costs by 1.20–1.79× over baselines. For Qwen7B-R1 (32,768-token sequences, 4-GPU tensor parallelism), SLO violations drop by 1.56–3.24× and costs by 1.39–2.28×. The Prompt Bank achieves 90% of ideal-candidate ITA performance and outperforms induction initialization by 1.81×, 1.38×, and 1.28× ITA speedup for GPT2-Base, GPT2-Large, and Vicuna-7B, respectively. Prompt reusing alone reduces SLO violations by 13–23% and costs by 30–40%; runtime reusing contributes additional SLO reduction. Scheduling overhead is 13–67 ms, negligible relative to minute-level SLOs. Strengths: - The two-layer Prompt Bank data structure is a principled engineering contribution: it achieves near-ideal prompt selection quality with a 40× reduction in evaluation overhead and sub-10-second lookup latency, which is critical for meeting short SLOs. - The characterization study (§2–3) is thorough and well-motivated, quantitatively exposing why existing training and inference systems each fail LPT workloads along distinct axes (static provisioning vs. initialization overhead vs. prompt sensitivity). - Evaluation is comprehensive: physical cluster deployment (not simulation), multiple LLMs including large models (LLaMA-30B, Qwen7B-R1), ablations of all key components, and scalability tests up to 96 GPUs strengthen the validity of claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The Prompt Bank relies on a static offline corpus of public prompts; its effectiveness degrades for novel or highly domain-specific LPT tasks where no similar public prompts exist, a scenario not stress-tested in evaluation. - The system assumes SLOs are specified as a fixed multiple of minimum GPU-time, which may not capture real user intent (e.g., hard wall-clock deadlines independent of GPU allocation); the sensitivity of results to this SLO formulation is not thoroughly analyzed. - Evaluation uses only gradient-based soft prompt tuning; compatibility with other prompt optimization paradigms (reinforcement learning, zero-order methods) is asserted but not demonstrated, limiting claims of generality.
Recommended 2603.05087 PDF
#84 — Moderately Innovative (49)

$ P^ 2$ GNN: Two Prototype Sets to boost GNN Performance

Arihant Jain, Gundeep Arora, Anoop Saladi, Chaosheng Dong · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: P²GNN's plug-and-play dual-prototype mechanism is a reasonable architectural contribution, and the industrial-scale deployment on 400M-edge e-commerce graphs is notable. However, prototypes-as-virtual-nodes and prototypes-as-cluster-centers are individually well-explored ideas, and the combination, while effective, represents an engineering integration rather than a conceptual breakthrough.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Message-passing GNNs (MP-GNNs) face two structural limitations that impede performance in real-world applications such as fraud detection and e-commerce recommendation. First, MP-GNNs rely exclusively on local neighborhood context, lacking access to global graph-level information. Second, they assume strong homophily among connected nodes, making them brittle when neighborhood signals are noisy or heterophilous — for example, GAT underperforms a plain MLP by 2.6% on the Film dataset. These challenges are especially acute in industry settings where malicious nodes (fraudsters) deliberately connect to benign nodes to camouflage themselves. Existing heterophily-aware methods address the noise problem partially but do not provide a mechanism for global context, and prior prototype-based GNN methods are limited to binary classification or require a separate pre-training phase. Methodology: P²GNN introduces two complementary sets of learnable prototype vectors that serve as plug-and-play augmentations to any message-passing GNN layer. The first set, Prototypes as Neighbours (P_N), consists of K_N class-independent pseudo-nodes with directed edges to every real node in the graph. During message passing, each node aggregates from both its local neighbors and from all prototypes, using a mixed-attention mechanism (Eq. 2) to adaptively weight contributions from each source. This provides a low-complexity O(K×|V|) alternative to explicit graph densification (O(|V|²)), enabling scalable global context injection. The second set, Prototypes for Message Alignment (P_A), consists of K_A prototypes that act as cluster centers in hidden space. Before the final layer output, the aggregated node message H_N is aligned to the nearest P_A prototypes via a softmax attention (Eq. 3), effectively denoising the message by projecting it toward a learned cluster representative — analogous to hard-clustering in prototypical networks. The final representation mixes H_N and the aligned H_A via another learned attention (Eq. 4). Three auxiliary losses — alignment (cosine similarity maximization), diversity (entropy maximization over assignments), and sparsity (ℓ1/Frobenius regularization) — shape the prototype representations and are combined with the downstream task loss. Unlike ProtoGNN, P²GNN is class-independent and task-agnostic, requiring no pre-training and scaling to multiclass settings without linear growth in prototype count. Key Results: P²GNN is evaluated across 18 datasets: 8 small-scale open-source, 6 large-scale open-source, 2 fraud detection, and 2 proprietary e-commerce datasets. On 8 small open-source datasets (random 60/20/20 split), P²GNN achieves an average rank of 1.5 against 18 baselines including specialized heterophily methods (ACM-GCN, GloGNN++, H2GCN), besting all competitors with statistical significance on 6 of 8 datasets (paired t-test, p < 0.05). On 6 large-scale datasets, P²GNN achieves the top average rank of 1.2 with only modest training overhead (e.g., 2.02s vs. 1.52s per epoch vs. ACM-GCN on Penn94). On fixed-split benchmarks (Table 11), P²GNN achieves average rank 2.0, with top results on Texas (88.65%), Wisconsin (88.43%), and Cornell (86.49%). On proprietary e-commerce recommendation (700K–3.5M nodes, 10M–400M edges), P²GNN improves over the deployed GCN baseline by ~4% across all metrics on E-comm1 and ~1.5% on E-comm2. Ablation confirms both prototype types contribute incrementally, and performance improvements hold across all three tested GNN backbones (GCN, SGC, ACM-GCN). T-SNE visualizations show progressively better class separability as each prototype type is added. Strengths: - Plug-and-play design integrates with any MP-GNN backbone without architectural constraints, demonstrated concretely on GCN, SGC, and ACM-GCN - Scalable complexity: O(K×|V|) vs. O(|V|²) for graph densification, with only ~30–50% per-epoch overhead on large graphs - Evaluated at industrial scale on production recommendation systems (up to 3.5M nodes, 400M edges), and deployed in an e-commerce setting — rare for academic GNN papers Weaknesses / Limitations: - Despite claiming task-agnosticism, the method still requires careful tuning of at least five hyperparameters (K_N, K_A, λ_a, λ_d, λ_s) with dataset-specific optima; the recommended Bayesian search reduces cost but does not eliminate this burden - Performance on Squirrel and Chameleon datasets is below SOTA, attributed to duplicate-node data leakage issues in those benchmarks — a legitimate caveat, but it means the method's behavior on very high-noise, high-heterophily graphs remains less certain - The denoising rationale for P_A is conceptually sound but the alignment mechanism (soft-attention to learned cluster centers) may underperform hard-assignment approaches when noise is severe; no comparison to explicit neighborhood selection or edge-dropping denoising baselines is provided
Search Results 2603.09195 PDF
#85 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Machine Learning for Dynamic and Short-Term Prediction of Preeclampsia Using Routine Clinical Data

jamanetwork.com · Fei Wang, FAMIA, FIAHSI, FACMI - new articles
Why this rank: Scores 48 because while the rolling-window, gestational-age-anchored design is clinically sensible and addresses a real temporal gap in preeclampsia prediction, the core methodology (XGBoost on routine EHR features with SHAP) is thoroughly standard. The multi-site validation is rigorous but all sites belong to the same health system, and the low absolute PPVs (~0.06) leave clinical actionability unclear.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Preeclampsia affects 2–8% of pregnancies worldwide and is a leading cause of maternal and perinatal morbidity and mortality. Its unpredictable onset and rapid progression make timely risk prediction critical, yet existing tools have significant limitations: most focus on first-trimester risk stratification (to guide aspirin prophylaxis) but achieve only modest accuracy for late-onset and term preeclampsia (AUC ~0.6–0.7); many rely on specialized biomarkers (cell-free DNA, uterine artery Doppler, angiogenic markers) that are costly and unavailable in routine or resource-limited settings; and virtually all treat risk as static rather than dynamically updating it as pregnancy progresses. The key unaddressed dimension is when preeclampsia will occur — short-term prediction windows that could provide actionable lead time for intervention are largely unexplored. Methodology: The study develops XGBoost-based binary classifiers using a rolling gestational-age-anchored observation-and-prediction-window design across 58,839 pregnancies delivered at three demographically distinct NewYork-Presbyterian hospitals (WCMC, LMH, BMH) between October 2020 and May 2025. For each pregnancy, all EHR data up to a specified observation week is used to predict preeclampsia onset within a subsequent 1-, 2-, or 4-week window. Features are exclusively routine EHR data: demographic and obstetric characteristics (fixed covariates), blood pressure summaries (median/IQR within each observation window), and the most recent laboratory test results. Continuous variables were median-imputed and z-standardized within training folds to prevent information leakage. Models were trained with nested stratified 5-fold cross-validation using class-weighted loss. Generalizability was assessed via direct transfer, fine-tuning, and retraining across sites. Model interpretability used SHAP values. Baselines included logistic regression, random forest, and a conventional risk-score model. A Cox proportional hazards model was also evaluated for time-to-event framing. Key Results: Predictive performance follows a consistent increase-peak-decrease-rebound pattern across gestational age, peaking at 32–34 weeks' observation (AUC 0.863 at WCMC training; 0.808–0.834 at LMH and BMH via direct transfer), decreasing at 38 weeks, then rebounding near delivery. At 90% sensitivity, specificity improved from 0.321 at 28 weeks to 0.569 at 34 weeks (WCMC, 2-week window). Positive predictive values (PPV) were low in absolute terms due to low prevalence (~1–5%) but showed 2–4× enrichment over baseline prevalence across all sites and gestational ages, peaking near 36 weeks (mean PPV 0.057 at LMH and 0.046 at BMH). Negative predictive values remained ≥0.993 throughout. Fine-tuning at LMH improved AUC from 0.834 to 0.868 and retraining further to 0.871 (2-week window, 34-week observation). The full model consistently outperformed BP-only and lab-only ablations (e.g., at 32 weeks, full model AUC 0.863 vs. 0.797 for BP-only at WCMC). SHAP analysis revealed that systolic and diastolic BP dominate throughout, while laboratory features (albumin, alkaline phosphatase, hematologic markers) contribute most at 32–34 weeks; demographic and obstetric factors (pregravid BMI, parity, number of term deliveries) gain relative importance near delivery. No substantial fairness concerns were found across age and racial subgroups. Strengths: - Rolling-window design is clinically meaningful: it produces continuously updated, short-term risk estimates aligned with visit-by-visit obstetric decision-making rather than single static assessments - Large, multisite cohort (58,839 pregnancies across three demographically distinct hospitals) with rigorous external validation via direct transfer, fine-tuning, and retraining — directly addresses the generalizability gap common in prior single-cohort studies - Exclusive reliance on routinely available EHR data (no specialized biomarkers) makes the approach practically deployable across diverse and resource-limited settings Weaknesses / Limitations: - All three hospitals belong to the same NewYork-Presbyterian health system, limiting true external generalizability; validation in genuinely independent health systems, community hospitals, or low- and middle-income country settings remains absent - Absolute PPVs remain low (peak ~0.06 at best sites) even at 90% sensitivity, reflecting the low baseline prevalence of preeclampsia; the clinical utility in terms of avoided unnecessary interventions versus triggered unnecessary ones is not quantified - Preeclampsia labels derived from ICD-10 codes in the EHR introduce misclassification risk, and the longitudinal data has irregular sampling and substantial missingness — both inherent to real-world EHR data but potential sources of bias that the study does not fully characterize
Recommended
#86 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Towards Understanding Hybrid Protein Language Model Design: A Systematic Ablation and Interpretability Study

openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 48 as a well-executed controlled ablation study that provides useful architectural guidance for protein language model design, particularly the clean identification of MoE's task-dependent trade-off and the actionable insight about load-balancing loss removal during fine-tuning. However, this is fundamentally a comparison study at 20M parameters—far below practical deployment scale—using an aging benchmark suite, and introduces no new architectural components or training paradigms, placing it squarely in the solid-but-incremental category.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Protein language models (PLMs) have rapidly adopted novel architectural primitives—mixture-of-experts (MoE) layers, state-space models (SSMs), and hybrid SSM-attention designs—each demonstrating compelling results in isolation. However, these advances are evaluated under inconsistent conditions (different scales, corpora, training protocols, benchmarks), making it impossible to attribute performance gains to specific architectural choices. The field lacks a controlled study that isolates the contribution of each component. This paper fills that gap with a systematic ablation study holding all confounding variables constant. Methodology: All models are trained under a unified ESM-2-style masked language modeling protocol on UniRef50, with identical BERT-style 15% masking, AdamW optimizer, 500K training steps, and a fixed 20M active-parameter budget with hidden dimension 512. Five architecture families are compared: (1) dense Transformer baseline (ESM-2 style, 6 layers); (2) pure bidirectional SSM using Hydra (quasiseparable matrix-based, natively bidirectional); (3) hybrid SSM-Transformer at two ratios—4H:1T (SSM-heavy) and 2H:2T (balanced); (4) MoE Transformer variants using SonicMoE at two sparsity levels—64 experts/top-8 and 8 experts/top-2; (5) hybrid MoE combining SSM layers with MoE-augmented Transformer blocks. All models are evaluated on the TAPE benchmark suite covering stability, fluorescence, secondary structure (TS115, CB513, CASP12), remote homology (family/superfamily/fold), and contact prediction (medium/long-range P@L/5). An interpretability analysis probes MoE expert specialization by computing P(secondary structure label | expert) across layers. Key Results: Hybrid SSM-Transformer architectures are the most broadly effective. The balanced 2H:2T hybrid achieves the best non-MoE contact prediction (medium: 0.420, long: 0.410), best fluorescence (0.678), and strong secondary structure (cb513: 0.761). The 4H:1T hybrid achieves the best fold-level remote homology overall (0.249). MoE introduces a consistent task-dependent trade-off: adding MoE to either dense or hybrid backbones improves stability (hybrid MoE achieves best stability: 0.747–0.748) and secondary structure (2H:2T hybrid MoE best on CASP12: 0.795), but consistently degrades remote homology and long-range contact prediction. The higher-sparsity 64:8 MoE configuration outperforms 8:2 in most settings. Expert utilization analysis confirms emergent specialization: MoE experts develop clear helix- and strand-preferring routing behaviors, deepening with layer depth, consistent with alpha-helix prevalence in UniRef50. Removing auxiliary load-balancing loss during fine-tuning sharpens expert specialization and improves secondary structure prediction, but does not benefit global tasks. Strengths: - Rigorous controlled experimental design—all architectures trained under identical data, compute, and capacity budgets—enabling clean attribution of performance differences to architectural choices, a methodological standard rarely met in the PLM literature. - Mechanistic interpretability analysis directly connects expert routing behavior to downstream task performance, providing a principled explanation (token-level vs. sequence-level granularity) for the observed MoE trade-off rather than just reporting empirical numbers. - Practical actionable insight: the finding that selectively removing load-balancing loss during fine-tuning improves per-residue tasks is immediately useful for practitioners deploying MoE-based PLMs. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments are conducted at 20M active parameters—far below the 650M–3B scale at which PLMs are typically deployed in practice. Whether these architectural trade-offs persist at larger scale is unknown and explicitly acknowledged as future work, significantly limiting immediate practical applicability. - The evaluation suite (TAPE) is narrow and aging; it excludes important tasks like fitness landscape prediction, protein-protein interaction modeling, and inverse folding, leaving open whether the observed patterns generalize beyond the five benchmarks studied. - The paper does not explore segment- or sequence-level MoE routing as an alternative to token-level routing, which the authors themselves identify as the likely root cause of MoE's failure on global tasks—this is mentioned as future work but leaves the central limitation unaddressed within the paper.
Citations
#87 — Moderately Innovative (48)

FlashDriveVLA: Towards Real-time Inference for Autonomous Driving Vision-Language-Action Model

openreview.net · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: FlashDriveVLA scores 48 as competent systems engineering that applies known optimization techniques (KV caching, speculative decoding, quantization, CUDA graphs) to achieve a meaningful 4.9× speedup. The streaming inference with pre-RoPE key caching shows domain-specific care, but the individual techniques are largely imported from NLP inference optimization rather than novel contributions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like Alpamayo1-10B offer strong reasoning and trajectory prediction for autonomous driving but are too slow for real-time edge deployment — reaching 769.2 ms latency (~1.3 Hz) on an NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000, far below the control frequencies required for SAE Level 4 autonomy. The paper systematically diagnoses bottlenecks across all four inference stages (encode, prefill, decode, action) and finds severe spatial redundancy as the root cause, with decoding alone consuming 37.8% of total latency. Methodology: FlashDriveVLA is an algorithm-system co-design framework targeting each bottleneck independently. At the system level: (1) CUDA Graph compilation eliminates CPU kernel launch overhead across all four pipeline stages; (2) kernel fusion packs QKV projections and MLP gate/up-projections into single launches under max-autotune mode. At the algorithm level: (1) Streaming Inference exploits the ~75% frame overlap between consecutive sliding windows (4 frames × 4 views) by caching KV states for the prior three frames and encoding only the new frame — requiring pre-RoPE key storage for dynamic positional recomputation and a specialized streaming attention mask to preserve causal dependencies; (2) Speculative Reasoning via DFlash replaces autoregressive CoC token generation with a lightweight 2-layer block diffusion drafter (block size=8) trained on ~40k NVIDIA AV dataset clips, generating candidate token blocks in parallel with an average accept length of 4.8 tokens; (3) Few-Step Diffusion reduces the action module from 10 to 4 flow-matching steps; (4) AWQ W4A16 quantization shrinks model size from ~31.6 GB to ~15.5 GB to fit edge memory budgets. Key Results: The combined system achieves 158.2 ms end-to-end latency (4.9× speedup, 6.3 FPS) on an RTX PRO 6000, with per-stage speedups of 7.2× (encode: 90.1→12.5 ms), 2.6× (prefill: 165.6→63.6 ms), 7.3× (decode: 290.8→40.0 ms), and 5.3× (action: 222.7→42.1 ms). Accuracy degradation is modest: minADE6@6.4s rises from 0.798 m to 1.203 m (+0.4 m) and ADE from 1.846 m to 2.184 m (+0.3 m). System optimizations alone yield 1.56× speedup; streaming inference contributes the largest single gain (~3× on encode/prefill); speculative decoding delivers 5.2× decoding throughput improvement (54.3→329.4 tokens/s); few-step diffusion gives 2.5× action speedup; quantization adds a final 14.7 ms reduction. Strengths: - The stage-by-stage profiling methodology is disciplined and the ablation table (Table 1) clearly attributes contributions of each component, making the engineering rationale transparent and reproducible. - Streaming inference with pre-RoPE key caching is a non-trivial adaptation that correctly handles Alpamayo1's view-first token ordering and positional semantics under sliding window shifts — not a naive KV reuse. - The 4.9× end-to-end speedup with only ~0.4 m accuracy loss is practically meaningful; the claim that this falls within standard lane safety margins is at least plausible and consistent with real-world driving tolerances. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a 4-page workshop-style submission evaluated on a single GPU (RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell) with only 100 clips; generalizability to true edge hardware (e.g., automotive-grade SoCs, Orin, or lower-end GPUs) is not demonstrated. - The accuracy evaluation is thin: only ADE and minADE6 on trajectory prediction are reported; closed-loop safety metrics, collision rates, or benchmark suite results (nuPlan, Waymo Open) are absent, making it difficult to assess real-world safety impact of the 0.4 m degradation. - DFlash (Chen et al., 2026) is an external dependency cited as a future/concurrent work; the paper provides no ablation of its draft model training data size or sensitivity to domain shift, leaving reproducibility partially open.
Search Results
#88 — Moderately Innovative (47)

OptiLeak: Efficient Prompt Reconstruction via Reinforcement Learning in Multi-tenant LLM Services

Longxiang Wang, Xiang Zheng, Xuhao Zhang, Yao Zhang, Ye Wu, Cong Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: OptiLeak's automated DPO annotation mechanism—using token prediction likelihood to identify hard tokens without human labeling—is a technically clean contribution that removes a practical bottleneck. The two-stage SFT→DPO pipeline is well-ablated and the 12× ARPT reduction is striking. However, this is fundamentally an optimization of a known attack vector rather than a new attack surface, and the threat model's requirement for domain-aligned public data constrains real-world applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-tenant LLM serving frameworks (vLLM, SGLang) share Key-Value (KV) caches across user requests to improve inference efficiency. This shared cache creates a timing side-channel: an adversary can detect cache hits by measuring Time-to-First-Token (TTFT), revealing whether their probe query shares a prefix with a victim's cached query, and thereby reconstruct the victim's prompt token by token. Prior work demonstrated this attack surface exists but focused on expanding attack vectors rather than optimizing attack efficiency, reporting impractically high request counts that underestimate the true privacy risk. The gap motivates a systematic study of how an optimized adversary can minimize the number of queries needed to reconstruct victim prompts. Methodology: OptiLeak frames prompt reconstruction as an adversarial optimization problem and addresses it with a two-stage fine-tuning pipeline applied to a locally held attacker model. In Stage 1, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is applied to a base model using domain-specific auxiliary data (e.g., medical QA, financial QA) to familiarize it with domain language patterns. In Stage 2, Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is applied using an automated annotation scheme that avoids manual labeling: the SFT model is used to rank the likelihood of each token in training samples, and tokens that rank high in difficulty (low predicted likelihood) are identified as "hard tokens" — domain-specific terms that carry sensitive information but are hard to predict. Preferred responses (s_win) are completions containing the hard token; dispreferred responses (s_lose) are greedy-decoded alternatives at the same position. This constructs a preference dataset for DPO without any human annotation. The attack itself operates token-by-token against an LLM server using the Longest Prefix Match (LPM) scheduling policy in SGLang: at each step, the adversary sends a batch of candidate queries and dummy queries, then observes response ordering via TTFT to detect cache hits, recovering one token per successful hit. Key Results: Evaluated on MedQA, PubMedQA, and FinanceBench using Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B, 14B) and Llama-3.1-8B, OptiLeak achieves up to 12.48× reduction in Average Requests Per Token (ARPT) compared to the base LLM approach on FinanceBench with Qwen-2.5-3B-Instruct. On MedQA, ARPT drops from 41.99 (base) to 21.60 with OptiLeak — a 48.6% reduction. Attack Success Rate at 1,000 requests (ASR_1000) reaches 98–100% across multiple settings. A key finding is that model size does not predict attack quality: the 3B model consistently achieves the lowest ARPT and highest ASR, outperforming 7B and 14B variants, because targeted fine-tuning alignment matters more than raw capacity for this task. Generalizability experiments show that training on a related but distinct domain (PubMedQA vs. MedQA) still yields 27.9% ARPT reduction over the base, while a fully misaligned domain causes performance degradation, confirming that domain proximity is the primary driver of efficiency. Ablation confirms SFT alone reduces ARPT by ~37% but suffers overfitting at extended training; DPO applied after SFT provides an additional ~18% reduction with more stable training dynamics. Strengths: - The automated DPO annotation mechanism — using token prediction likelihood to identify hard tokens without human labeling — is a practically deployable and technically grounded contribution that eliminates a key bottleneck in prior RL-based attack work. - The two-stage SFT→DPO pipeline is carefully motivated: SFT provides domain cold-start while DPO addresses hard-token alignment; the ablation study directly validates this decomposition and shows that DPO alone provides negligible improvement without SFT. - The experimental scope is thorough: three domain-specific benchmarks, four model sizes from two model families, four levels of adversarial prior knowledge, and ablation over training epochs — all with clear metrics (ARPT, ASR, w/l rate) that directly measure practical attack cost. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The threat model assumes the attacker has domain knowledge and access to a public dataset from the same domain as the victim, which is a meaningful precondition that limits applicability in highly specialized or novel domains where no public proxy data exists. - The evaluation uses only the LPM scheduling policy in SGLang; generalization to other scheduling mechanisms (FCFS, priority-based) and semantic caching systems (GPTCache, AWS semantic cache) is discussed qualitatively but not experimentally validated, leaving the scope of practical applicability partially unconfirmed. - The paper does not quantify the computational cost or wall-clock time required to conduct a full attack against a production service, which is a practically relevant metric for assessing real-world threat severity beyond the request-count metrics reported.
Recommended 2602.20595 PDF
#89 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Intrinsic Thematic Separability in Sentence-Transformer Embeddings: A Controlled Geometric Study from Synthetic to Real-World Text

researchsquare.com · 2 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Asks a timely question—whether sentence-transformer embeddings encode topic independently of authorial or generative style—and answers it with a well-structured controlled experiment spanning synthetic to real-world corpora. However, the synthetic upper bound is trivially achievable (TF-IDF also hits ARI=1.0), the cross-LLM test covers only one alternative model, and the geometric diagnostic regressions over just six conditions lack statistical power. The findings largely confirm expected behavior rather than revealing surprises.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: With the rapid adoption of LLMs for content generation, a critical methodological question emerges for computational text analysis: can unsupervised clustering pipelines distinguish thematic categories when all texts are produced by the same generative model? Prior validation studies of the SBERT+UMAP+HDBSCAN pipeline have assumed heterogeneous authorship, leaving open whether topic separability is an intrinsic property of sentence-transformer embeddings or merely an artifact of author-style variation. The paper also addresses whether results on controlled synthetic corpora translate meaningfully to real-world text with genuine semantic overlap. Methodology: The study employs a controlled geometric design across seven corpora of increasing difficulty. A synthetic baseline of 8,000 ChatGPT-generated texts across four semantically distant domains (Motorsport, Politics, Recipes, Sports) establishes an upper bound. This is compared against a human-authored Reddit corpus (8,000 comments from matched subreddits) and three NLP benchmarks: AG News (50,000 articles, 4 categories), Reuters-21578 (7,221 documents, 8 categories), and 20 Newsgroups (18,331 posts, 20 categories). All texts are encoded with all-mpnet-base-v2 (768D), with BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5 (1024D) used for architectural generalization. Macro-topic recovery uses K-Means and Ward clustering directly in the native 768D space. Micro-topic discovery applies UMAP (3D projection) followed by HDBSCAN leaf clustering into sub-clusters. A cross-LLM experiment (ChatGPT + Claude, 8,580 texts) tests whether embeddings encode generation source. Geometric diagnostics—separation ratio, cosine silhouette, Davies-Bouldin index—are correlated with observed ARI across conditions. A stylistic-vs-semantic decomposition tests nine surface-level features. TF-IDF+SVD(300D) provides a sparse baseline, and sub-cluster coherence is validated via NPMI on 394 AG News HDBSCAN clusters. Key Results: K-Means and Ward achieve perfect macro-topic recovery (ARI=1.000) on the synthetic corpus in native 768D space, demonstrating separability is intrinsic and independent of UMAP. The cross-LLM experiment (ChatGPT+Claude combined) achieves ARI=0.994 against topic labels but ARI≈0 against source labels, confirming embeddings encode topic not generation source. UMAP+HDBSCAN discovers 66 thematically pure sub-clusters (88.7% of points assigned; 64/66 are single-theme). Performance degrades systematically across real-world corpora: Human Reddit (ARI=0.820), AG News (0.568), Reuters top-8 (0.469), 20 Newsgroups (0.403–0.404). The TF-IDF+SVD baseline matches embeddings on synthetic data (both ARI=1.000) but underperforms substantially on real-world benchmarks by +0.13 to +0.43 ARI. The cosine silhouette is the most robust geometric diagnostic (Pearson r=0.83, p=0.042; Spearman ρ=0.83, p=0.042). NPMI validation confirms all 394 AG News sub-clusters are significantly more coherent than chance (Cohen's d=6.03; 91.0% holdout nearest-centroid accuracy). Nine surface-level stylistic features capture neither topic (ARI=0.016) nor source (ARI=0.000), ruling out style as a confound. Strengths: - The controlled experimental design—using synthetic text as an explicit upper bound and progressively harder real-world corpora—cleanly disentangles embedding geometry from authorial style, addressing a genuine methodological gap in the clustering literature. - The two-level evaluation framework (macro ARI for K-Means/Ward; NPMI coherence for HDBSCAN sub-clusters) is principled and avoids the misleading conclusion that HDBSCAN "fails" when it is optimizing a different objective than macro-label recovery. - The cross-LLM experiment and stylistic decomposition provide converging, concrete evidence that topical signal dominates over generation-source signal in the relevant geometric structure, with practical implications for pipelines applied to mixed-LLM corpora. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The synthetic corpus uses four maximally distant domains (Motorsport, Politics, Recipes, Sports), making perfect clustering trivially achievable by any reasonable representation—the TF-IDF+SVD baseline also hits ARI=1.000, so the synthetic result does not isolate embedding-specific power. The most informative region of the gradient (near-overlapping synthetic domains) is unexplored. - Geometric diagnostics are evaluated over only n=6 conditions with k confounded by dataset, yielding wide bootstrap confidence intervals (slope CI [-0.75, 2.12] for separation ratio; Pearson CI [-0.89, 1.00]) that render the regression structurally uncertain and unsuitable as a predictive instrument on unlabelled data. - Cross-LLM generalization is tested only against Claude; open-weight models (Llama, Mistral) are absent, and the human Reddit corpus differs from synthetic text in length, register, and lexical entropy—not only authorial heterogeneity—so the 18-percentage-point ARI drop conflates multiple confounds beyond the claimed "homogeneity advantage."
Citations
#90 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Practical Type Inference : High- Throughput Recovery of Real-World Structures and Function Signatures

Lukas Seidel, Sam Thomas, Konrad Rieck · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: XTRIDE achieves impressive engineering results with 200-100,000x speedups over LLM/constraint approaches while maintaining competitive accuracy, and the calibrated confidence scoring via isotonic regression is a principled addition. However, the methodology is fundamentally an optimized extension of STRIDE's n-gram matching with known techniques (isotonic regression, bias correction), and the closed-vocabulary limitation caps its conceptual contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Recovering variable types and struct layouts from stripped binaries is essential for producing readable, analyzable decompiler output, yet it remains a bottleneck in automated reverse engineering pipelines. Existing approaches either use heavyweight LLM-based or constraint-solving methods with prohibitive runtime (200–8500 ms per function), or use n-gram matching (STRIDE) without calibrated confidence scores, making threshold-based filtering impossible. Neither camp handles both layout recovery and semantic naming simultaneously with deployable speed. XTRIDE targets this gap: it aims to be fast enough for continuous large-scale scanning, accurate enough to be useful, and principled enough to support automated abstention on low-confidence predictions. Methodology: XTRIDE extends STRIDE's core approach of treating decompiled code as text and matching variable context windows against a database of known (n-gram, type) pairs extracted from debug-symbol-annotated training binaries. Key technical improvements over STRIDE include: (1) Optimized training configuration — scaling from 75k to 300k training binaries and reducing from 16 overlapping n-gram databases to 4 (sizes {2,8,12,48} for 64-bit), dramatically cutting memory footprint while improving out-of-distribution accuracy; (2) Bitness separation — maintaining separate 32-bit and 64-bit databases to avoid false positives from pointer-size alignment differences; (3) Calibrated confidence score — using isotonic regression on a held-out validation set to map raw aggregated scores to probabilities, enabling threshold τ selection for precision-recall trade-offs without requiring labeled test data; (4) Primitive type bias correction — avoiding STRIDE's frequency-based tie-breaking that favored ubiquitous int32_t/uint64_t over struct types; (5) Function signature recovery — treating function call sites as variable occurrences and matching against a separate function-signature vocabulary database, with global aggregation across all call sites per function. The system is implemented in Rust (~5,000 lines) with memory-mapped I/O and multi-processing for O(1) hash lookups, targeting pipeline-scale throughput. Key Results: On the DIRT benchmark (142,193 test functions across 9,457 binaries), XTRIDE achieves 90.15% overall type accuracy, improving over STRIDE by 5.09 percentage points (statistically significant, Mann-Whitney U p=0.040), with 98.26% in-training and 68.66% out-of-training accuracy (vs. STRIDE's 65.51%). Runtime is 0.04 ms per function versus STRIDE's 8.2 ms (Python) and DIRTY's 200–8500 ms, yielding a 200–100,000× speedup over static-analysis/LLM pipelines. For struct recovery on real-world binaries (coreutils, wget, grep, gzip, lighttpd), the base XTRIDE achieves F1=0.768 on layout recovery vs. HyRES's 0.795 and TypeForge's 0.555, while running 700–2300× faster than HyRES and 70–180× faster than TypeForge. The in-domain fine-tuned XTRIDE_PLUS reaches F1=0.944 on layout recovery with full-match accuracy of 0.943, surpassing all baselines. Confidence calibration successfully enables selective operation: at τ=0.90, the model achieves near-zero error rate on retained predictions at the cost of coverage. Function signature recovery on embedded firmware achieves F1≈0.43 (precision 0.52, recall 0.37) on ARM binaries at threshold 0.4. Strengths: - Dramatic throughput improvement (orders of magnitude over LLM/constraint approaches) with only modest accuracy trade-off, making large-scale automated deployment genuinely feasible. - Calibrated confidence scoring via isotonic regression is principled and provides a deployable knob for precision-recall tuning absent in all prior n-gram and most ML-based systems. - Vocabulary-grounded predictions yield fully-qualified type and field names upon correct identification, giving automatic layout correctness without a separate name-recovery step — a property neither HyRES nor TypeForge provides natively. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Closed-vocabulary design fundamentally limits generalization: types unseen during training cannot be recovered, making the system unsuitable for open-world or novel-type-heavy environments, and improvements in training corpus coverage merely push, not remove, this ceiling. - Struct identification recall in the base configuration is weak (F1=0.329 at struct identification), and competitive layout recovery requires in-distribution fine-tuning (XTRIDE_PLUS) that introduces substantial train-test overlap (40.9–71.7%), making direct comparison with open-world baselines like HyRES methodologically fraught. - Function signature recovery is an auxiliary heuristic with moderate precision and low recall (average F1≈0.43), heavily biased toward frequently occurring library functions; rare or novel function signatures are largely missed.
Search Results 2603.08225 PDF
#91 — Moderately Innovative (46)

Autorubric: A Unified Framework for Rubric-Based LLM Evaluation

Delip Rao, Chris Callison-Burch · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Autorubric is a well-executed engineering consolidation that pairs scattered LLM-as-judge techniques with a unified implementation and bridges NLP evaluation with psychometric principles. The CHARM-100 benchmark with heterogeneous criterion types fills a genuine gap. However, the core intellectual contribution is synthesis and software rather than new methodology — the individual techniques (ensemble judging, position shuffling, few-shot calibration) are all drawn from prior work, making this solidly useful but not conceptually novel.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-as-a-judge (LaaJ) evaluation has become the default approach for assessing text generation at scale, yet the techniques that make rubric-driven evaluation reliable — ensemble judging, position bias mitigation, few-shot calibration, uncertainty handling — are scattered across disconnected papers with inconsistent terminology. Practitioners must independently discover and combine these approaches, resulting in ad hoc implementations, difficulty comparing results across studies, and repeated reinvention of the same solutions.
